Regular activity after birth helps your body recover from childbirth and may help prevent postnatal depression. Walking with the pram, swimming, and postnatal exercise classes are all good ways to rebuild fitness gradually.

When can I exercise after a caesarean or complicated birth? Recovery takes longer after a caesarean section or a more complicated delivery, and your return to exercise should be slower. According to the NHS, if you had a more complicated delivery or a caesarean, your recovery time will be longer than after a straightforward birth, and you should talk to your midwife, health visitor, or GP before starting anything strenuous. Gentle movement, such as short walks, is usually encouraged early on as you feel able, because it supports circulation and recovery, but abdominal exercise, heavy lifting, and high-impact activity need to wait until your body has healed and a health professional has advised it is safe. Pelvic floor exercises remain important after a caesarean too, because pregnancy itself, not just vaginal delivery, weakens these muscles. Listen to your body, stop if anything hurts, and build up activity gradually over weeks and months.

Why are pelvic floor exercises important after birth and when can I start? Your pelvic floor muscles sit at the bottom of your pelvis and help support your bladder, bowel, and womb. According to NHS guidance, these organs and muscles are affected by pregnancy and delivery regardless of how your baby was born, including after a caesarean, which is why exercising the pelvic floor after birth matters for everyone. Strengthening the pelvic floor helps prevent and improve urinary leakage and supports recovery of the pelvic organs. Pelvic floor exercises are quick and simple, can be done anywhere as long as you are comfortable, and can be started within the first days following delivery. A typical exercise involves squeezing and lifting the muscles you would use to stop the flow of urine, holding briefly, then relaxing, repeated several times a day. If leakage persists, ask your GP about referral to a physiotherapist.
source_name: NHS (King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is lochia and what bleeding is normal after birth? Lochia is the vaginal blood loss that everyone experiences after giving birth, as the womb heals and returns to its pre-pregnancy size. According to NHS guidance, the amount and colour of lochia varies with several normal factors. It may be heavier in the morning or after a period of lying down, because blood pools while you are horizontal. If you are breastfeeding, the hormone oxytocin released during feeds makes the womb contract, so lochia may briefly increase or return to a redder colour during or just after a feed. As you increase your activity level, for example going for gentle walks or returning to household chores, you may notice slightly heavier loss, especially in the first two weeks, although the colour should stay brownish or pinkish rather than turning bright red. Good hand hygiene when changing pads is important because infection risk is higher in the first weeks.
source_name: NHS (University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust)

text: When should I be concerned about bleeding after giving birth? While vaginal blood loss (lochia) after birth is normal and gradually reduces over weeks, some patterns need medical review. According to NHS guidance, if you are concerned about your blood loss while at home, it helps to keep any heavily stained pads or clots you have passed and show them to your midwife or GP; if that is not practical, a photo on your phone can help them assess the amount of blood or the size of a clot. Signs that need prompt assessment include suddenly heavier bright red bleeding after the loss had been settling, passing large clots, foul-smelling loss, fever, or feeling generally unwell, as these can indicate retained tissue or infection. Very heavy bleeding, such as soaking pads rapidly, is an emergency: call for urgent medical help. During the first weeks you are at increased risk of bacterial infection, so wash hands before and after changing pads.
source_name: NHS (University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust)

text: How much should I drink during pregnancy? Staying well hydrated matters throughout pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, you should drink plenty of water when you are pregnant to keep hydrated and to help prevent constipation, which is especially important in the last three months of pregnancy when constipation is more common. Water is the best everyday drink; milk and diluted fruit juice also contribute to fluid intake, while drinks containing caffeine count toward the recommended pregnancy caffeine limit and should be moderated. One safety note: if your drinking water comes from a private supply such as a well, borehole, or spring, you should boil it before drinking, because the quality of private water supplies can vary a lot and poor quality water can cause health problems. Signs of good hydration include pale-coloured urine; dark urine, headaches, and dizziness can indicate you need to drink more, particularly in hot weather.

When should I go to the hospital or birth centre in labour? Most people spend the early part of labour at home, and going in too early often just means being sent home again. According to the NHS, if you go into hospital before your labour has become established, meaning before your cervix has dilated to about 4 centimetres with strong regular contractions, the staff may suggest you go home again for a while; this is routine and not a setback. Call your midwife or maternity unit for guidance when contractions are becoming regular, stronger, longer, and closer together, and they will advise you when to come in based on your progress, your history, and your distance from the hospital. Go in or call urgently regardless of contraction pattern if your waters break, you are bleeding, your baby is moving less than usual, or you feel a strong urge to push.

What happens at 20 weeks pregnant? At 20 weeks you are now halfway through your pregnancy, a major milestone. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, you may have your anomaly scan this week, the detailed ultrasound that checks how your baby's body is developing. Common signs of pregnancy at this stage can include tiredness and sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen and bleeding gums, pains on the side of your belly caused by your expanding womb (known as round ligament pains), headaches, nosebleeds, bloating and constipation, indigestion and heartburn, sore breasts, leg cramps, feeling hot, dizziness, swollen hands and feet, urine infections, vaginal infections, darkened patches of skin on the face, greasier or spotty skin, and thicker, shinier hair. Most of these are normal effects of pregnancy hormones and your growing womb, but mention anything troubling to your midwife at your appointments.

What symptoms are common at 21 weeks pregnant? At 21 weeks, as you begin the second half of your pregnancy, you are entering a period of rapid growth for your baby, and your body reflects it. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, common symptoms at this stage include tiredness and sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen and bleeding gums, pains on the side of your baby bump caused by your expanding womb (round ligament pains), piles, headaches, backache, nosebleeds, indigestion and heartburn, bloating and constipation, leg cramps, feeling hot, dizziness, swollen hands and feet, urine infections, and vaginal infections. Some people also notice darkened skin on the face or brown patches, known as chloasma or the mask of pregnancy, and greasier, spotty skin. These symptoms are common and usually harmless, but always tell your midwife or doctor about symptoms that are severe, sudden, or worrying you.

What should I expect at 25 weeks pregnant? At 25 weeks you are approaching the third trimester, and according to the NHS week-by-week guide you might be feeling more tired again, and perhaps a bit more clumsy and uncoordinated than usual as your bump grows and your centre of gravity shifts. The common pregnancy symptoms of mid-pregnancy often continue at this stage, including tiredness and sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen and bleeding gums, round ligament pains at the sides of your bump, piles, headaches, backache, indigestion and heartburn, bloating and constipation, leg cramps, feeling hot, dizziness, swollen hands and feet, urine infections, vaginal infections, and skin changes such as chloasma. Take extra care with balance and trip hazards as coordination changes, keep up gentle activity and rest, and continue attending your antenatal appointments where your blood pressure and urine will be checked.

What checks happen at antenatal appointments around 38 weeks? In the final weeks of pregnancy, antenatal checks focus on keeping watch for late-pregnancy complications while your body prepares for labour. According to the NHS week-by-week guide for week 38, at your appointments your urine will be checked for protein, because protein in the urine can indicate a dangerous condition called pre-eclampsia, which becomes more likely toward the end of pregnancy. Your blood pressure is also measured for the same reason, and your midwife will check your bump to assess the baby's growth and position. Many normal pregnancy symptoms can continue at this stage, including sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen gums, round ligament pains, piles, headaches, backache, indigestion, constipation, leg cramps, dizziness, and swollen hands and feet. Report any severe headache, vision changes, sudden swelling, reduced baby movements, bleeding, or leaking fluid immediately rather than waiting for your next appointment.

Why am I so tired during pregnancy? It is common to feel tired, or even exhausted, during pregnancy, especially in the first 12 weeks. According to the NHS, hormonal changes at this time can make you feel tired, nauseous, and emotional, and the only real answer is to rest as much as possible. Later in pregnancy, tiredness often returns because of the extra weight you are carrying, and because a bigger bump can make it difficult to get a good night's sleep, whether from discomfort lying down or from needing the toilet frequently. Feeling tired will not harm you or your baby, but it can make daily life feel harder, particularly in the early weeks before you have told people about your pregnancy. Make time to sit with your feet up during the day, and accept offers of help from colleagues, friends, and family.

How can I manage pregnancy tiredness? While rest is the main remedy for pregnancy fatigue, several other steps help. According to the NHS, being tired and run-down can make you feel low, so look after your physical health by eating a healthy diet and getting plenty of rest and sleep. Perhaps counterintuitively, exercise can actually help you feel less tired, so try to fit in some activity, such as a walk at lunchtime or going swimming, even on days when you feel tired. Antenatal yoga or pilates classes can also help, as long as the instructor knows you are pregnant. Accept practical help from the people around you, and prioritise the tasks that genuinely matter over those that can wait. If lack of sleep or persistent exhaustion is really bothering you, talk to your partner, a friend, your doctor, or your midwife for support.

How can I sleep better during pregnancy? Sleep often becomes harder as pregnancy progresses, but good habits can improve it. According to the NHS, try to relax before bedtime so you are not wide awake when you lie down; relaxation techniques can help, and your midwife or antenatal classes can teach you some, or you can use a pregnancy relaxation recording. It is best not to nap in the afternoon or evening, as this can mean you are not tired at bedtime. Avoid tea, coffee, and cola drinks in the evening, because the caffeine can make it harder to fall asleep. Daytime exercise, such as walking or swimming, also supports better sleep at night. As your bump grows, experiment with pillows to support your bump and back, and expect some broken sleep from toilet trips, which is normal. Talk to your midwife if sleeplessness is significantly affecting you.

Are stretch marks in pregnancy harmful and can I treat them? Stretch marks are narrow streaks or lines that can appear on the skin of the tummy, thighs, and breasts as pregnancy progresses and the skin stretches. According to NHS guidance, stretch marks are not harmful: they do not cause medical problems, and there is no specific treatment for them. They happen when the deeper layer of skin stretches quickly, and whether you get them depends largely on your skin type and genetics rather than anything you did. During pregnancy they often look red, pink, or purple; after birth they typically fade gradually to paler, silvery lines that are much less noticeable, although they may not disappear completely. Many creams and oils are marketed for preventing stretch marks, but evidence for them is limited. Keeping weight gain gradual and skin moisturised may help comfort, but stretch marks are a normal part of many pregnancies.
source_name: NHS (North West Anglia NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What are round ligament pains in pregnancy? Round ligament pains are pains felt on the side of your belly or baby bump, and they are a common pregnancy symptom from mid-pregnancy onward. According to the NHS week-by-week guidance, these pains are caused by your expanding womb: the ligaments that support the womb stretch and thicken as it grows, and sudden movements, coughing, laughing, or standing up quickly can trigger a sharp, brief pain or a longer dull ache on one or both sides of the bump. Round ligament pain is normal and not harmful to you or the baby. Moving more slowly when changing position, supporting your bump, resting, and a warm bath can ease it. However, tummy pain that is severe, constant, accompanied by bleeding, fever, or contractions, or simply feels wrong should always be checked by your midwife or maternity unit rather than assumed to be ligament pain.

Why do I have dark patches on my face during pregnancy? Darkened skin on your face or brown patches appearing during pregnancy is a recognised condition known as chloasma, sometimes called melasma or the 'mask of pregnancy'. According to the NHS week-by-week guidance, chloasma is one of the common skin changes of pregnancy, alongside greasier, spotty skin and the dark line (linea nigra) that can appear down the middle of the tummy. Chloasma happens because pregnancy hormones stimulate the cells that produce skin pigment, and the patches typically appear on the cheeks, forehead, nose, or upper lip. It is harmless to you and your baby. Sun exposure makes the darkening more pronounced, so using sunscreen and limiting direct sun on the face helps prevent patches from deepening. Chloasma usually fades gradually after the baby is born as hormone levels settle, although it can take some months.

Is itching normal during pregnancy? Mild itching is common in pregnancy and is usually harmless. It often happens because of increased blood supply to the skin and because the skin of the growing bump stretches, and hormonal changes can also play a part. According to the NHS, these ordinary itching symptoms are usually not harmful in pregnancy, and a GP can offer advice and treatment if they are bothering you. To ease itching and prevent damage to your skin, try to pat or tap your skin instead of scratching it, keep skin moisturised, wear loose cotton clothing, and avoid overheating. However, itching in pregnancy can sometimes be caused by a liver condition called intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP), which can be serious and needs to be treated with medicine, so certain itching patterns, particularly itching involving the palms of the hands and soles of the feet, need prompt medical assessment.

When should I get urgent help for itching in pregnancy? Most pregnancy itching is harmless, but some patterns can signal intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP), a liver condition that needs urgent treatment. According to the NHS, you should contact your maternity unit if you have itching, particularly itching that affects the palms of your hands or the soles of your feet, itching that is worse at night, or itching alongside dark urine, pale stools, or yellowing of the skin and eyes (jaundice). Call your maternity unit if you have the phone number; if you cannot contact your maternity unit, call the NHS 111 service. Do not wait for your next routine appointment, because ICP is diagnosed with blood tests measuring liver function and bile acids, and identifying it promptly allows proper monitoring and treatment. Itching without a rash that keeps getting worse should always be reported to your midwife or doctor.

What is intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (obstetric cholestasis)? Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP), also called obstetric cholestasis, is a liver condition of pregnancy in which bile acids build up in the body. According to NHS guidance, the main symptom is itching, usually without a rash, which is often worse at night and most noticeable on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet, although it can occur anywhere on the body. Less common symptoms include tenderness or pain on the right side just under the rib cage, dark urine, and jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes). Symptoms typically start from around 30 weeks of pregnancy, though ICP can develop earlier. ICP is diagnosed by excluding other causes of itching and by blood tests, including liver function tests and bile acid tests. Itching is very common in pregnancy and only a small number of people will have ICP, but unexplained itching should always be checked.
source_name: NHS (Barking, Havering and Redbridge University Hospitals NHS Trust)

text: How is intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy monitored and managed? If you are diagnosed with intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP), your care team will monitor you closely for the rest of your pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, bile acid levels can rise and fall, so you will be tested regularly during pregnancy to keep track of your levels. Recent research shows that the risk of stillbirth is increased mainly in women carrying one baby whose bile acids reach 100 micromoles per litre or more; most women with ICP have bile acids below this level, so their stillbirth risk is similar to that of pregnant women in general, which many people find reassuring. Depending on your bile acid levels and how your pregnancy is progressing, you may be offered an induction of labour to reduce the risk of stillbirth. Possible complications of ICP include premature birth, which affects roughly 1 in 10 diagnosed women, and the baby passing meconium before or during labour.
source_name: NHS (Barking, Havering and Redbridge University Hospitals NHS Trust)

text: Why do I have thrush during pregnancy and can it harm my baby? Thrush is a yeast infection caused by the Candida species of fungus, usually Candida albicans. According to the NHS, this fungus lives harmlessly in the vagina of many people because normal bacteria keep its growth under control, but if that balance changes, for example because you are pregnant, taking antibiotics, or taking the combined contraceptive pill, the fungus can overgrow and cause symptoms. Typical symptoms are a white discharge with a cottage cheese appearance that does not usually smell, along with itching and irritation around the vagina and vulva. Pregnancy makes thrush more likely because of the changes going on in the body, especially during the third trimester. Reassuringly, there is no evidence that thrush can harm an unborn baby, but it can be uncomfortable, and effective pregnancy-safe treatment is available.

How is thrush treated during pregnancy? If you think you have thrush while pregnant, do not simply buy an over-the-counter treatment: according to the NHS, you should see a GP or midwife before using treatments for thrush during pregnancy, because not all thrush treatments are recommended when you are pregnant. Thrush during pregnancy can be treated with a cream or a tablet inserted into the vagina, called a pessary, containing clotrimazole or a similar antifungal medicine, which act locally and are considered safe in pregnancy. Your doctor or midwife can confirm the diagnosis, since itching and discharge can also be caused by other conditions that need different treatment, and can advise on the right product and course length. To reduce recurrence, wear breathable cotton underwear, avoid perfumed soaps and hygiene products around the genital area, and change out of damp clothing promptly. Return to your GP if symptoms do not clear with treatment.

When will I first feel my baby move? You should start to feel your baby move between 16 and 24 weeks of pregnancy. According to the NHS, if this is your first baby, you might not feel movements until after 20 weeks, because first-time mothers often take a little longer to recognise the sensations. Early movements can feel like a gentle swirling or fluttering, sometimes described as butterflies, and as pregnancy progresses you will feel more definite kicks and jerky movements. Importantly, if you have not felt your baby move by 24 weeks, tell your midwife; they will check your baby's heartbeat and movements, and further checks such as an ultrasound can be arranged if needed. Once movements are established, you should continue to feel your baby move right up to and during labour, so movements never normally stop or tail off as the due date approaches.

What should I do if my baby is moving less than usual? Contact your midwife or maternity unit immediately if your baby's movements slow down, change, or stop. According to the NHS, they will need to check your baby's movements and heartbeat, and you should not wait until the next day: call immediately, even if it is the middle of the night. There is no set number of movements you should feel each day, because every baby is different, and you do not need to count kicks; instead, get to know your baby's own normal pattern of movements, and act on any change from that pattern. Regular, strong fetal movements are a key indicator of your baby's wellbeing, and a drop in movements can sometimes mean the baby needs help, so prompt checking matters. Never use a home doppler to reassure yourself instead of calling; hearing a heartbeat does not mean the baby is well.

Do babies move less at the end of pregnancy? No, this is a myth. According to NHS guidance, babies do not move less during the later stages of pregnancy due to lack of space; you should continue to feel your baby move right up to and during labour, although the type of movement may change from sharp kicks to bigger rolling or squirming movements. If something does not feel right about your baby's movements at any stage, call your maternity assessment unit and discuss your concerns with a midwife rather than waiting. It is also reassuring to know that fewer movements do not automatically mean something is wrong: checks usually reveal that everything is fine, and most people who experience one episode of reduced movements go on to have a straightforward pregnancy and a healthy baby. However, every episode of reduced movements should be checked the same day, every time, even if previous checks were normal.
source_name: NHS (Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is the 20-week scan and what does it look for? The 20-week scan, formally the fetal anomaly ultrasound scan, is a screening test that takes place between 18 weeks and 20 weeks plus 6 days of pregnancy. According to NHS screening programme information, the scan looks for 11 specific physical conditions in your baby by examining in detail the baby's abdomen, bones, brain, face, heart, kidneys, and spinal cord. Most scans are carried out by specially trained staff called sonographers, who can usually tell you the findings at the time of the scan. Screening is your choice, and you do not have to have this scan. It is also important to understand the scan's limits: it cannot find every condition, and there is always a chance a baby may be born with a condition that could not be seen on the scan. All babies are offered a full physical examination after birth as well.

When should I have the whooping cough vaccine in pregnancy? The whooping cough (pertussis) vaccine is recommended in every pregnancy to protect your newborn baby. According to the NHS, you should usually have the whooping cough vaccine around the time of your mid-pregnancy scan, usually at 20 weeks pregnant, but you can have it from 16 weeks. To give the best protection to your baby, aim to have the vaccine before 32 weeks; if you miss that window, you can still have it later, though it may be less effective very close to birth. Vaccination in pregnancy is highly effective in protecting your baby from developing whooping cough in the first weeks of life: the immunity you build passes to your baby through the placenta and provides passive protection until they are old enough to receive their own routine whooping cough vaccination at 8 weeks old. If you are 20 weeks pregnant and have not been offered it, contact your midwife or GP surgery.

Is the whooping cough vaccine safe in pregnancy? Yes, the evidence strongly supports its safety. According to the NHS, pertussis-containing vaccine has been used routinely in pregnant women in the UK since October 2012, and the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) carefully monitors the ongoing safety of all medicines. An MHRA study of around 20,000 vaccinated pregnant women, published in the British Medical Journal, found no evidence of risks to pregnancy or to babies. Many other countries, including the United States, Argentina, Belgium, Spain, Australia, and New Zealand, also recommend whooping cough vaccination in pregnancy. Published research from the UK programme shows that vaccinating pregnant women against whooping cough has been highly effective in protecting young babies during their most vulnerable early weeks, before they can receive their own first vaccines. If you have concerns about the vaccine, discuss them with your midwife or GP, who can address your specific situation.

What pain relief options are available during labour? Labour pain can be managed in many different ways, and there is no right or wrong choice; what works well for one person may not suit another. According to NHS guidance, options range from self-help techniques such as breathing exercises, relaxation, movement, massage, and warm water, through to gas and air (Entonox), painkilling injections such as pethidine, and epidurals, which give the most complete pain relief. TENS machines can also help, particularly in early labour. Not every option is available in every birth setting: gas and air and self-help methods are available at home and in midwife-led units, while epidurals require an anaesthetist and are only available in hospital delivery suites. It is best to keep an open mind, learn about the options in advance through your midwife and antenatal classes, and switch approaches during labour if something is not working for you.

How do breathing and relaxation techniques help with labour pain? Breathing and relaxation techniques are non-drug pain relievers you can learn before labour, usually in antenatal classes. According to NHS guidance, pain often feels worse when you are tense and anxious, while when you are relaxed your body releases its own hormone-like pain relievers called endorphins. Learning relaxation techniques therefore helps prevent pain from becoming overwhelming and helps you cope with contractions without being controlled by them. These techniques also keep you in control, which many people find empowering during labour, and they can be combined with any other form of pain relief at any stage. Typical approaches include slow, deep breathing in through the nose and out through the mouth during contractions, focusing attention on the breath rather than the pain, muscle relaxation exercises, massage from a birth partner, and staying mobile and upright. They are available in every birth setting, including home births.

How does gas and air work during labour? Gas and air, also known as Entonox, is a mixture of nitrous oxide and oxygen that you breathe in during contractions. According to NHS guidance, you breathe it through a mouthpiece that you hold and control yourself, and it takes about 15 to 20 seconds to work, so the key technique is to take slow, deep breaths starting just as a contraction begins, so the effect peaks with the contraction. Once the contraction is over you remove the mouthpiece and breathe normally. Gas and air does not remove all pain, but it takes the edge off and helps you focus on breathing, supporting a feeling of calm and control. Most people like it because it is easy to use, you stay mobile while using it, and there are no known harmful side effects for your baby. It can make you feel lightheaded, dizzy, or briefly sick, but these effects pass quickly when you stop breathing it.

What is pethidine and when is it used in labour? Pethidine is a painkilling injection given into your thigh or buttock to relieve labour pain, and it can also help you relax. According to the NHS, it takes about 20 minutes to work after the injection, and the effects last between 2 and 4 hours, so it is not recommended if you are getting close to the pushing (second) stage of labour. A similar medicine called diamorphine is sometimes used instead. There are side effects to be aware of: these medicines can make you feel woozy, sick, or forgetful, and if pethidine or diamorphine is given too close to the time of delivery, it may affect the baby's breathing, in which case another medicine is given to reverse the effect. These medicines can also interfere with the baby's first feed. Your midwife will discuss whether pethidine is suitable for your situation and timing.

What is an epidural and how well does it work? An epidural is a type of local anaesthetic that numbs the nerves carrying pain signals from the birth canal to the brain. According to the NHS, in most cases an epidural gives complete pain relief, and it can be especially helpful in a long or particularly painful labour. Only an anaesthetist can give an epidural, so it is not available for home births, and if you think you might want one, it is worth checking whether anaesthetists are always available at your chosen hospital. How much you can move your legs afterwards depends on the local anaesthetic used; some hospitals offer 'mobile' epidurals that let you walk around, though these require remote monitoring of the baby's heart rate, which not all hospitals have. An epidural provides very good pain relief, but it is not always completely effective, and the anaesthetist can adjust it if needed.

What should I consider before choosing an epidural? An epidural is the most effective form of labour pain relief, but it involves trade-offs worth knowing in advance. According to NHS guidance, an epidural takes time: around 20 to 40 minutes to insert, during which you need to sit or lie very still, and around another 20 minutes to start working, so if your labour is progressing very fast there may not be time for it to work effectively. With an epidural you are more likely to have an instrumental birth, using forceps or ventouse, because the numbness can make it harder to push effectively in the second stage. You will also need a drip in your arm, regular blood pressure checks, and usually continuous monitoring of the baby's heart rate. Hospitals aim to start an epidural promptly after you ask for one, but during busy periods you may need to wait, and other pain relief can be offered meanwhile.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Can I use water for pain relief during labour? Yes, labouring in warm water is a recognised method of pain relief. According to NHS guidance, relaxing in water is one of the simple approaches to easing labour discomfort, and many birth centres and hospitals offer birthing pools; a warm bath at home can help in early labour too. If you use a birthing pool, the water is kept at a comfortable temperature, but it will not be above 37 degrees Celsius, to keep you and the baby safe from overheating. Warm water supports your weight, helps your muscles relax, and can make contractions feel more manageable, and being in the pool still allows you to change positions freely. Water can be combined with gas and air in most settings. Ask your midwife about pool availability where you plan to give birth, since pools can be in use by others when you arrive.
source_name: NHS (Ashford and St Peter's Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Does a TENS machine help with labour pain? TENS stands for transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation. A TENS machine delivers mild electrical pulses through pads placed on your back, which are thought to interfere with pain signals and stimulate the body's own pain-relieving endorphins. According to NHS guidance, you can hire your own TENS machine to use in labour, including at home in early labour. TENS has not been shown to be as effective during the active phase of labour, when contractions become longer, stronger, and more frequent; it is probably most effective in the early stages, when many women experience low back pain. Because it is drug-free, self-controlled, and has no known effects on the baby, TENS is a popular first option that can be combined with, or followed by, other forms of pain relief as labour progresses. If you plan to use one, practise putting it on and using the controls before labour starts.
source_name: NHS (Ashford and St Peter's Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What are the baby blues? Many people feel a bit down, tearful, or anxious in the first week after giving birth. According to NHS guidance, this is often called the baby blues, and it is so common that it is considered a normal part of the postnatal period. The baby blues are thought to be linked to the sudden hormonal and chemical changes that take place in the body after childbirth, combined with exhaustion and the enormity of new parenthood. Typical experiences include mood swings, irritability, tearfulness, and feeling mildly low at a time when you expected to feel happy, which can itself be confusing. The key feature of the baby blues is that they are short-lived: they do not last for more than 2 weeks after giving birth. If your symptoms last longer than 2 weeks, or start later, you could have postnatal depression and should seek advice from your midwife, health visitor, or GP.

What is postnatal depression and when can it start? Postnatal depression is a type of depression that many parents experience after having a baby. According to NHS guidance, it is a common problem, affecting more than 1 in every 10 women within a year of giving birth, and it can also affect fathers and partners. Postnatal depression affects everyone differently: it can start at any point in the first year after giving birth, it may develop suddenly or gradually, and it can also continue on from depression that began during pregnancy. It is different from the short-lived baby blues, which settle within 2 weeks of birth. It is important to seek help as soon as possible if you think you might be depressed, because symptoms can last months or get worse without support, affecting you, your baby, and your family. With the right support, most people make a full recovery.
source_name: NHS (South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What are the symptoms of postnatal depression? According to NHS guidance, the main symptoms of postnatal depression include a persistent feeling of sadness and low mood, lack of enjoyment and loss of interest in the wider world, lack of energy and feeling tired all the time, trouble sleeping at night while feeling sleepy during the day, difficulty bonding with your baby, withdrawing from contact with other people, problems concentrating and making decisions, loss of appetite, and excessive anxiety about the baby. Some people also experience frightening thoughts, for example about hurting their baby; these thoughts can be very distressing to have, but they are very rarely acted upon, and having them is a recognised symptom rather than a reflection of who you are as a parent. Many people do not realise they have postnatal depression because it can develop gradually. If several of these symptoms describe your experience, talk to your GP, midwife, or health visitor; effective treatment is available.

Can depression happen during pregnancy, not just after? Yes. Depression during pregnancy, known as antenatal depression, is common. According to NHS guidance, around 1 in 8 people will experience depression during their pregnancy, and other mental health symptoms such as anxiety and panic attacks can also occur during or after pregnancy. Antenatal depression matters in its own right and also because untreated depression in pregnancy can increase the chance of developing postnatal depression, so asking for help while you are pregnant is important rather than waiting to see how you feel after the birth. If you are already taking medication for your mental health when you become pregnant, do not stop or change it on your own; speak to your GP or psychiatrist, who can review your treatment safely. Support in pregnancy can include talking therapies, specialist perinatal mental health services, and medication where appropriate, and your midwife can help you access it.
source_name: NHS (South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Is anxiety normal during pregnancy and after birth, and when is it a problem? It is common for most new parents to experience some anxiety around pregnancy, childbirth, and becoming a new parent; a degree of worry is a normal response to a major life change. According to NHS guidance, the concern arises when this anxiety does not ease, or when worries become constant and start to affect your day-to-day activities; this may be a sign of perinatal anxiety, a recognised and treatable condition. The worries can be about the safety of your child, about yourself, or about your ability to cope, and perinatal anxiety can include restlessness, racing thoughts, physical tension, and panic attacks. It can occur during pregnancy or after birth, with or without depression. If anxiety is interfering with your sleep, relationships, or daily functioning, talk to your midwife, health visitor, or GP; talking therapies and other effective support are available through perinatal mental health services.

Can I recover from postnatal depression? Yes. According to NHS guidance, it is possible for postnatal depression to start any time in the first year after giving birth, but with the right support most people make a full recovery. The most important step is seeking help early: symptoms can last for months or get worse if untreated, so speak to your GP, midwife, or health visitor as soon as you think something is wrong rather than hoping it will pass. Support options range from self-help strategies and peer support, through guided self-help courses and talking therapies, to antidepressant medication for more severe depression, including options that are safe to use while breastfeeding. Practical steps also help alongside professional treatment: maintaining a healthy lifestyle, accepting help with the baby, resting when possible, and having someone you can talk to. Postnatal depression is an illness, not a failure of parenting, and treating it benefits both you and your baby.

What can I do to protect my mental health as a new or expectant parent? Having a baby is a life-changing event that can sometimes trigger depression or anxiety even in people with no history of mental health problems, because adapting to parenthood takes time and looking after a small baby can be stressful and exhausting. According to NHS guidance, there are things you can do to help keep yourself well: maintain a healthy lifestyle with reasonable sleep, food, and activity where possible, and make sure you have someone you can talk to and turn to for support. Going to antenatal classes and making friends with other pregnant women or new parents can be particularly helpful, because it builds a support network of people going through the same stage of life. Risk factors such as a history of mental health problems make postnatal depression more likely, so tell your midwife about any previous mental health difficulties early in pregnancy so extra support can be planned.
source_name: NHS (South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust)

text: How should I plan for pain relief before labour starts? It helps to learn about pain relief options during pregnancy rather than deciding for the first time during labour. According to NHS guidance, learning about labour and what is going to happen can itself help you feel more prepared and in control; ask your midwife what to expect and try antenatal classes for more information. Practising relaxation and breathing techniques during pregnancy gives you a reliable tool for staying calm and managing pain in labour. It is sensible to note pain relief preferences in your birth plan while keeping an open mind, because labours are unpredictable: one method may work well for some people and not others, and if something is not working during labour, you can change approach. Discuss the different kinds of pain relief with your midwife, doctor, or an anaesthetist in advance, including what is available at your planned birth location, since options differ between home, birth centres, and hospital.

What is a caesarean section and how common is it? A caesarean section, or C-section, is an operation to deliver your baby through a cut made in your lower abdomen and into your womb. According to NHS guidance, the cut is usually 15 to 20 centimetres long, just below the bikini line. Around 1 in 4 pregnant women in the UK have a caesarean, so it is a very common way to give birth. Caesareans are done for many different reasons: some are planned in advance (elective), for example when the baby is in a breech position with feet or bottom first instead of head down, when the placenta is lying low, or for certain medical conditions; others are unplanned (emergency), decided during labour when a vaginal birth becomes unsafe for mother or baby. Recovery generally takes about six weeks, although this varies from person to person.
source_name: NHS (North Tees and Hartlepool NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What happens during a caesarean section? Caesarean sections are carried out in hospital. According to the NHS, on the day you will change into a hospital gown, a thin flexible tube called a catheter will be inserted into your bladder to keep it empty during the operation, and a small area of pubic hair may be trimmed if necessary. Most caesareans are done under a spinal or epidural anaesthetic, so you are awake but numb from the chest down, and a screen is placed across your body. The surgeon makes a cut in your tummy and womb and delivers the baby, usually within the first few minutes; the whole procedure typically takes around 40 to 50 minutes. Your womb is closed with dissolvable stitches, and the cut in your tummy is closed with dissolvable stitches or with stitches or staples that are removed after a few days. You are then moved to a recovery room and observed regularly.

How do I prepare for a planned caesarean section? If there is time to plan your caesarean, you will be given a date for the operation. According to the NHS, you will be asked to attend a hospital appointment in the week before the caesarean is due, where checks are done and the procedure is explained. You will be given some medicine to take before the procedure; this may include antibiotics to prevent infection, anti-sickness medicine (anti-emetics), and medicine to reduce the acidity of your stomach acid (antacids). You will need to stop eating and drinking a few hours before the operation, and your doctor or midwife will tell you exactly when. It helps to pack your hospital bag as you would for any birth, arrange support at home for the recovery weeks, and prepare questions in advance, since caesarean recovery takes longer than recovery from a straightforward vaginal birth.

What happens in hospital after a caesarean section? You will probably be in hospital for 1 or 2 days after a caesarean section. According to the NHS, most women can leave hospital 1 to 2 days after the operation, once you and your baby are well. While in hospital you will be given treatment to reduce the risk of blood clots, which may include compression stockings, injections of blood-thinning medicine, or both, because clot risk is raised after surgery and birth. You can have food and water as soon as you feel hungry or thirsty, and you will be offered help with breastfeeding your baby if you want it; a caesarean does not prevent breastfeeding or immediate skin-to-skin contact in most cases. Staff will help you get up and moving as soon as possible, because early gentle movement supports recovery. When you go home, arrange for someone to drive you, as you will not be able to drive for a few weeks.

How do I recover at home after a caesarean section? Most women have some discomfort for the first few days after a caesarean, and for some the pain lasts several weeks, so plan to take things easy. According to the NHS, make sure you have regular painkillers at home for as long as you need them; for most women paracetamol or ibuprofen are suitable, but aspirin and the stronger painkiller codeine found in co-codamol are not usually recommended if you are breastfeeding, so ask your doctor about the best option for you. You will have some vaginal bleeding: use period pads rather than tampons to reduce the risk of spreading infection, and get medical advice if bleeding is heavy. Stay gently mobile with activities like a daily walk to reduce the risk of blood clots, but do not overexert yourself. You should be able to hold and carry your baby once you are home.

How do I look after my caesarean wound and what will the scar be like? Your midwife will advise you on caring for your caesarean wound as it heals. According to the NHS, if your wound was closed with non-dissolvable stitches or staples, these are usually taken out by your midwife after 5 to 7 days; dissolvable stitches disappear on their own. Keep the wound clean and dry, watch for signs of infection such as increasing redness, swelling, pain, or discharge, and report them promptly. If the wound is sore, paracetamol or ibuprofen (but not aspirin) are usually the better painkillers while breastfeeding. The wound will eventually form a scar, usually a horizontal line about 10 to 20 centimetres long just below your bikini line; in rare cases the scar is vertical, below the bellybutton. The scar fades over time and is typically hidden by underwear. Full recovery generally takes about six weeks, though everyone heals at their own pace.

Can I have a vaginal birth after a previous caesarean (VBAC)? In most cases, yes. VBAC stands for vaginal birth after caesarean, and according to NHS guidance, most women who have had a previous caesarean section can safely have a vaginal birth in a subsequent pregnancy. The success rates are encouraging: overall, about three out of four women (75%) with a straightforward pregnancy who go into labour spontaneously give birth vaginally after one previous caesarean, and if you have also had a vaginal birth at some point, before or after your caesarean, about nine out of ten women (90%) who choose VBAC give birth vaginally. Most reasons for a first caesarean are unlikely to recur. A small number of women are advised to have another caesarean, depending on the reason for the first one or complications in the current pregnancy, so discuss your individual situation with your midwife and obstetrician.
source_name: NHS (Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What are the benefits of a vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)? A successful VBAC has real advantages compared with a repeat caesarean. According to NHS guidance, you will recover more quickly after the birth than with a caesarean and go home sooner, which matters especially if you have other children at home. The risk of your baby having breathing problems after birth is lower with a VBAC than with a caesarean, and you are more likely to be successful with breastfeeding. Giving birth vaginally also gives many women a sense of satisfaction, and a successful vaginal birth makes future labours less complicated with fewer risks, whereas each additional caesarean makes future births more complicated. Success is more likely if labour starts spontaneously, if you have given birth vaginally before, and if you were a normal weight for your height at the start of pregnancy. Your care team will help you weigh these benefits against the risks for your situation.
source_name: NHS (Sandwell and West Birmingham NHS Trust)

text: How is a VBAC labour monitored and why? If you plan a vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC), some extra precautions are recommended during labour. According to NHS guidance, women planning a VBAC are advised to give birth on a delivery suite (labour ward) rather than at home or in a standalone birth centre, so that your baby's heartbeat can be monitored continuously during labour to ensure their wellbeing. Continuous monitoring matters because changes in the baby's heartbeat can be an early sign of problems with your previous caesarean scar, the main specific risk of VBAC. Being on the delivery suite also means there is easy access to an operating theatre and the medical team if a caesarean is recommended during labour. Many units still support mobility during monitored VBAC labour, including cordless monitoring and sometimes birth pool access, so ask what facilities your hospital offers when making your birth plan.
source_name: NHS (Kingston and Richmond NHS Foundation Trust)

text: When is a planned repeat caesarean carried out? If you choose an elective repeat caesarean section (ERCS) after a previous caesarean, the timing of the operation is chosen carefully. According to NHS guidance, you will usually have the operation after 39 weeks of pregnancy. This is because babies born by caesarean section earlier than 39 weeks are more likely to need admission to the special care baby unit for help with their breathing; the final weeks of pregnancy help the baby's lungs mature and clear fluid, which the process of labour would otherwise assist with. In considering your options between a planned repeat caesarean and a vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC), your previous pregnancies and medical history are important factors, and structured decision tools and antenatal classes for planned caesareans can help. If labour starts before your planned date, contact your maternity unit straight away for advice.
source_name: NHS (Bradford Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Why is anaemia common in pregnancy? Anaemia means you do not have enough healthy red blood cells or haemoglobin to carry oxygen around your body, and the most common type is iron-deficiency anaemia. According to NHS guidance, it is common for women to develop iron deficiency during pregnancy because the demand for iron and other vitamins is increased: the mother must increase her production of red blood cells, and the baby also needs its own supply of iron, which can only be obtained from the mother. It is also normal for haemoglobin levels to drop a little in pregnancy, because there is far more fluid in the blood diluting the red blood cells; this dilution on its own is a normal feature of pregnancy. Symptoms of true anaemia can include tiredness, weakness, breathlessness, and paleness. Blood tests during routine antenatal care identify anaemia so it can be treated before birth.
source_name: NHS (Hull University Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust)

text: When is anaemia checked for during pregnancy? Screening for anaemia is a routine part of antenatal care, so most cases are picked up through standard blood tests rather than symptoms. According to NHS guidance, your blood is checked for anaemia at your booking visit early in pregnancy and again at around 28 weeks of pregnancy, when the demands of the growing baby are rising and iron stores are most likely to have run low. If you are at increased risk of anaemia, for example because of low iron stores before pregnancy, a pre-existing blood condition such as sickle cell disease or thalassaemia, a twin pregnancy, or a bowel condition affecting iron absorption, you may be tested more often. If a test shows you are anaemic, your midwife or doctor will discuss treatment, usually starting with iron tablets and dietary changes, and a follow-up blood test checks the treatment is working.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Coventry and Warwickshire NHS Trust)

text: Which foods are good sources of iron during pregnancy? A good balanced diet is important for getting enough iron in pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, the easiest iron for your body to absorb comes from red meat, fish, and poultry, so including a portion regularly helps maintain iron levels. There are also good vegetarian sources, including lentils, fortified breakfast cereals, and leafy green vegetables such as spinach, along with beans and other pulses. Vitamin C helps your body absorb iron from food, so pairing iron-rich meals with vitamin C sources like orange juice, other fruits, and vegetables improves uptake. Equally important is knowing what blocks absorption: tea, coffee, and calcium-rich foods and drinks such as milk and other dairy products can make it harder to absorb iron, so avoid taking them at the same time as iron-rich meals or iron tablets. Spacing them a couple of hours apart solves the problem.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Coventry and Warwickshire NHS Trust)

text: How is iron-deficiency anaemia treated in pregnancy? Iron-deficiency anaemia in pregnancy is treated with dietary changes plus iron supplements, and how you take the tablets makes a real difference. According to NHS guidance, the recommended tablets are ferrous sulfate, and they work best when taken with orange juice about one hour before breakfast, because vitamin C boosts iron absorption while an empty stomach avoids interference from food; if you do not drink orange juice, another vitamin C-containing drink works too. Avoid taking iron tablets with tea, coffee, milk, or other calcium-rich foods and drinks, and with multivitamins, as these reduce absorption. If you are prescribed iron, it is important to take the tablets and finish the course, and a follow-up blood test 2 to 4 weeks after starting checks that your levels are rising. Iron tablets can cause side effects such as constipation, dark stools, or nausea; tell your midwife if these are a problem, because alternatives exist.

What should I know at 22 weeks pregnant? At 22 weeks, your baby is at a crucial stage of lung development, which makes this an especially important time to stay smoke-free; cigarettes starve your baby of oxygen and increase the risk of miscarriage, stillbirth, and cot death, and free NHS stop smoking support is available through your midwife or doctor. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, this is also the period when you should make sure you have had the whooping cough vaccine, usually offered around week 20 but available from week 16 to week 32, to protect your baby from this dangerous infection after birth. A common symptom around now is piles (haemorrhoids), which are swellings inside or around your bottom, often triggered by pregnancy hormones and constipation; a high-fibre diet, plenty of fluids, and not straining all help, and your pharmacist can suggest suitable creams.

What symptoms are common at 23 weeks pregnant? At 23 weeks, in the middle of the second trimester, your body continues adapting to your growing baby. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, common signs of pregnancy at this stage include tiredness and sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen and bleeding gums, and pains on the side of your baby bump caused by your expanding womb, known as round ligament pains. Two symptoms that often surprise people at this stage are sore or leaky breasts, as the breasts begin preparing for feeding, and a white, milky pregnancy discharge from the vagina, which is normal. Light spotting can sometimes occur, but you should always seek medical advice for any vaginal bleeding in pregnancy rather than assuming it is harmless. Skin changes such as darkened patches on the face (chloasma) may also continue. Mention any symptom that is severe, sudden, or worrying to your midwife.

Can I fly while pregnant and until when? Flying is generally possible in a healthy pregnancy, but planning matters. According to NHS guidance, if you have any pregnancy complications, check with your midwife or GP that there is no medical reason to stop you flying, such as high blood pressure or a raised risk of deep vein thrombosis. Airline rules are the other key factor: most airlines will not let you fly if you are within about a month of your due date, and some require a letter from your GP or midwife confirming you are fit to fly once you are around 7 months pregnant, so always check with the airline before you book. If you are planning to travel abroad, also discuss vaccinations and travel insurance with your midwife or doctor, and be aware that recovering from jet lag may take a bit longer than usual while pregnant.

What is the DVT risk when travelling during pregnancy? Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot in a deep vein in your leg, calf, or pelvis, and pregnancy already increases your risk of blood clots, so long journeys need extra care. According to NHS guidance, if you are travelling on a long-distance flight or will be sitting still for 4 hours or more, talk to your midwife before you travel, because you may need medication or compression stockings to reduce the risk. During any long journey, drink plenty of water, move your feet and ankles regularly, take breaks to walk around when possible, and wear the compression stockings your midwife or doctor recommends. Seek urgent medical help if you develop a swollen, painful, red, or warm leg (especially one leg), or sudden breathlessness or chest pain during or after travel, as these can be signs of a clot needing immediate treatment.

Can I take over-the-counter medicines while pregnant? Be cautious with all medicines during pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, only a few prescription and over-the-counter medicines have been shown to be safe and effective while you are pregnant, so try to avoid over-the-counter medicines if possible, and discuss any you feel you need with your pharmacist, who can advise on pregnancy-safe options. While you are pregnant, your doctor will only prescribe medicines where the benefits are greater than the risks, which is why it is essential to tell every prescriber and pharmacist that you are pregnant. The same caution applies to complementary and herbal therapies: few complementary therapies are known to be safe and effective during pregnancy, so check with your midwife, doctor, or pharmacist before using them. Never stop a prescribed medicine for a long-term condition on your own; sudden stopping can be more dangerous than continuing, so always talk to your doctor first.

How does smoking affect my baby during pregnancy? Babies receive all the oxygen, nutrients, and antibodies they need from their mother's blood supply. According to NHS guidance, when a pregnant woman smokes, or breathes in other people's smoke, she inhales carbon monoxide and the other damaging chemicals in cigarettes, and this restricts the amount of oxygen the baby gets. Every cigarette affects the baby's growth and development and makes the baby's heart beat harder. Continuing to smoke during pregnancy increases the risks of serious complications, including miscarriage, poor growth, premature birth, stillbirth, and sudden infant death after birth. The encouraging news is that stopping helps you and your baby immediately: once you stop smoking, carbon monoxide clears from your bloodstream quickly and your baby starts getting more of the oxygen needed to grow. The earlier in pregnancy you stop, the better, but quitting at any stage brings real benefits.

What help is available to stop smoking during pregnancy? Pregnancy is one of the best opportunities to quit smoking, and structured support dramatically improves your chances. According to NHS guidance, your chance of quitting is around 3 times higher with the help of a friendly stop smoking adviser, who can help you understand your smoking, build a personalised quit plan, and address concerns specific to pregnancy. Ask your community midwife to refer you to your local stop smoking service; support is free, non-judgemental, and available for other members of your household too, since a smoke-free home protects the baby after birth as well. Stopping smoking is widely regarded as the single best thing you can do for your baby's health in pregnancy. A note on vaping: vaping is not harmless, and if you have never smoked, you should not start vaping. Discuss the safest quitting approach for you with your midwife or adviser.

Are recreational drugs and cannabis safe during pregnancy? No. According to NHS guidance, recreational drugs should not be taken during pregnancy, as they may seriously harm you and your baby. This includes cannabis: cannabis smoking can have similar effects on the lungs as smoking tobacco and may also be harmful to your baby, so it should be avoided throughout pregnancy, including in edible or vaped forms whose effects on the developing baby are not established as safe. Drugs can affect the baby's growth, brain development, and wellbeing after birth, and some cause withdrawal symptoms in newborns. If you use recreational drugs and become pregnant, the most important step is honesty with your midwife or doctor: maternity services are there to help, not to judge, and confidential specialist support is available to help you stop safely, since suddenly stopping some substances also needs medical guidance. Getting support early gives your baby the best outcome.

Why does my newborn cry so much? Crying is normal behaviour for babies, and it is the only way your baby can tell you how they feel or what they need. According to NHS guidance, common reasons a baby might cry include hunger or thirst, a wet or dirty nappy, tiredness, being too hot or too cold, wanting comfort and closeness, or being unwell. The first weeks and months with a new baby can be challenging, and it can feel especially overwhelming when you are struggling to soothe a crying baby while exhausted yourself. It helps to remember two things: crying is normal, and it will stop as your baby matures. Work through the basics first — offer a feed, check and change the nappy, check temperature — and then try comfort techniques. If your baby's cry sounds unusual, or they seem unwell alongside the crying, trust your instincts and seek medical advice.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: How can I soothe my crying baby? Once you have checked that your baby is not hungry or thirsty and has a clean nappy, there are several comfort techniques to try. According to NHS guidance, cuddling and skin-to-skin contact are powerful soothers, and importantly, you cannot 'spoil' a baby by giving them too many cuddles and too much close contact; responding to your baby builds security rather than bad habits. Gently rocking your baby in your arms or a cradle can help, as can quiet talking or singing, movement such as a walk in the pram or sling, a warm bath, and reducing stimulation by dimming lights and noise. Be patient with yourself and your baby: these techniques may not always work immediately, and it may take a combination of approaches or more than one attempt to settle your baby. Different babies respond to different things, and you will gradually learn what works for yours.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What should I do if my baby's crying feels like too much? Persistent crying is genuinely hard to cope with, especially when you are exhausted, and feeling stressed or desperate is a normal parental experience, not a failure. According to NHS guidance, the crucial safety rule is this: never get cross with your baby physically and never shake them, because shaking can cause serious brain injury. If you start to feel desperate or overwhelmed, place your baby somewhere safe, such as their cot, and walk into another room for a few minutes to calm yourself down; a crying baby who is safe in a cot will come to no harm while you take a breather. Then return when you feel steadier, or ask someone else to take over for a while. If crying is wearing you down day after day, talk to your health visitor or GP; support exists, and helplines for parents of crying babies can help in the moment.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Coventry and Warwickshire NHS Trust)

text: What is colic in babies? Colic is a name used to describe long, frequent episodes of crying in an otherwise healthy baby. According to NHS guidance, it is a common problem affecting up to one in five babies. People used to think colic was caused by problems with the baby's digestion or bowel, but there is no evidence this is true, and the actual cause is unknown. Some perspective on normal crying helps: at 6 to 8 weeks of age, the average baby cries for 2 to 3 hours per day, usually more in the afternoon and evening, and after about 8 weeks babies typically start to cry less and less each week. A doctor may diagnose colic if your baby cries a lot but is otherwise generally well and gaining weight. Colic does not mean you are doing anything wrong as a parent, it will not harm your baby, and it goes away on its own, in most babies by 6 months old.

When is a crying baby a medical emergency? Most crying is normal, but certain features alongside crying suggest a baby is unwell and needs urgent assessment. According to NHS guidance, go to the nearest hospital emergency department or call emergency services if your baby becomes stiff for a prolonged period, has rhythmic jerky movements of the arms or legs that do not stop when you touch them (a possible fit or seizure), becomes extremely agitated and cries inconsolably despite all distraction, or becomes floppy, very lethargic, or difficult to wake. Fever also needs action in young babies: a baby aged 3 to 6 months with a temperature of 38C (100.4F) or above should be assessed, so ring your GP surgery or call the NHS 111 service; in babies under 3 months, any fever warrants urgent same-day medical advice. Crying due to colic, hunger, or tiredness looks different from an unwell baby: trust your instincts and seek help when something feels wrong.
source_name: NHS (Healthier Together, Hertfordshire and West Essex)

text: How do I care for my baby's umbilical cord stump? After birth, the umbilical cord is clamped and cut, leaving a small stump that dries out and falls off by itself. According to NHS guidance, keep the cord clean and dry, and fold the nappy down under the cord so it stays outside the nappy area, which encourages drying and prevents contamination with urine and stools. There is no need to use wipes, powders, or creams on it; if the cord does get dirty, clean it with plain water only, and wash your hands before and after handling the area. Avoid tight clothing so air can circulate around the stump, and try not to touch the cord unnecessarily. When bathing your baby, give a shallow bath and do not immerse the cord in water. The stump will dry, turn dark, and typically fall off between about 5 and 15 days after birth.

What is normal when the umbilical cord stump falls off, and what are signs of infection? The umbilical cord stump usually separates between 5 and 15 days after birth. According to NHS guidance, during separation it is normal to see small amounts of a sticky discharge at the junction between the cord and the skin, and this alone is not a cause for concern. Separation may be delayed if your baby has received antibiotics or if the area has become infected. Watch for the signs of infection, which do need medical review: redness spreading on the skin around the stump, swelling, a bad smell, pus, bleeding beyond a few spots, or your baby developing a fever or seeming unwell. Because the drying stump can be an entry route for infection, hand hygiene matters most: wash your hands before and after all baby care, keep the stump dry and exposed to air, and contact your midwife, health visitor, or GP promptly if anything looks infected.

How many wet and dirty nappies should my newborn have? Nappy contents are one of the best simple indicators that a newborn is feeding well. According to NHS guidance, in the first 48 hours your baby is likely to have only two or three wet nappies, because feed volumes are tiny at first. Wet nappies should then become steadily more frequent, reaching at least six a day from day 5 onwards. Poo changes are equally telling: at the beginning your baby passes meconium, a black sticky poo built up before birth; by day 3 the poo should change to become lighter, runnier, and greener; and by day 5 it should be yellow. A breastfed baby should be passing about two yellow poos a day from day 5. If your baby has fewer wet nappies than expected, or the poo has not changed colour on schedule, tell your midwife promptly, as it can mean the baby needs more milk.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy? Group B Streptococcus, or GBS, is a common bacterium carried in the vagina and rectum of roughly 2 to 4 in 10 women (20 to 40%) in the UK. According to the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG), GBS is not a sexually transmitted disease, and most women carrying it have no symptoms at all. Carrying GBS is not harmful to you, but it can affect your baby around the time of birth: GBS can occasionally cause serious infection in newborn babies, and very rarely during pregnancy before labour. It is important to keep perspective: many babies come into contact with GBS during labour or around birth, and the vast majority of these babies do not become ill. GBS is sometimes found by chance during pregnancy through vaginal or rectal swabs or a urine test taken for other reasons.

Is everyone tested for group B Strep during pregnancy? No. According to RCOG and NHS guidance, the NHS does not routinely offer all pregnant women screening for GBS in the UK. This is a deliberate policy: GBS carriage is very common and usually harmless, a swab result taken weeks before birth may not reflect carriage at delivery, and screening cannot accurately predict which babies will actually develop an infection. GBS is instead often detected by chance through a vaginal or rectal swab or a urine test done for other reasons during pregnancy. If GBS was found in a previous pregnancy, you may be offered a specific swab test (the enriched culture medium, or ECM, test) at 35 to 37 weeks in your next pregnancy to help decide about antibiotics in labour. If you want to know more about testing options, discuss them with your midwife.

What happens if group B Strep is found during my pregnancy? If GBS is detected, the main protective step is antibiotics during labour rather than treatment during pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, most early-onset group B Strep infections in babies can be prevented by giving intravenous (IV) antibiotics during labour to women whose babies are at higher chance of developing the infection. You will be recommended IV antibiotics in labour if you have had a GBS-positive swab or urine test from an NHS or accredited laboratory during your current pregnancy. A urine infection caused by GBS is different: it should be treated with antibiotic tablets straight away when found, and you will still be recommended IV antibiotics during labour as well. Make sure GBS is clearly recorded in your maternity notes, and call your maternity unit promptly when labour starts or your waters break so antibiotics can be given in good time.
source_name: NHS (Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is PPROM (waters breaking early)? PPROM stands for preterm pre-labour rupture of membranes, which means your waters break before 37 weeks of pregnancy and before labour has started. According to NHS guidance, PPROM happens in about 3 in every 100 pregnancies. When it happens, it can, but does not always, trigger early labour. The causes are not always known, but infection is one recognised factor. The risks and the treatment depend on what stage of pregnancy you are at when your waters break; breaking very early, before 24 weeks, carries higher risks because the baby's lungs are still developing and need amniotic fluid around them to develop normally. If you think your waters have broken early, whether a gush or a slow trickle, contact your maternity unit immediately for assessment, because prompt monitoring and treatment significantly affect outcomes for you and your baby.
source_name: NHS (East Kent Hospitals University NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What happens in hospital if my waters break early (PPROM)? If your waters have broken before 37 weeks, you will usually be advised to stay in hospital, where you and your baby are closely monitored for signs of infection; this may be for a few days or longer. According to NHS guidance, monitoring includes having your temperature, blood pressure, and pulse taken regularly, blood tests to check for infection, and regular monitoring of your baby's heart rate. You may be offered antibiotics to help reduce the chances of infection developing, and depending on your stage of pregnancy, steroid injections may be given to help mature the baby's lungs in case of early birth. If assessment shows your waters have not actually broken, you should be able to go home; if you then continue to leak fluid at home, contact the hospital immediately for a further check-up. Your team will discuss what PPROM means for your baby at your specific gestation.
source_name: NHS (East Kent Hospitals University NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is placental abruption and why is it an emergency? Placental abruption is when the placenta separates, or starts to separate, from the wall of the uterus before the baby is born. According to NHS guidance, it can cause pain and heavy bleeding and can be dangerous for both you and your baby, because the placenta is the baby's source of oxygen and nutrients. Placental abruption is an emergency: if you have sudden severe abdominal pain, a hard or tender womb, heavy vaginal bleeding, or reduced baby movements, seek medical attention immediately or call the emergency services. One point is especially important to know: sometimes there can be heavy internal bleeding from an abruption with little or no visible blood loss, and sometimes severe pain with no visible bleeding at all, so severe constant abdominal pain in later pregnancy must never be waited out at home even without obvious bleeding.
source_name: NHS (East Kent Hospitals University NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What are the symptoms of mastitis? Mastitis is inflammation of the breast tissue, common in breastfeeding women, and it usually affects one breast with symptoms that come on quickly. According to the NHS, symptoms include a swollen area on the breast that may feel hot and painful to touch (the area may look red, though redness can be harder to see on black or brown skin), a wedge-shaped breast lump or hard area, a burning pain in the breast that may be constant or only during feeds, and nipple discharge that may be white or streaked with blood. You may also get flu-like symptoms such as aches, a high temperature or feeling hot, cold, or shivery, and tiredness. Mastitis is often caused by a build-up of milk, though women who are not breastfeeding, and men, can also get it. If symptoms do not improve within about a day of self-care, or you feel increasingly unwell, contact your GP.

Should I keep breastfeeding if I have mastitis? Yes. It may seem counterintuitive when your breast is painful, but continuing to feed is a key part of treating mastitis. According to the NHS, if you are breastfeeding you should continue to breastfeed your baby when they want and for as long as they want, and you can also offer a feed whenever your breasts feel uncomfortably full. Feeding keeps milk moving and prevents the build-up from getting worse; stopping suddenly typically makes mastitis worse quickly. If breastfeeding from the affected breast is too painful, or your baby does not want to feed from it, hand express enough milk to feed your baby and to relieve the fullness. The milk is safe for your baby even with mastitis. Alongside feeding, rest, drink fluids, and use appropriate pain relief; if things do not improve or you develop worsening fever, see your GP, as a course of antibiotics may be needed.

What is breast engorgement and why does it happen? Breast engorgement is when your breasts become overly full, making them feel hard, tight, and painful. According to the NHS, in the early days engorgement is often due to your milk coming in: newborns need feeding little and often, and it can take a few days for your milk supply to adjust to match your baby's needs. Engorgement can also happen later if feeds are missed or spaced out suddenly. Engorgement is more than a comfort issue: when the breast is engorged, the nipple can become overstretched and flattened, which makes it hard for your baby to attach well and take milk, creating a cycle where the breast stays too full. If your baby is struggling to attach to an engorged breast, ask your midwife, health visitor, or a breastfeeding specialist for advice immediately, both to relieve the engorgement and to prevent it happening again.

How can I relieve engorged breasts? Several simple measures ease engorgement while keeping milk flowing. According to NHS guidance, a warm bath or shower, or a warm compress such as a warm flannel applied to the breast just before a feed or before expressing, stimulates the let-down reflex and encourages milk to flow; avoid warm compresses between feeds, as warmth then can increase swelling. Hand expressing a small amount of milk before your baby feeds softens the breast tissue around the nipple, making it easier for your baby to attach; you can collect this milk in a sterilised container and store it for later use. Gently massaging the breast as your baby feeds encourages flow and can relieve the tight sensation, and trying different feeding positions helps drain different parts of the breast. Avoid expressing large amounts between feeds, because removing too much signals the breasts to make even more milk.

What is a blocked milk duct and how do I clear it? A blocked duct is when milk does not flow easily through part of the breast, causing a small, tender lump or sore spot.
