According to the NHS, you can help clear a blocked duct by gently massaging the lump towards your nipple while your baby feeds, so the baby's sucking helps draw the milk through, and by applying a cold compress, such as a cloth soaked in cold water, between feeds to reduce pain and swelling. Continue feeding frequently, starting feeds on the affected side when possible, and check that your baby is well attached, since poor attachment leaves parts of the breast undrained. It is important to deal with a blocked duct quickly, because if it is left untreated it can lead to mastitis. If the lump does not clear within a day or two, or you develop fever or spreading redness, contact your midwife, health visitor, or GP.

Why do my nipples hurt when breastfeeding? One of the most common causes of nipple or breast pain during breastfeeding is your baby's position and how well they are attached to the breast. According to the NHS, small changes to your baby's position and attachment can make feeding much more comfortable, which is why painful feeding should prompt a feeding assessment rather than simply enduring it. Signs of good attachment include the baby's chin touching the breast, a wide-open mouth taking a large mouthful of breast (not just the nipple), and rhythmic sucking and swallowing. Breast and nipple pain can sometimes also be caused by a bacterial, viral, or fungal infection, which may come with itching or changes in how the nipple or breast looks. Contact your midwife, GP, or the NHS 111 service if you have pain in your breast or nipple that does not go away, and ask your midwife or a breastfeeding specialist to observe a feed.

How does expressing breast milk work? Expressing means removing milk from your breasts by hand or with a pump, so it can be given to your baby or stored. According to NHS guidance, milk production is driven by hormones that are stimulated by removal of milk, by touching and being close to your baby, and by breast massage and nipple stimulation, which means the more you express, the more breast milk you make. In the first few days after birth, hand expressing into a syringe is recommended, because the early milk (colostrum) comes in small quantities and is quite thick; pump expressing works well once your milk flow increases. Expressing also has practical uses beyond feeding: hand expressing a little milk can encourage a reluctant baby to feed at the breast, soften an engorged breast so the baby can attach, and relieve blocked ducts. Your midwife can teach you effective hand expressing technique.
source_name: NHS (Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is a breast abscess and can I still breastfeed? A breast abscess is a painful collection of pus that forms in the breast, usually as a complication of mastitis that was not treated early or did not respond to treatment. According to the NHS, an abscess can develop if mastitis does not respond to frequent feeding plus a course of antibiotics. An abscess typically appears as a firm, very tender lump, often with redness, warmth, and fever, and it is diagnosed with examination and usually an ultrasound scan. Treatment involves draining the pus, usually with a needle under local anaesthetic, along with antibiotics. Reassuringly, you can carry on breastfeeding while you are having treatment for a breast abscess, and continuing to keep milk moving supports recovery. The best prevention is treating engorgement, blocked ducts, and mastitis promptly, so seek help early whenever breast pain, lumps, or fever develop while breastfeeding.

What follow-up do I need after gestational diabetes? After a pregnancy affected by gestational diabetes, follow-up testing confirms your blood sugar has returned to normal and protects your long-term health. According to NHS guidance, you should have a fasting plasma glucose blood test between 6 and 13 weeks after giving birth to check whether your glucose levels have returned to the normal range. Because gestational diabetes significantly raises the lifetime risk of developing type 2 diabetes, healthy diet and lifestyle habits remain important after birth, and breastfeeding is encouraged partly because it helps reduce the long-term risk of developing type 2 diabetes. Planning matters for future pregnancies too: discuss contraception and the timing of your next pregnancy with your care team, and if you become pregnant again, you should be offered an early glucose tolerance test in the next pregnancy rather than waiting for the usual screening window.

When do I need to start contraception after giving birth? Sooner than most people expect. According to the NHS, you can get pregnant from 3 weeks (21 days) after the birth of a baby, including if you are breastfeeding and your periods have not started again. This is because ovulation happens about two weeks before a period, so you can conceive before your first period returns. You therefore need to use contraception every time you have sex after giving birth unless you want to get pregnant again, and you should not wait for your periods to return or for your 6-week postnatal check before starting contraception. Your midwife will discuss contraception with you after your baby is born, and you can talk to your GP, health visitor, or a sexual health (family planning) clinic about your options at any time. Many methods can be started immediately after birth.

Which contraception can I use straight after birth, including while breastfeeding? Several methods are safe to start immediately after giving birth. According to NHS guidance, progestogen-only methods, which include the progestogen-only pill (mini pill), the contraceptive implant, the injection, and the hormonal coil, can be safely started at any time after having your baby, and they are safe if you are breastfeeding: they have no adverse effects on lactation, infant growth, or development. The implant can even be fitted in hospital before you go home. Contraceptive coils (IUD or IUS) can be fitted within 48 hours of giving birth; if not fitted in that window, it is recommended to wait until 4 weeks after the birth. Combined methods that contain oestrogen, such as the combined pill, patch, or vaginal ring, need more caution: if you are breastfeeding, discuss with your nurse or doctor whether and when these are suitable, typically from 6 weeks after birth.

Can breastfeeding be used as contraception? Only under strict conditions, and it is not foolproof. According to NHS guidance, breastfeeding can act as a method of contraception, known as the lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM), and it can be up to 98% effective at preventing pregnancy for up to 6 months after birth, but only if all of these conditions are met: you are fully breastfeeding, meaning your baby has breast milk and no other liquids or solids; you breastfeed regularly, with no more than 4 to 6 hours between feeds, including at night; your baby is under 6 months old; and your periods have not returned. If any condition stops being true, for example when solids are introduced, feeds space out, a dummy or supplements are used, or your period returns, effectiveness drops. Because these conditions are easy to break unintentionally, NHS advice is to use another form of contraception alongside breastfeeding.

When can we have sex again after the baby is born? There are no rules about when to start having sex again after giving birth; it is a personal choice that depends on how you feel and when both you and your partner are ready. According to NHS guidance, you will probably feel tired and sore in the weeks after birth, so there is no pressure to rush before you feel fully healed. Hormonal changes after birth, particularly while breastfeeding, can make the vulva and vagina drier, which can cause discomfort during sex; over-the-counter lubricants can make sex more comfortable and enjoyable. If penetrative sex hurts, stop, and remember there are many other forms of intimacy in the meantime. If you are still experiencing pain when you have your 6-week postnatal check, tell your GP, because persistent pain can be treated. And remember contraception is needed from 3 weeks after birth, even before periods return.

How long should I wait before getting pregnant again? Spacing pregnancies protects the health of both mother and baby. According to NHS guidance, getting pregnant again too soon after giving birth puts both mother and baby at risk of complications, and it is recommended to wait between 18 and 24 months, but less than 5 years, before attempting your next pregnancy. The time between one birth and the start of the next pregnancy is called the inter-pregnancy interval. A short interval gives the body less time to rebuild nutritional stores such as iron and folate, recover from birth, and, after a caesarean, allow the scar to heal fully, which is why reliable contraception in the post-birth period is so important even for families planning more children soon. Discuss your family plans with your midwife or GP, who can help you choose a contraceptive method that matches when you hope to conceive again.

How long can I store expressed breast milk? Expressed breast milk keeps well if stored correctly. According to the NHS, breast milk can be stored in the fridge for up to 8 days at 4C or lower; if you are not sure of your fridge's temperature, or it is higher than 4C, use the milk within 3 days. Store milk at the back of the fridge, not in the door, and in small quantities to avoid waste. Milk cooled in the fridge can be carried in a cool bag with ice packs for up to 24 hours. For longer storage, breast milk can be frozen; label and date it first. In the freezer it keeps for up to 6 months at -18C or lower, or for 2 weeks in the ice compartment of a fridge. If your baby is premature or sick, ask the hospital staff caring for your baby for advice, because stricter storage rules apply for vulnerable babies.

How do I safely warm and defrost breast milk? Handle stored breast milk carefully to keep it safe. According to the NHS, you can feed expressed milk straight from the fridge if your baby is happy to drink it cold, or you can warm it to body temperature by putting the bottle in a jug of warm water or holding it under warm running water. Never use a microwave to heat or defrost breast milk, because microwaving creates hot spots that can burn your baby's mouth. Frozen milk is best defrosted slowly in the fridge; if you need it straight away, defrost it in a jug of warm water or under warm running water. Once defrosted, give the milk a gentle shake if it has separated and use it straight away; do not re-freeze milk that has been defrosted. Once your baby has drunk from a bottle of breast milk, use it within the time your midwife advises and discard leftovers.

What should I do if expressing breast milk is difficult or uncomfortable? Expressing is a skill that often needs support to get right, and struggling with it is common rather than a personal failing. According to the NHS, if you are finding it difficult or uncomfortable to express your breast milk, ask your midwife, health visitor, or a breastfeeding supporter for help; they can check your technique, suggest adjustments to hand expressing or pump use, and tell you about other breastfeeding support available near you. You can also call the National Breastfeeding Helpline, which is available 24 hours a day, for advice whenever you need it. If you are expressing because your baby is premature or sick, the hospital staff caring for your baby can give specific guidance on expressing frequency, technique, and storage for your situation, and specialist organisations provide additional advice for parents of premature babies. Persistent pain during expressing always warrants a check, since technique fixes usually resolve it.

When will my periods return after giving birth? The timing varies widely and depends heavily on how you feed your baby. According to NHS guidance, the earliest your periods can return is around five to six weeks after the birth if you are not breastfeeding. Breastfeeding usually delays the return of your periods, because the hormone prolactin, which drives milk production, suppresses ovulation; if you breastfeed exclusively, you are unlikely to have periods while your baby is under 6 months old. The crucial point for family planning is that you can become pregnant before your period returns, because ovulation occurs about two weeks before you would get that first period. This means the absence of periods is not proof you cannot conceive, and contraception is needed from 3 weeks (21 days) after birth if you want to avoid pregnancy. When periods do return, the first few may be irregular or different from before.
source_name: NHS (Great Western Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Why is expressing at night important for milk supply? If you are establishing or maintaining milk supply by expressing, for example because your baby is in neonatal care, the timing of expressing sessions matters. According to NHS guidance, try to have gaps of no longer than 4 hours between expressing sessions during the day and no more than 6 hours at night, and it is important to express at least once during the night, between about 2am and 4am. This is because your body releases more prolactin, the hormone that makes your milk, during the night, so a night expressing session gives a stronger signal to build supply. This pattern also mirrors how babies naturally feed around the clock, keeping supply matched to a baby's real needs. Around 3 to 5 days after giving birth, the amount of milk you make starts to increase, and consistent frequent expressing through that window is what establishes a robust long-term supply.
source_name: NHS (Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is the safest sleeping position in late pregnancy? From 28 weeks of pregnancy onwards, the safest position to fall asleep in is on your side, either left or right. According to the NHS, research suggests that going to sleep on your back after 28 weeks of pregnancy can increase the risk of stillbirth, and it is thought this may be related to the flow of blood and oxygen to the baby when lying flat on the back. The advice applies to falling asleep, including naps: start every sleep on your side. Do not worry or feel guilty if you wake up on your back; the research looked at the position people fall asleep in, so simply turn onto your side before going back to sleep. Pillows behind your back or between your knees can help you settle and stay on your side more comfortably as your bump grows.

How can I reduce the risk of stillbirth? Not all stillbirths can be prevented, but there are things you can do to reduce the risk. According to the NHS, these include not smoking and avoiding alcohol and drugs during pregnancy, which can seriously affect your baby's development as well as increasing the risk of miscarriage and stillbirth; attending all your antenatal appointments so midwives can monitor your baby's growth and wellbeing; being a healthy weight before trying to get pregnant, since obesity increases stillbirth risk; protecting yourself against infections and avoiding certain foods; and going to sleep on your side, not your back, from 28 weeks. Two same-day rules matter especially: report any tummy pain or vaginal bleeding to your midwife on the same day, and be aware of your baby's movements, reporting any change or reduction straight away. Routine blood pressure and urine checks also catch conditions like pre-eclampsia early.

What is induction of labour and when is it offered? Induction of labour means starting labour artificially rather than waiting for it to begin on its own. According to the NHS, induction is usually planned in advance, giving you the chance to discuss the advantages and disadvantages with your doctor and midwife and understand why they recommend it. A common reason is a pregnancy continuing past the due date: induction will be offered if you do not go into labour naturally by 42 weeks, because beyond that point there is a higher risk of stillbirth or problems for the baby. Induction may also be recommended earlier for medical reasons such as pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, or concerns about the baby's growth. You can decline an induction; if you do, discuss it with a health professional so a personalised care plan can be made, and if you pass 42 weeks you should be offered increased monitoring of your baby's wellbeing.

What are the downsides of induced labour? Induction is often the right choice, but it has trade-offs worth understanding before you decide. According to NHS guidance, an induced labour can take longer to get going, sometimes up to two days from the start of the process, and your baby will need to be monitored more closely during it. Induced labours can also be more intense than spontaneous ones, and with an induction you are more likely to need an assisted delivery with forceps or ventouse, or a caesarean section. Induction is not always successful, and labour may not start; if that happens, your obstetrician and midwife will assess you and your baby, and you may be offered another method of induction, a caesarean section, or reassessment after a few hours. One myth worth clearing up: there are no proven ways of starting labour yourself at home, whether hot baths, particular foods, sex, or herbal supplements.

What is an assisted birth (forceps or ventouse delivery)? An assisted birth, also known as an instrumental delivery, is when forceps or a ventouse suction cup are used to help deliver the baby during a vaginal birth. According to the NHS, an assisted delivery is used in about 1 in 8 births, and it may be needed if there are concerns about the baby's wellbeing and they need to be born quickly, if the baby is in an awkward position or labour is not progressing, if you have been advised not to push because of an underlying health condition such as very high blood pressure, or during the vaginal birth of a premature baby, where forceps can help protect the baby's head. Assisted delivery is more likely if you have an epidural and less common in women who have had a spontaneous vaginal birth before. Ventouse and forceps are safe and only used when necessary, and your consent is needed before the procedure.

What is the difference between ventouse and forceps? A ventouse is a suction device with a cup that attaches to the baby's head; the doctor or midwife waits for a contraction and gently pulls while you push to guide the baby out, often over several contractions. Forceps are smooth, curved metal instruments that fit around the baby's head and are used the same way, pulling gently in time with your pushing. According to NHS guidance, both are usually safe and effective, and the choice between them depends on factors including how well your epidural is working, the wellbeing of the baby, the gestational age, and the position of the baby's head. The ventouse can leave a temporary swelling or a bruise (cephalohaematoma) on the baby's head that resolves over days to weeks; forceps may leave small marks or bruising that usually fade within a day or two. If one instrument is not effective, the team may recommend the other or a caesarean.
source_name: NHS (Northern Lincolnshire and Goole NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What are CVS and amniocentesis? Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) and amniocentesis are diagnostic tests that can tell if your baby definitely has certain serious conditions by checking their chromosomes and genetic information. According to NHS screening programme information, both are invasive tests, meaning they involve taking a sample from within the mother's body: CVS takes a tiny sample of tissue from the placenta, which contains some of the baby's cells, while amniocentesis takes a sample of the fluid around the baby. These tests are not offered routinely; they are usually offered when a screening test result shows a higher chance of a condition such as Down's syndrome, Edwards' syndrome, or Patau's syndrome. Whether to have one is entirely your decision, and it helps to talk through possible results and outcomes with your doctor or midwife first. If you decline, you still receive all other parts of routine antenatal care.

When is CVS done and what happens during the test? Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) is usually done from weeks 11 to 14 of pregnancy, although it can be done later. According to NHS screening programme information, the test takes a tiny sample of placental tissue, which contains some of the baby's cells with their genetic information (DNA). The sample is most often taken with a needle through the skin of the abdomen under continuous ultrasound guidance, after the skin is cleaned and numbed with local anaesthetic; this transabdominal method is more common because it is often easier to carry out. If the doctor decides it is not safe to do the procedure on the day, for example because of the position of the placenta, you may be offered another appointment 7 to 14 days later when the placenta is larger and easier to reach, or occasionally an amniocentesis after 15 weeks instead. Results timing will be explained at the appointment.

When is amniocentesis done and what are the risks? Amniocentesis is a diagnostic test performed from around week 15 of pregnancy onwards. According to the NHS, the test is only done after the 15th week because the risk of complications is higher if it is carried out earlier. A fine needle is passed through the abdomen under ultrasound guidance to take a small sample of the amniotic fluid surrounding the baby; the sampling itself usually takes only a minute or so. The main risk to understand is miscarriage: amniocentesis carries a small additional risk of miscarriage, up to about 1 in 200 procedures on current NHS figures. The alternative diagnostic test, chorionic villus sampling (CVS), carries a similar miscarriage risk but can be done earlier, between weeks 11 and 14, giving you more time to consider the results. Your doctor or midwife will help you weigh the certainty these tests provide against the small procedural risk.

What happens after an assisted (forceps or ventouse) birth? After an assisted birth, some extra checks and care are routine. According to the NHS, a children's doctor (paediatrician) is usually present to check your baby's condition straight after the birth, which is a standard precaution rather than a sign something is wrong. You may be given antibiotics through a drip after the birth to reduce your risk of infection. If you had a tear or a cut (episiotomy) during the delivery, it will be repaired with dissolvable stitches, and you will usually have had a local anaesthetic to numb the area if you did not already have an epidural. Babies born by forceps or ventouse are usually well at birth and are not usually affected in the long term; temporary marks, bruising, or swelling on the baby's head generally fade within days. Most people recover well, and pelvic floor exercises support recovery afterwards.

What is NIPT (non-invasive prenatal testing)? NIPT is a blood test that looks at small fragments of your baby's DNA circulating in your bloodstream, used to assess the chance of your baby having Down's syndrome (trisomy 21), Edwards' syndrome (trisomy 18), or Patau's syndrome (trisomy 13). According to NHS guidance, NIPT is more accurate than the standard first screening tests, but it does not give a definite diagnosis: it is an advanced screening test, not a diagnostic one. Because it is a simple blood test taken from the mother's arm, NIPT is non-invasive and carries no miscarriage risk, unlike the diagnostic tests CVS and amniocentesis. It is available from 10 weeks of pregnancy. On the NHS, NIPT is offered as part of routine care to women who have received a higher-chance result from initial screening, and a specialist midwife will explain the test and provide your results.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals of Leicester NHS Trust)

text: Can NIPT tell me for certain whether my baby has Down's syndrome? No. According to NHS guidance, no screening test is 100% accurate, and NIPT cannot tell for definite whether your baby has Down's syndrome, Edwards' syndrome, or Patau's syndrome; it gives a more accurate estimate of chance than the first screening test, but a high-chance NIPT result still needs to be confirmed with a diagnostic test, either CVS or amniocentesis, before any certain conclusion can be drawn. This is because, rarely, the DNA fragments analysed come from the placenta and may not represent the baby itself, which can lead to false positive results. NHS guidance also notes that if you have received a very high chance result from initial screening for Edwards' or Patau's syndrome, NIPT is less accurate, which may affect which next step is right for you. Your midwife or screening team will talk you through the options after any higher-chance result.

What is an episiotomy and when is it done? An episiotomy is a cut made by a doctor or midwife in the perineum, the area between the vagina and anus, during childbirth. According to the NHS, an episiotomy makes the opening of the vagina a bit wider, allowing the baby to come through more easily, and in some births it can help prevent a severe tear or speed up delivery when the baby needs to be born quickly. Episiotomies are not done routinely in England; if your doctor or midwife feels you need one during labour, they will discuss it with you and should always gain your consent first. If you have an episiotomy or a tear, it will usually be repaired with stitches under local anaesthetic in the room where you gave birth, and dissolvable stitches are used, so they do not need to be removed later.

What are the different degrees of perineal tears? Perineal tears during vaginal birth are graded by depth. According to NHS guidance, first degree tears are shallow and often need no stitches, while second degree tears are deeper, affect the muscles of the perineum, and usually need stitches; most perineal injuries fall into these two categories and heal well. Third degree tears extend into the muscle that controls the anus (the anal sphincter), and if the tear extends further into the lining of the anus or rectum it is a fourth degree tear; these deeper injuries are less common and are repaired in an operating theatre with careful follow-up. They usually happen unexpectedly and cannot be reliably predicted, but they are more likely in a first vaginal birth (about 6 in 100) than a second (about 2 in 100). After a third or fourth degree tear, any future pregnancy will include a consultant review to plan the next birth.

Can I reduce my chance of tearing or needing an episiotomy during birth? Yes, two evidence-supported approaches help. According to the NHS, massaging the perineum in the last few weeks of pregnancy can reduce the chances of having an episiotomy during birth; most approaches involve inserting one or two fingers into the vagina and applying downward or sweeping pressure towards the perineum, with the most benefit seen in women who repeated the massage every day. During the birth itself, your midwife can help you avoid a tear at the moment the baby's head becomes visible: they will ask you to stop pushing and to pant or puff a couple of quick short breaths, blowing out through your mouth, so the baby's head emerges slowly and gently, giving the skin and muscles of the perineum time to stretch. Pelvic floor exercises during pregnancy also support the area and help with healing afterwards.

How long does an episiotomy or perineal tear take to heal? Stitches to repair a tear or episiotomy are normally done within an hour after birth, under local anaesthetic, using dissolvable stitches that do not need removing. According to NHS guidance, perineal grazes and smaller tears are usually healing well after a couple of weeks, and it can take up to a month for an episiotomy to fully heal and for the stitches to dissolve. During healing, keep the area clean, change pads regularly, and do pelvic floor exercises: strengthening the muscles around the vagina and anus helps with healing and reduces pressure on the wound. Call your GP or midwife if you notice signs of infection, such as increasing pain, smelly discharge, or the wound opening, as these need prompt treatment. If excessive, raised, or itchy scar tissue forms later, or the scar causes ongoing pain or discomfort during sex, tell your doctor, because treatment is available.

Can I have a vaginal birth after a third or fourth degree tear? Often, yes. According to NHS guidance, in general, if you have good control over your bowel movements and no evidence of significant remaining damage to the anal sphincter, a subsequent vaginal delivery can be attempted after a third or fourth degree tear. If you continue to experience symptoms from the tear, such as difficulty controlling wind or bowel movements, you will be counselled about your options and offered a planned caesarean section, although a caesarean carries its own risks which will be explained. One common misconception is worth correcting: there is no evidence that a routine preventive episiotomy stops an anal sphincter injury happening again, so an episiotomy in the next birth is only recommended if specific risk factors are present, such as a very large baby or an awkward position. In any future pregnancy you will be referred to a consultant to make an individual birth plan.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Coventry and Warwickshire NHS Trust)

text: How common is tearing during a first vaginal birth? Very common, and mostly minor. According to NHS guidance, up to nine in ten first-time mothers who have a vaginal birth will have some sort of tear, graze, or episiotomy. This high number sounds alarming, but the large majority of these are first or second degree injuries, grazes and shallower tears that heal well within a couple of weeks, often with simple stitches or none at all. The most serious injuries, third and fourth degree tears involving the anal sphincter, are much less common. A tear happens spontaneously as the baby stretches the vagina during birth, and an episiotomy is a deliberate cut made only with your consent, sometimes to prevent a worse tear or to speed up delivery. Knowing that some perineal injury is a normal, expected, and treatable part of most first births helps many people approach labour with less fear.
source_name: NHS (Kent and Medway Local Maternity System)

text: Why should I avoid too much vitamin A during pregnancy? Vitamin A is needed only in small amounts, and you should be able to get enough from a normal diet. According to NHS guidance, vitamin A is stored in your body, which means it is possible to build up too much, and high levels may be harmful to your baby: in high doses vitamin A can harm the development of an unborn baby's nervous system. For this reason, during pregnancy you should avoid any supplements that contain more than 700 micrograms of vitamin A, and avoid eating liver and liver products such as pâté, as well as fish liver oils, because these can contain very high levels of the vitamin. This is also why standard multivitamins are not suitable in pregnancy and pregnancy-specific supplements should be used instead, since these are formulated without high-dose vitamin A. Check labels or ask a pharmacist if unsure.

Do I need iodine during pregnancy and where does it come from? Iodine is a mineral needed to make thyroid hormones, which are important for your baby's brain development during pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, if you eat foods including pasteurised milk, pasteurised dairy products, and fish, you will probably get enough iodine from your diet without needing a supplement. However, if you do not eat these foods, for example because you follow a vegan diet or avoid dairy and fish for other reasons, you are more likely to be short of iodine, and you should talk to your midwife or GP for advice about suitable supplements. It is important not to self-prescribe high-dose iodine, particularly kelp or seaweed supplements, which can contain excessive and variable amounts; a healthcare professional can recommend an appropriate dose. Iodine needs are one of the reasons a varied, balanced pregnancy diet matters.

Do I need vitamin C supplements during pregnancy? Routine vitamin C supplements are not specifically recommended in pregnancy, because a normal varied diet provides enough. According to NHS guidance, vitamin C's most practical role in pregnancy nutrition is that it helps iron to be absorbed from food and from iron tablets, which matters because iron needs increase during pregnancy. Good dietary sources of vitamin C include citrus fruits, orange juice, berries, tomatoes, peppers, broccoli, and potatoes, so pairing these with iron-rich foods such as meat, lentils, fortified cereals, or leafy greens improves iron uptake at the same meal. In some health systems, pregnant women are entitled to free vitamins that include folic acid, vitamin C, and vitamin D, without needing to qualify; ask your midwife about local schemes such as Healthy Start. If you take an iron supplement, taking it with a vitamin C-containing drink rather than tea or coffee makes it work better.

Can I follow a vegetarian or vegan diet during pregnancy? Yes. According to NHS guidance, a varied and balanced vegetarian diet should provide enough nutrients for you and your baby during pregnancy, but you might find it more difficult to get enough iron and vitamin B12, which are most abundant in meat and animal products. Talk to a midwife or doctor about how to make sure you are getting enough of these important nutrients; good vegetarian iron sources include lentils, beans, fortified breakfast cereals, and leafy green vegetables, while B12 comes from eggs, dairy, and fortified foods. If you are vegan, or you follow a restricted diet because of a food intolerance, such as a gluten-free diet for coeliac disease, or for religious reasons, tell your midwife or GP and ask to be referred to a dietitian for tailored advice on getting all the nutrients you and your baby need, including B12, iodine, and vitamin D.
source_name: NHS (Surrey and Sussex Healthcare NHS Trust)

text: Should every pregnant woman take iron supplements? No. According to NHS guidance, most women do not need to take extra iron during pregnancy, and taking routine iron supplements when you are not deficient will not necessarily benefit your health, while potentially causing unpleasant side effects such as heartburn, constipation, or diarrhoea. Instead of routine supplementation, your iron status is monitored: your midwife checks your blood at your booking appointment and again at 28 weeks of pregnancy, and you will be advised to take iron only if you are found to be anaemic or are at increased risk of becoming anaemic, for example if you are carrying twins. In the meantime, a diet including iron-rich foods such as lean red meat, pulses, fortified cereals, and green leafy vegetables, paired with vitamin C to aid absorption, maintains iron levels for most people. If you do develop symptoms like unusual tiredness or breathlessness, mention them to your midwife.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Coventry and Warwickshire NHS Trust)

text: What does a healthy pregnancy diet look like? A healthy pregnancy diet follows the same principles as healthy eating generally, with a few pregnancy-specific additions and cautions. According to NHS guidance, base your meals on starchy foods such as potatoes, bread, rice, and pasta, choosing wholegrain versions where possible for extra fibre; eat plenty of fruit and vegetables; include protein sources such as lean meat, fish, eggs, beans, and pulses; and include dairy or fortified alternatives for calcium. Eating a healthy, varied diet will help you get most of the vitamins and minerals you need, and it is not necessary to eat for two. Beyond diet, the recommended pregnancy supplements are folic acid (400 micrograms daily until at least week 12) and vitamin D (10 micrograms daily); expensive supplement brands are no better than cheaper ones, and pregnancy multivitamins are available from any pharmacy or supermarket. Speak to a GP if you need higher-dose folic acid.

Who needs the higher 5mg dose of folic acid in pregnancy? The standard folic acid dose for pregnancy is 400 micrograms daily, started ideally before conception and continued until at least the end of the 12th week of pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, some people need a higher prescription-only dose of 5 milligrams (5mg) daily instead, which a GP can prescribe. You may need the higher dose if you or the baby's father has a neural tube defect such as spina bifida, if a previous pregnancy was affected by a neural tube defect, or if you have certain conditions; NHS guidance in some regions also advises the higher dose for women with a raised body mass index, with diabetes, or taking certain epilepsy medicines. The higher dose exists because these situations carry an increased chance of neural tube defects, and more folic acid provides stronger protection. If any of these apply to you, speak to your GP before or as soon as you become pregnant.
source_name: NHS (Royal Devon University Healthcare NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Can I get free pregnancy vitamins? In many parts of the UK, yes. According to NHS guidance, you may be able to get free vitamin supplements through the Healthy Start scheme, which supports pregnant women and young children with vitamins and help towards the cost of healthy food; eligibility rules apply in England and Wales and are based on benefits or age. In Scotland, NHS guidance states that all pregnant women are entitled to free vitamins containing folic acid, vitamin C, and vitamin D, without having to qualify; you simply ask your midwife. These free vitamins cover the supplements actually recommended in pregnancy, which is worth knowing because expensive branded pregnancy multivitamins are not better than cheaper options or the free ones; the active ingredients that matter are the folic acid and vitamin D doses. Ask your midwife at your booking appointment what vitamin support is available in your area and how to access it.

What are Braxton Hicks contractions? Braxton Hicks contractions are a sign that the muscles of your womb are tightening in preparation for birth; they are often called practice contractions. According to NHS guidance, they may be uncomfortable, but they are not painful, and women often describe them as feeling like mild or strong menstrual cramps, or a tightening in a specific area of the stomach that comes and goes, with the bump feeling hard for a short time. Braxton Hicks are usually irregular, short, and fade away, unlike labour contractions, which become progressively longer, stronger, and closer together, and which cause the cervix to open. They tend to occur more often later in pregnancy, and some people notice them from mid-pregnancy while others never feel them at all; both are normal. If tightenings become regular, painful, or are accompanied by bleeding or fluid loss, contact your midwife or maternity unit for assessment.

How can I relieve leg cramps during pregnancy? Leg cramps, sudden painful tightening of the muscles usually in the calf or foot, are a common pregnancy symptom, particularly at night and in later pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, when a cramp strikes, it may help to pull your toes hard up towards your ankle, stretching the calf muscle, or to rub the muscle firmly until the cramp releases. Regular gentle exercise during the day, ankle-circling and calf-stretching movements before bed, and staying well hydrated can reduce how often cramps occur. Cramps themselves are harmless, but it is important to distinguish them from a more serious problem: pain, swelling, and tenderness in one leg, usually the calf, that persists rather than releasing, especially if the pain worsens when you bend your foot up towards your knee, can be a sign of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and needs urgent medical assessment rather than stretching.

How do I tell normal pregnancy leg discomfort from a blood clot (DVT)? Swelling or discomfort in the legs is common during pregnancy, so leg symptoms on their own do not always mean something serious. According to NHS guidance, deep vein thrombosis (DVT), a blood clot in a deep vein usually in the leg, is not common in pregnancy, but pregnant women at any stage of pregnancy, and up to 6 weeks after birth, are more likely to develop DVT than non-pregnant women of the same age. Contact your maternity assessment unit urgently if you have pain, swelling, and tenderness in one leg, usually at the back of the lower leg (calf), particularly if the pain is worse when you bend your foot up towards your knee, or if the leg is red or warm. The key distinguishing features are that DVT symptoms affect one leg, persist, and worsen, unlike the symmetrical mild puffiness of normal pregnancy swelling.

What symptoms are common at 33 weeks pregnant? At 33 weeks, in the third trimester, your womb may start preparing for birth with painless practice contractions around your bump, known as Braxton Hicks contractions. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, other common signs of pregnancy at this stage include sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen and bleeding gums, round ligament pains at the sides of the bump, piles, headaches, backache, indigestion and heartburn, bloating and constipation, leg cramps, feeling hot, dizziness, swollen hands and feet, urine infections, vaginal infections, darkened patches of skin on the face (chloasma), and greasier, spotty skin. Most of these are normal effects of late pregnancy, but stay alert to the late-pregnancy warning signs: severe headache, vision changes, sudden swelling, reduced baby movements, bleeding, regular painful contractions before 37 weeks, or waters breaking all need prompt contact with your midwife or maternity unit.

What should I know at 35 weeks pregnant? At 35 weeks you are in the final stretch of pregnancy, and your body is actively preparing for labour. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, common signs of pregnancy at this stage include painless Braxton Hicks contractions around your bump, sleeping problems, stretch marks, swollen and bleeding gums, round ligament pains, piles, headaches, backache, indigestion and heartburn, bloating and constipation, leg cramps, feeling hot, dizziness, swollen hands and feet, urine infections, vaginal infections, and skin changes such as chloasma. This is a good time to finalise practical preparations: pack your hospital bag, install the car seat, confirm your birth plan and who to call when labour starts, and keep monitoring your baby's movements, contacting your midwife immediately about any change. Antenatal appointments now include checks of the baby's position as well as your blood pressure and urine.

What happens at 40 weeks pregnant? At 40 weeks you have reached your estimated due date, though it is completely normal for babies to arrive before or after it; only a small proportion of babies are born on the due date itself. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, common signs of pregnancy at this point include painless Braxton Hicks contractions, sleeping problems, stretch marks, round ligament pains, piles, headaches, backache, indigestion and heartburn, constipation, leg cramps, feeling hot, dizziness, swollen hands and feet, urine infections, vaginal infections, and skin changes such as chloasma. Watch for the true signs of labour: regular contractions that grow longer, stronger, and closer together, a show, or your waters breaking. Keep monitoring your baby's movements, which should continue right up to and during labour, and remember that if you have not given birth by 42 weeks, you will be offered an induction because of the increased risks beyond that point.

When is stomach pain in pregnancy serious? Stomach pains or cramps in pregnancy are common and usually not serious; harmless causes include ligament stretching, constipation, trapped wind, and, in later pregnancy, Braxton Hicks contractions. According to NHS guidance, if cramps go away after resting, going to the toilet, or passing wind, they are unlikely to be a concern, but if the pain does not go away after these steps, call your GP or midwife, because persistent pain can be a sign of something more serious. Seek urgent same-day help from your maternity unit if stomach pain comes with bleeding or spotting, regular cramping or tightenings, unusual vaginal discharge, lower back pain, pain or burning when urinating, or if the pain is severe or continues after resting. Serious causes of abdominal pain in pregnancy can include miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, urinary tract infection, pre-eclampsia, placental abruption, and premature labour, all of which need prompt assessment. Trust your instincts and call if something feels wrong.

Is it normal for my newborn to lose weight after birth? Yes. According to the NHS, it is normal for babies to lose some weight in the first few days after birth, mostly fluid, and steady weight regain after that is one of the signs that a baby is healthy and feeding well. Your baby will be weighed during their first 2 weeks to check they are regaining their birthweight, and most babies are back at, or above, their birthweight by 3 weeks of age. If your baby loses a large amount of weight or has not regained their birthweight by 3 weeks, a midwife or health visitor will support you: they will talk through how feeding is going, may ask to observe a feed if you are breastfeeding, and will look at your baby's general health. In most cases, extra weight loss reflects feeding that needs adjusting rather than illness, and improves quickly with support.

How often should my baby be weighed? After the initial checks, babies do not need frequent weighing. According to the NHS, your baby is weighed during their first 2 weeks to confirm they are regaining their birthweight; after that, routine weighing is spaced out, and your baby will usually only be weighed more often if you ask for it or if there are concerns about their health or growth. Weights are recorded on growth charts in your baby's health record, where the curved centile lines show the average weight gain for babies of different ages; your baby's weight may not follow a centile line exactly, and that is normal, since babies grow in their own patterns. What matters is the overall trend rather than any single measurement. Weighing is done naked on properly maintained baby scales at clinics, so home bathroom-scale estimates are only rough. Ask your health visitor if you have any concerns about growth between reviews.

How often should I bathe my newborn? Less often than many parents expect. According to NHS guidance, in the first few weeks there is no need to bath your baby more than once or twice a week, especially in the winter months; on other days, 'topping and tailing', washing the face, neck, hands, and bottom with cotton wool and warm water, is perfectly acceptable and keeps your baby warmer. Newborn skin is delicate, and frequent bathing can dry it out, so plain water is all that is needed at first. NHS neonatal guidance also recommends that the first proper immersion bath takes place at least 24 hours after birth, once the baby is stable and holding their own temperature, and at a time convenient for the parents; delaying that first bath protects the baby's temperature and skin. As your baby grows, you can move to more frequent baths if your family prefers, and bathtime often becomes an enjoyable routine.
source_name: NHS (Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)

text: How do I bathe my baby safely? Preparation is the key to a safe, calm bath. According to NHS guidance, before you start, make sure the room is warm, at approximately 22C, and free from draughts, because babies lose heat quickly when wet. Check the water is at the right temperature, around 30C, testing it with your elbow, which is more sensitive to heat than your hand; the water should feel comfortably warm, never hot. Have all the essentials within arm's reach before undressing your baby: towel, clean nappy, and clothes, warmed if necessary, so you never need to step away. Support your baby's head and shoulders above the water with one arm while washing with the other, and keep baths short at first. The most important safety rule: never leave a baby alone in the bath, even for a few seconds, and even in very shallow water.
source_name: NHS (Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What happens if my newborn loses too much weight? If a newborn's weight loss exceeds the expected range, a structured review is carried out rather than an immediate assumption of illness. According to NHS guidance, in most cases significant newborn weight loss is the result of poor milk transfer, meaning the baby is not yet taking enough milk during feeds, rather than an underlying disease. The care team will perform a clinical assessment looking for signs of dehydration or illness, take a detailed feeding history, and usually directly observe a feed with someone trained in breastfeeding or bottle-feeding assessment. The usual plan is feeding support plus supplementation for a few days, ideally with the mother's own expressed breast milk, which rehydrates the baby and restores weight while protecting breastfeeding; skin-to-skin contact is encouraged because it promotes milk-making hormones. Finding out your baby has lost a lot of weight can feel overwhelming, but with support most babies recover their weight within days.

What is topping and tailing a baby? Topping and tailing is a simple way of keeping your newborn clean on days when you do not give a full bath. According to NHS guidance, in the first few weeks babies only need bathing once or twice a week, and topping and tailing on other days is completely acceptable, keeping the baby warmer than a full bath, which matters especially in colder months. To top and tail, use cotton wool and plain warm water: wash your baby's face and neck first, using a fresh piece of cotton wool for each eye and wiping from the inner corner outwards, then clean the hands, and finally the nappy area, always working from clean areas to less clean ones and using fresh cotton wool as you go. Dry each area gently as you finish, paying attention to skin folds. Choose a warm, draught-free spot and have everything ready before you undress your baby.
source_name: NHS (Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Which fish should I avoid or limit during pregnancy because of mercury? Fish is a healthy part of a pregnancy diet, but a few types need avoiding or limiting because of mercury, which can harm a baby's developing nervous system if consumed in excess. According to the NHS, avoid shark, swordfish, and marlin completely, because these large predatory fish contain the highest mercury levels. Limit tuna, which contains more mercury than most other fish: eat no more than 2 tuna steaks (about 140g cooked or 170g raw) or 4 medium-sized cans (about 140g drained each) per week. Separately, limit oily fish, including salmon, trout, mackerel, sardines, and herring, to no more than 2 portions a week, because oily fish can contain pollutants; note that tuna does not count as an oily fish for this limit. White fish such as cod, haddock, and plaice can be eaten freely, and fish remains a valuable source of protein and omega-3 in pregnancy.

Can I eat sushi, smoked salmon, and shellfish while pregnant? It depends on how they are prepared. According to the NHS, ready-to-eat cold-smoked and cured fish, such as smoked salmon or gravlax, including in sushi, is best avoided unless it has been cooked until steaming hot, because it can be contaminated with listeria bacteria, which can cause listeriosis, an infection that can lead to miscarriage or stillbirth or make a newborn seriously ill. Sushi is fine if the fish has been thoroughly cooked. Cooked shellfish is safe to eat, but raw shellfish, such as oysters, should be avoided because of the risk of food poisoning; cold pre-cooked ready-to-eat prawns should be cooked until steaming hot before eating. Pre-packaged sandwiches and salads containing fish or shellfish carry a low listeria risk too, so keep them chilled and eat them before the use-by date. Thoroughly cooked fish of any kind is safe.

Can I eat runny or raw eggs during pregnancy? It depends on where the eggs come from. According to the NHS, British Lion hen eggs, and hen eggs produced under the Laid in Britain scheme, are less likely to contain salmonella, so raw or runny eggs from these schemes, including soft-boiled eggs, can be eaten in pregnancy; look for the lion stamp on the shell or box. Salmonella is unlikely to harm your unborn baby directly, but it can give you food poisoning, which can make you seriously unwell, which is why egg safety matters in pregnancy. Eggs that are not from these assurance schemes, including eggs from other birds such as duck or quail eggs, should be cooked thoroughly until both the white and yolk are solid. Also watch out for foods made with raw egg, such as homemade mayonnaise or mousse, especially in restaurants; shop-bought mayonnaise is pasteurised and fine. Well-cooked eggs are always safe and are a good protein source.

Which cheeses are safe during pregnancy? Most cheese is fine; the rules depend on type and pasteurisation. According to the NHS, safe cheeses include all hard cheeses, such as cheddar, gruyère, and parmesan, whether pasteurised or unpasteurised, and pasteurised soft cheeses, such as cottage cheese, cream cheese, processed cheese spreads, mozzarella, feta, paneer, ricotta, mascarpone, halloumi, burrata, and goats' cheese without a white rind. Cheeses to avoid unless cooked until steaming hot are mould-ripened soft cheeses with a white coating on the outside, such as brie, camembert, and chèvre, and soft blue cheeses, such as Danish blue, gorgonzola, and Roquefort, whether pasteurised or not, plus anything made from unpasteurised milk; these soft, less acidic cheeses can harbour listeria bacteria. The good news: any cheese that has been cooked until steaming hot, including soft and blue cheeses, is safe, so baked brie or a fully cooked blue cheese sauce is fine.

Why can't I eat liver or pâté during pregnancy? Liver and liver products are on the avoid list for two separate reasons. According to NHS guidance, liver and liver products such as liver pâté and liver sausage contain large amounts of vitamin A, and high doses of vitamin A can be harmful to an unborn baby, potentially affecting the development of the nervous system; because vitamin A is stored in the body, regular liver consumption can build up harmful levels. For the same reason, avoid fish liver oil supplements and any supplement containing vitamin A in pregnancy. Separately, all types of pâté, including vegetable pâtés, should be avoided regardless of vitamin A content, because pâté can contain listeria bacteria, which can cause an infection dangerous to the pregnancy. It is rare for women in developed countries to be deficient in vitamin A, so cutting out liver does not create any nutritional gap; a normal varied diet provides all the vitamin A needed.
source_name: NHS (Sandwell and West Birmingham NHS Trust)

text: I ate something on the avoid list before I knew I was pregnant. Should I panic? No. Many women eat or drink things on the avoid list before they realise they are pregnant, or before they learn what to avoid, and the individual risks from a single exposure are low. The food avoidance rules in pregnancy exist to minimise small risks across the whole population, not because one portion of soft cheese, pâté, undercooked meat, or an alcoholic drink usually causes harm. The practical NHS-aligned advice is: stop consuming the item now that you know, and try not to worry. If you feel unwell after eating a risky food, particularly with fever, vomiting, diarrhoea, or flu-like symptoms, contact your midwife or GP, because infections such as listeriosis and salmonella are treatable and prompt treatment protects the pregnancy. If you are anxious about a specific exposure, mention it at your next appointment; your midwife can assess whether any follow-up is needed.
source_name: NHS (Hull University Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust)

text: Which fish can I eat freely during pregnancy? Plenty. According to NHS guidance, white fish such as cod, plaice, and haddock can be eaten without restriction during pregnancy, and cooked shellfish, including well-cooked prawns, mussels, and crab, is also safe. Fish is an excellent pregnancy food: it provides high-quality protein, and oily fish provides omega-3 fatty acids that support the baby's brain and eye development, which is why the advice is to include fish in your diet rather than avoid it, within the limits. The limits to remember are: no shark, swordfish, or marlin at all; tuna capped at 2 steaks or 4 medium cans a week; oily fish (salmon, trout, mackerel, herring, sardines) capped at 2 portions a week; and no raw shellfish or uncooked cold-smoked fish. Within those rules, aiming for a couple of fish portions a week, including one oily, is a healthy pregnancy pattern.
source_name: NHS (Hull University Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust)

text: What does it mean to be rhesus (RhD) negative in pregnancy? Everyone belongs to one of four blood groups (A, B, AB, or O) and is also either rhesus (RhD) positive or negative. According to NHS guidance, in the UK around 85% of people are RhD positive and 15% RhD negative; people who are RhD negative simply do not have the rhesus D antigen, a molecule on the surface of red blood cells, and this is inherited from your parents. Your baby may not have the same blood group as you, which is completely normal: an RhD negative mother can carry an RhD positive baby if the baby inherits the antigen from the father. Being RhD negative causes no problems for your own health, but it matters in pregnancy because if the baby's RhD positive blood mixes with yours, your immune system can react, which is why RhD negative women are offered anti-D injections during pregnancy.
source_name: NHS (Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is rhesus sensitisation and why does it matter for future pregnancies? Sensitisation happens when a woman with RhD negative blood is exposed to RhD positive blood, usually small amounts of her baby's blood crossing into her circulation, and her immune system responds by producing antibodies against it. According to NHS guidance, this is not usually dangerous in a first pregnancy, because the antibodies form too late to cause harm; the risk is to later pregnancies, where the ready-made antibodies can cross the placenta and attack the red blood cells of an RhD positive baby. This can cause haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN), which ranges from mild to serious anaemia and jaundice in the baby. The crucial reassurance is that sensitisation is now largely preventable: anti-D immunoglobulin injections, standard practice in the UK for decades, stop the antibodies from being made in the first place, protecting all your future pregnancies.
source_name: NHS (East Kent Hospitals University NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is the anti-D injection and when is it given? Anti-D immunoglobulin is a medication given by injection to RhD negative pregnant women to prevent rhesus disease. According to the NHS, anti-D works by neutralising any RhD positive antigens from the baby that may have entered the mother's blood; if the antigens are neutralised, the mother's immune system does not produce antibodies, avoiding sensitisation. Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis (RAADP) is recommended for all pregnant RhD negative women who have not already been sensitised, most commonly as a single dose between weeks 28 and 30 of pregnancy; some services use a two-dose schedule, and the two approaches are equally effective. Because the protection is not lifelong, anti-D is offered again in every pregnancy. You will also be offered extra anti-D after any event where the baby's blood may have entered yours, such as vaginal bleeding, an invasive procedure like amniocentesis, or an abdominal injury. Anti-D does not affect the baby.

Can doctors find out my baby's blood group before birth? Yes, with a simple blood test from the mother. According to NHS guidance, fetal RhD screening is a test offered to RhD negative pregnant women that predicts the unborn baby's blood group from a maternal blood sample taken from around 11 weeks of pregnancy onwards; at this stage, some of the baby's DNA is detectable in the mother's bloodstream, making the prediction possible. This matters because anti-D injections are only needed if an RhD negative mother is carrying an RhD positive baby, and in about one in three such pregnancies the baby is actually RhD negative, meaning anti-D would be unnecessary. Screening the baby's blood group therefore reduces the number of unnecessary anti-D injections given. An important privacy point: this is the only information about your baby's DNA screened for in this test. Ask your midwife whether fetal RhD screening is available at your hospital.
source_name: NHS (Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN)? HDFN is a condition in which antibodies in the mother's blood cross the placenta and attack the baby's red blood cells, causing anaemia and jaundice in the baby. According to the NHS, it most classically occurs when a previously sensitised RhD negative mother carries an RhD positive baby, which is why it is sometimes called rhesus disease. If there is a risk of HDFN during pregnancy, you will usually be offered anti-D injections to stop antibodies developing in the first place, and pregnancies at risk are monitored closely so treatment can start early. If a baby develops anaemia later in pregnancy, treatments can include blood transfusions to the baby before birth, and delivery by caesarean section before the due date may be needed. The risk of serious complications is low because the treatments are usually very effective, though a very unwell baby may need care in a neonatal intensive care unit.

When might I need an extra anti-D injection during pregnancy? Beyond the routine dose at 28 to 30 weeks, RhD negative women are given additional anti-D after any 'sensitising event', an event that may have allowed the baby's blood to cross into the mother's circulation. According to NHS guidance, sensitising events include vaginal bleeding at any stage of pregnancy, abdominal injury such as a fall or a car accident (even a minor bump to the tummy), invasive procedures such as amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling, external attempts to turn a breech baby, miscarriage or threatened miscarriage, and ectopic pregnancy. The routine 28-week dose exists precisely because small, undetectable crossings of the baby's blood can happen with no obvious event at all. If you are RhD negative and experience any bleeding or abdominal knock during pregnancy, contact your midwife or maternity unit promptly, both to have the event checked and so anti-D can be given within the effective time window.
source_name: NHS (Harrogate and District NHS Foundation Trust)

text: How is my baby developing at 15 weeks pregnant? At 15 weeks, your baby is around 10.1 centimetres long from head to bottom, which is about the size of an apple, and is growing quickly, about to have another growth spurt. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, your baby has been busy growing a soft layer of fine hair called lanugo all over their body, and their eyebrows and eyelashes are starting to develop. Two lovely milestones happen around now: your baby's eyes become sensitive to light, and your baby starts to hear. Talk to your baby and they will probably hear you; they will also hear your heartbeat and the noises made by your digestive system, sounds that will be familiar and comforting after birth. You will probably have put on some weight over the past few weeks as your baby grows, which is expected and healthy at this stage.

What changes at 17 weeks pregnant? At 17 weeks, your waist will start to vanish as your womb moves up and out of your pelvis, making you look more obviously pregnant. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, this is also a period when emotional wellbeing deserves attention: around 1 in 5 women feel stressed or anxious during their pregnancy, and hormones can make even small problems feel overwhelming; if you feel sad or worried, talk to your doctor or midwife, because a big support network exists for you. You may be approaching the exciting milestone of first movements: many women spot the first signs of their baby moving between 18 and 24 weeks. Continue the healthy basics: a balanced diet without eating for two, 10 micrograms of vitamin D daily, and around 150 minutes of exercise a week, listening to your body throughout.

How common are stretch marks in pregnancy and what do they look like? Stretch marks are one of the most universal body changes of pregnancy, affecting up to 8 in 10 women. According to the NHS, depending on your skin colour you might notice pink, red, purple, or brown streaks appearing over your stomach and breasts, and they can also appear on your bottom and thighs. They are usually caused by pregnancy hormones combined with the physical stretching of your skin as your bump and body grow. The most reassuring fact about stretch marks is what happens after birth: they usually fade to a silvery colour and become much less noticeable once your baby is born, although they may not disappear entirely. Because they are driven largely by hormones and genetics, they are not a sign you did anything wrong, and no cream has been proven to prevent them; keeping skin moisturised mainly helps with comfort and itching.

Why is 24 weeks a significant milestone in pregnancy? At 24 weeks, your baby is now considered 'viable', which means they could survive if they were born right now and given the right support in a neonatal intensive care unit. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, this legal and medical milestone is reassuring for many parents, although a baby born this early would need many weeks of intensive care and the aim is always for pregnancy to continue as close to full term as possible. Around this time you may start to feel really hungry, but there is no need to eat any more until the third trimester begins at week 28; you are likely to be putting on weight, and you should not worry if you can barely see your bump, because every pregnancy carries differently. It is also a good time to check you have had the whooping cough vaccine and to start thinking about your birth plan.

What is a birth plan and when should I write one? A birth plan sets out what you would ideally like to happen during labour, the birth, and after your baby is born, covering preferences such as where you want to give birth, pain relief choices, birth positions, who your birth partner will be, skin-to-skin contact, and feeding intentions. According to the NHS, mid-pregnancy, around weeks 24 to 30, is a good time to start thinking about your birth plan; you can find guidance on making one and download a birth plan template, and it helps to talk it through with your partner, family, midwife, or doctor. A birth plan is a statement of preferences, not a contract: every birth is different, and plans sometimes need to change on the day for safety, so the most useful birth plans state priorities while staying flexible. Your midwife will discuss your plan with you at antenatal appointments and keep a copy with your notes.

What changes at 28 weeks pregnant? Week 28 marks the start of the third trimester, the final stage of pregnancy. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, from now you may need an extra 200 calories a day, which is not much, roughly two slices of wholemeal toast with margarine; before this point no extra calories were needed. This is also the deadline zone for the whooping cough vaccine, usually offered around week 20 but available from week 16 to week 32, so speak to your midwife or GP if you have not had it. Infection precautions matter in the third trimester: wash your hands regularly with soap and water for at least 20 seconds, particularly around children and nappies, which can carry cytomegalovirus (CMV); wear gloves when emptying a cat litter tray or gardening, or ask someone else to do it, because cat poo can carry the bug that causes toxoplasmosis.

What is the RSV vaccine in pregnancy and when is it given? The RSV (respiratory syncytial virus) vaccine is offered during pregnancy to protect your newborn baby from severe RSV illness. According to the NHS, the vaccine is usually offered at around week 28 of pregnancy, but it can be given later if needed, right up until you go into labour. Having the vaccination lowers your baby's risk of a severe lung infection called bronchiolitis, which is caused by RSV and can make it difficult for a young baby to breathe and feed, sometimes requiring hospital admission. Like the whooping cough vaccine, the protection you build passes to your baby before birth, covering them during their most vulnerable early months before they can be protected directly. If you have reached 28 weeks and have not been offered the RSV vaccine, ask your midwife or GP surgery about arranging it.

Why am I so breathless at 30 weeks pregnant? At 30 weeks, it is probably more of an effort to walk up the stairs without getting out of breath, and there is a simple mechanical reason: your growing baby is pushing up against your lungs, reducing the space they have to expand. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, this kind of gradual breathlessness on exertion is a normal feature of the third trimester and eases when the baby drops lower into the pelvis before birth. Other common symptoms at this stage include sore or leaky breasts, a white milky vaginal discharge, and the familiar third-trimester set of backache, heartburn, leg cramps, and swelling. Seek medical advice for any vaginal bleeding, and seek urgent help if breathlessness is sudden, severe, at rest, or accompanied by chest pain or a racing heart, as that pattern is different from normal pregnancy breathlessness and needs assessment.

What screening tests will my baby be offered after birth? Newborn screening tests are offered soon after birth to check for conditions that benefit from early treatment. According to the NHS, these screening tests are recommended because they can make sure your baby is given appropriate treatment quickly if a condition is found; they typically include the newborn physical examination, a hearing test, and the blood spot (heel prick) test, which screens for several rare but serious conditions. Screening is your choice: your decisions about whether or not to have these tests will be respected, and healthcare professionals will support you either way. It is worth learning about newborn screening before the birth, during the third trimester, so the choices do not arrive as a surprise in the busy first days; ask your midwife or doctor for more information about what each test looks for and how results are shared with you.

What is hypothyroidism and why does it matter in pregnancy? Hypothyroidism means an underactive thyroid gland that does not make enough thyroid hormone. According to NHS guidance, the thyroid is a gland in the front of the neck whose hormones control the body's metabolism, and a pregnant woman's thyroid hormones are vital both for her and for the development of her baby, particularly the baby's brain, which relies on the mother's thyroid hormones in early pregnancy before the baby's own thyroid starts working. There is an increased risk of pregnancy complications if hypothyroidism is not carefully treated. The most common cause is the immune system targeting the thyroid gland, known as Hashimoto's disease or Hashimoto's thyroiditis; less commonly, hypothyroidism follows previous treatment for an overactive thyroid or a thyroid tumour. The reassuring news: hypothyroidism is safely and effectively treated in pregnancy with levothyroxine tablets, and well-treated women usually have healthy pregnancies.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Is levothyroxine safe to take during pregnancy and how should I take it? Yes, levothyroxine (thyroxine) is safe to take while pregnant and while breastfeeding. According to NHS guidance, it is a synthetic form of the natural T4 hormone produced by the thyroid gland, and it is the recommended treatment for an underactive thyroid in pregnancy. How you take it matters for absorption: levothyroxine should be taken on an empty stomach, ideally first thing in the morning, and at a different time from any vitamin or mineral supplements you are taking, including pregnancy multivitamins and supplements containing calcium or iron, because these interfere with how well the medicine is absorbed. Never stop levothyroxine when you find out you are pregnant; if anything, your body needs more thyroid hormone in pregnancy, not less, and your doctor will adjust your dose based on blood tests. Continuing correct treatment protects both you and your baby's development.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Can I have a healthy pregnancy with a thyroid problem? Yes, in the great majority of cases. According to the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG), if your thyroid problem is well treated, you are highly likely to have an uncomplicated pregnancy and to give birth to a healthy baby. The two main thyroid conditions are hypothyroidism, where the body does not make enough thyroid hormones, safely treated with levothyroxine tablets, and hyperthyroidism, where the body makes too much thyroid hormone, usually treated in pregnancy with anti-thyroid tablets depending on the cause. The key risk to avoid is leaving thyroid problems untreated, as this can cause complications in pregnancy and affect the baby's health. When you are pregnant, your body needs more thyroxine than usual, so if you already take levothyroxine, your healthcare professional will discuss increasing your dose and will monitor your levels with blood tests through pregnancy.

What are the risks of untreated hypothyroidism in pregnancy? Untreated overt hypothyroidism carries serious risks, which is exactly why treatment matters so much. According to NHS clinical guidance, overt untreated hypothyroidism is associated with pregnancy loss, gestational hypertension, placental abruption, low birthweight babies, poor perinatal outcomes, and severe neurodevelopmental delay in the child. The reason for the developmental risk is timing: the developing fetal brain requires optimal thyroxine levels from early in the first trimester, before the baby's own thyroid begins producing hormones at around 12 weeks of pregnancy, so in early pregnancy the baby depends entirely on the mother's supply. Because pregnancy increases the body's requirement for thyroid hormone, women already on levothyroxine usually need a dose increase when pregnancy is confirmed. These risks apply to untreated disease; with proper levothyroxine treatment and monitoring, outcomes are excellent, so the practical message is to seek care early rather than to worry.

I take levothyroxine for my thyroid. What should I do before trying to conceive? Plan ahead with your GP, because thyroid control before conception protects the earliest weeks of pregnancy, when the baby's brain depends on your thyroid hormones. According to NHS guidance, the preconception levothyroxine dose is adjusted with the aim of keeping TSH (thyroid stimulating hormone) below 2.5 mU/L before pregnancy, and it is good practice to agree a plan with your doctor in advance so you can start an agreed dose increase yourself as soon as pregnancy is confirmed, while waiting for blood tests and a GP appointment. Once pregnant, contact your GP promptly for thyroid function tests, mentioning the pregnancy and how many weeks along you are, since pregnancy-specific reference ranges apply, and self-refer to midwifery services early. Untreated or undertreated thyroid disease can also make it harder to conceive, so optimising treatment helps fertility too.

What is gestational hypertension and how is it different from pre-eclampsia? Gestational hypertension, also called pregnancy-induced hypertension, is new high blood pressure that develops after 20 weeks of pregnancy without protein in the urine or other features of pre-eclampsia; it typically resolves after the baby is born. Pre-eclampsia is the related but more serious condition in which high blood pressure occurs together with protein in the urine or other organ involvement. According to NHS guidance and March of Dimes information, this distinction matters because the two conditions are monitored and managed differently, although gestational hypertension can progress to pre-eclampsia, which is why blood pressure and urine are checked at every antenatal appointment once it is diagnosed. Around 1 in 10 pregnant women have high blood pressure at some point in pregnancy. If you are diagnosed with gestational hypertension, expect more frequent monitoring, possible blood pressure medication, and extra growth scans, and report warning symptoms such as severe headache or vision changes immediately.

How is antenatal care different if I'm expecting twins? Multiple pregnancies are classed as high risk, which sounds alarming but simply means you receive extra care to keep you and your babies as safe as possible. According to the NHS, your antenatal team should be experienced in caring for women having twins or triplets, and you will have more scans and antenatal check-ups with your obstetrician and midwife than in a singleton pregnancy, with extra ultrasound scans to monitor your babies' growth closely. The number of tests and scans depends on the type of twins you are having. Reassuringly, most people expecting twins have an uneventful pregnancy and healthy babies. At your first consultant appointment, a care pathway will be agreed with you, setting out your appointments and scans, and many hospitals run free antenatal classes designed specifically for parents expecting multiples; because twins often arrive early, aim to complete classes by around week 34.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Why does it matter whether my twins share a placenta? The type of placenta and membranes your twins have, called chorionicity, is one of the most important pieces of information in a twin pregnancy, because it determines your care pathway and risk profile. According to the NHS, women with multiple pregnancies should be offered an ultrasound scan at around 11 to 14 weeks, and it is important to attend, because this is the best time to determine chorionicity and confirm your dates; a nuchal translucency screening test can be done at the same appointment if you wish. Twins with separate placentas (dichorionic) have lower risks and fewer scans; identical twins sharing one placenta (monochorionic) also share a blood supply, which requires closer monitoring for complications such as twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome.
