Between 16 and 20 weeks you should also be offered a cervical length scan to assess your risk of premature birth, with progesterone offered if the cervix is short.

What is twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS)? Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome is a complication that affects identical twins who share a placenta (monochorionic twins). According to the NHS, it is caused by abnormal connecting blood vessels in the shared placenta, which create an imbalanced blood flow from one twin, known as the donor, to the other, the recipient, leaving one baby with a greater blood volume than the other. TTTS affects roughly 10 to 15% of monochorionic twin pregnancies and can have serious consequences for both babies if untreated, which is exactly why monochorionic twins are scanned frequently, typically every two weeks, so any imbalance is spotted early. If TTTS is detected on a scan, you will be referred to a specialist fetal medicine centre, where options including laser treatment of the connecting vessels can be considered; because every TTTS pregnancy is different, the right treatment is decided individually with your doctor.

How does screening work in a twin pregnancy? Screening in a twin pregnancy follows the same principles as in a singleton pregnancy, with some adjustments. According to NHS guidance, like all pregnant women you will be offered a scan at about 12 to 14 weeks to screen for chromosomal conditions such as Down's syndrome, with scan measurements combined with a blood test to assess the chance of one or both babies being affected; the chance of screening flagging a possible problem is slightly higher than in a singleton pregnancy simply because two babies are being assessed. You will also be offered an anomaly scan at around 20 weeks to examine your babies' development in detail, and in twin pregnancies this scan may be scheduled slightly later and take longer, giving the babies more time to grow and the sonographer time to check each baby thoroughly. Your specialist team will talk through the options and support your decisions if screening raises any concerns.
source_name: NHS (South Tees Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: How many appointments will I have with an uncomplicated twin pregnancy? More than in a singleton pregnancy, on a structured pathway. According to NHS guidance, women with an uncomplicated dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twin pregnancy, meaning two placentas and two sacs, have at least 8 antenatal appointments, alongside a schedule of growth scans to track both babies. Monochorionic twins, who share a placenta, are seen and scanned more often because of the additional risks of a shared blood supply. Throughout the pathway, all screening tests are discussed in detail with you by your doctor or midwife, and the plan for the birth, including timing and mode of delivery, is discussed in the antenatal clinic well before your due date, since twins are usually born earlier than singletons. Between appointments, the usual urgent rules apply: call your midwife or hospital promptly if your waters break, you think you are in labour, you have bleeding, or a baby's movements change.

When are twins usually born? Twins are usually born earlier than single babies, and for monitored twin pregnancies, planned birth before full term is standard care rather than a complication. According to NHS clinical guidance based on NICE recommendations, women with a monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA) twin pregnancy, identical twins sharing a placenta, are offered a planned birth from around 36 weeks, while uncomplicated dichorionic (DCDA) twins are usually offered birth from around 37 weeks; higher-risk configurations such as monoamniotic twins are delivered earlier, between about 32 and 34 weeks, because the risks of continuing the pregnancy rise. During a twin labour, both babies' heartbeats are monitored continuously and clearly labelled, and a bedside ultrasound at the start of labour confirms each baby's position. The exact timing and mode of birth, vaginal or caesarean, is agreed individually in the antenatal clinic based on the babies' positions, growth, and your preferences.

What should I do if my baby under 3 months has a fever? Take it seriously and get them seen. According to NHS guidance, if a baby less than 3 months old has a fever, it is more likely to be caused by a serious infection than a fever in an older child, so if your baby under 3 months has a temperature higher than 38C (100.4F), you should take them to see a healthcare professional straight away rather than managing it at home. There is one exception: if the fever starts within 48 hours after their routine vaccinations and the baby seems otherwise well, it is okay to give paracetamol at the correct infant dose without needing to see a doctor. A temperature below 36C in a young baby is also a warning sign. When in doubt with a young baby, always seek assessment; young babies can become unwell quickly, and no one will criticise you for being cautious.

How do I take my baby's temperature correctly? For young babies, the armpit method is the accurate one. According to NHS guidance, to check your baby's temperature, place a digital thermometer under their armpit and hold their arm gently close to their body to keep the thermometer in place for as long as the instructions say, usually about 15 seconds; many digital thermometers beep when the reading is ready. Do not check a young baby's temperature on the forehead or in the ear, because these methods are not accurate for young children and can miss a real fever or falsely suggest one. A normal temperature is around 36.4C, though it varies slightly; 38C (100.4F) or above counts as a fever. Keep a working digital thermometer at home from before the birth, and remember that how your baby looks and behaves, feeding, alertness, breathing, and colour, matters alongside the number.

Which fever symptoms in a baby mean I should call 999 or go to A&E? Certain features alongside a fever signal a potential emergency. According to NHS guidance, go to the nearest hospital emergency department or call 999 if your baby has a temperature above 38C or below 36C (outside the 48 hours after vaccinations with no other worrying features), is breathing very fast or is too breathless to feed, is working hard to breathe with the muscles drawing in below the ribs or making grunting noises, is unusually drowsy, floppy, or difficult to wake, has a fit or seizure, or develops a rash that does not fade when a glass is pressed against it (the glass test). If a fever lasts more than 5 days, or returns after settling, contact your GP even without red features. For anything in between, ring your GP surgery or the NHS 111 service, and keep watching for any change while you wait.

How can I make my feverish baby more comfortable at home? Once a healthcare professional has confirmed that home care is appropriate, or for an older baby with no red or amber warning features, there are safe ways to keep your child comfortable. According to NHS guidance, you may want to lower their temperature using infant paracetamol and/or ibuprofen, following the dose instructions on the packaging for your child's age; if you have given ibuprofen and your child is still hot or in discomfort 2 hours later, you can try giving paracetamol as well. Offer regular feeds or fluids to prevent dehydration, dress your baby in light clothing, and keep the room comfortable rather than cold; do not sponge with cold water. Keep monitoring for red or amber features, and seek help if they develop, if the fever passes 5 days, or if your instinct says something is wrong. Fever is the body fighting infection; treating discomfort matters more than chasing a normal number.

What is a health visitor and how can they help my family? A health visitor is a nurse or midwife with additional training who supports families through pregnancy and early childhood. According to NHS guidance, health visitors work with you through your pregnancy up until your child is ready to start school, promoting healthy lifestyles and preventing illness through the delivery of the Healthy Child Programme, which includes home visits, development reviews, feeding support, and advice on sleep, crying, weaning, and parental wellbeing. Health visitors can also make referrals to other health professionals on your behalf, for example for hearing or vision concerns, to community paediatricians, or to child and adolescent mental health services, making them a practical gateway into the wider health system. For non-urgent worries about your baby, your health visiting team, alongside your local pharmacist, is often the right first contact, keeping GP appointments free for medical problems.

How does smoking as a couple affect our chances of getting pregnant? Quitting together makes a measurable difference. According to NHS guidance, fertility begins to improve quickly when you and your partner go smoke-free: after 12 months of trying, women who are smoke-free are twice as likely to be pregnant than women who smoke tobacco. Even smoking a small number of cigarettes a day can make getting pregnant more difficult, and second-hand smoke, breathing in tobacco smoke from a partner or others in the household, is also likely to reduce the chances of conceiving, which is why this is genuinely a couples' issue rather than only the pregnant partner's. Smoking also affects sperm quality, adding a second fertility reason for the other partner to quit. Free NHS stop smoking support is available for both partners, and quitting before conception also protects the future pregnancy and creates the smoke-free home a newborn needs.
source_name: NHS (Humber and North Yorkshire Healthier Together)

text: What is CMV and why is it a concern in pregnancy? CMV (cytomegalovirus) is a common virus that is part of the herpes group, and CMV infections are especially common in young children. According to the NHS, most adults who catch CMV have mild or no symptoms, but infection can be dangerous during pregnancy because it can cause problems for unborn babies, such as hearing loss, visual impairment or blindness, learning difficulties, and epilepsy. CMV is particularly dangerous to your baby if you have not had the infection before, since first-time infection in pregnancy carries the highest transmission risk. It is not always possible to prevent CMV, but you can reduce the risk by washing your hands regularly with soap and hot water, particularly after changing nappies or if you work in a nursery or daycare centre, and by avoiding sharing food, cutlery, or dummies with young children and kissing them on the mouth.

What should I do if I'm pregnant and exposed to chickenpox? Act quickly, even though the odds are in your favour. According to the NHS, most adults in the UK are immune to chickenpox because they had it in childhood, but if you have never had chickenpox, or you are unsure whether you have had it, and you come into contact with a child or adult who has it, speak to your GP, obstetrician, or midwife immediately. A blood test will find out whether you are immune. Chickenpox in pregnancy can cause complications for both mother and baby, particularly if caught in the first 28 weeks or around the time of birth, and treatments are available that work best when given early after exposure, which is why prompt contact matters more than waiting to see if spots appear. If you develop a chickenpox rash while pregnant, contact your GP straight away rather than attending the surgery unannounced.

Is slapped cheek syndrome dangerous in pregnancy? Usually not, but exposure should be reported. Slapped cheek syndrome (fifth disease) is caused by parvovirus B19 and is common in children, spreading through coughs, sneezes, and contaminated surfaces; the classic sign is a bright red rash on the cheeks. According to the NHS, in most cases the baby is not affected if you have slapped cheek syndrome during pregnancy, and most pregnant women who catch it have healthy babies. However, there is a small risk of miscarriage and other complications, so if you are pregnant and come into contact with anyone infected, talk to your doctor, who can carry out a blood test to check whether you are immune; many adults already are. If the test shows a recent infection, you will be referred to a specialist so your baby can be monitored with extra ultrasound scans, and treatment can be given if the baby shows signs of being affected.

What is the risk of rubella (German measles) in pregnancy? Rubella is uncommon in the UK thanks to high uptake of the MMR (measles, mumps and rubella) vaccination, but it remains one of the most serious pregnancy infections when it does occur. According to the NHS, if you develop rubella in the first 4 months of pregnancy, it can lead to serious problems, including birth defects and miscarriage. If you are pregnant, contact your GP or midwife as soon as possible if you come into contact with someone who has rubella, or if you develop a rash or have contact with anyone who has a rash; it is unlikely to be rubella in these circumstances, but a blood test may be needed to check. Because rubella is vaccine-preventable, the best protection is before pregnancy: if you are pregnant and unsure whether you have had 2 doses of the MMR vaccine, ask your GP practice to check your vaccination history, and catch up after birth if needed, since MMR is not given during pregnancy.

How do I avoid toxoplasmosis during pregnancy? Toxoplasmosis is an infection caused by the parasite Toxoplasma gondii, found in the poo of infected cats and in infected meat, and while the chance of catching it for the first time in pregnancy is small, infection during pregnancy can cause miscarriage or serious health problems for the baby. According to NHS guidance, practical prevention steps include: wearing gloves when emptying cat litter trays, or asking someone else to do it, and changing litter daily; wearing gloves when gardening and washing hands afterwards; cooking all meat thoroughly with no pink or blood, and washing hands and surfaces after handling raw meat; washing fruit, vegetables, and salads to remove soil; and avoiding unpasteurised goats' milk products. Reassuringly, toxoplasmosis cannot be caught from stroking a cat or simply having a cat as a pet. If you are worried you may have picked up the infection, speak to your GP or midwife; it can be treated with antibiotics.

Why have I been offered a blood test for viruses during pregnancy? If an ultrasound scan estimates that your baby is smaller than expected, or notes another structural concern, you may be offered a blood test to look for common infections. According to NHS guidance, a number of infections, including CMV and toxoplasmosis, can affect a baby's growth and development if they cross the placenta, so the blood test checks for recent or past infection in the mother as part of investigating the scan finding. It is important to understand that for most women who have this blood test, the results are normal, so being offered the test is a routine precaution rather than a diagnosis. If an infection is identified, additional care can be offered, and in many cases treatments exist that can help reduce the impact on the baby, which is exactly why identifying infection early is worthwhile. Your doctor will explain any positive result and the follow-up plan in detail.

What is a molar pregnancy? A molar pregnancy, medically called a hydatidiform mole, is a rare condition in which abnormal placental-type tissue grows in the womb instead of a normally developing pregnancy. According to NHS guidance, in a complete mole there is only molar tissue in the womb, while in a partial mole there may be both molar tissue and tissue that looks like the normal start of a pregnancy; unfortunately, the pregnancy cannot continue healthily in either case. In the UK, about 1 in 600 pregnancies is a molar pregnancy. Because the growth of this tissue is not under the body's normal checks and controls, it can occasionally keep growing after the pregnancy is removed, and very rarely it can grow beyond the womb, which is why molar pregnancies are followed up very carefully. With monitoring and treatment where needed, outcomes are excellent, with an almost 100 per cent cure rate.

What does follow-up after a molar pregnancy involve? After a molar pregnancy is treated, careful follow-up monitors your hCG hormone levels to make sure no molar tissue is left growing. According to NHS guidance from the specialist molar pregnancy follow-up service, blood and urine hCG tests are done every two weeks until the result returns to normal, and from the date of the first normal sample the tests continue every four weeks for a period. Follow-up is usually organised by post: a sample kit is sent to your home, you return samples to the specialist laboratory, and a member of staff contacts you with the results. This monitoring matters because, if left untreated, remaining molar tissue can keep growing and, in rare cases, spread beyond the womb; the follow-up system identifies exactly who needs further treatment and when, which is why treatment success rates are so high. Attend or return every scheduled test even when you feel completely well.
source_name: NHS Tayside (Hydatidiform Mole Follow-up Service Scotland)

text: Can I get pregnant again after a molar pregnancy? Yes. According to NHS guidance from the specialist molar pregnancy follow-up service, you can try for a baby once your follow-up monitoring is complete, meaning your hCG levels have returned to normal and the scheduled surveillance period has finished. Waiting until follow-up is complete matters because a new pregnancy also raises hCG, which would make it impossible to tell whether rising levels were from the new pregnancy or from persisting molar tissue. The long-term outlook is reassuring: most women go on to have normal pregnancies and healthy babies after a molar pregnancy, and following a successful subsequent pregnancy, no further monitoring is required. In a future pregnancy, tell your midwife about the previous molar pregnancy so an early scan can confirm the new pregnancy is developing normally. Emotional recovery matters too; pregnancy loss support services can help alongside the medical follow-up.
source_name: NHS Tayside (Hydatidiform Mole Follow-up Service Scotland)

text: What is eclampsia? Eclampsia is a rare but serious complication of pre-eclampsia in which the high blood pressure condition progresses to cause seizures (fits) in the mother. According to NHS guidance on pre-eclampsia, although most cases of pre-eclampsia cause no problems and improve soon after the baby is delivered, there is a risk of serious complications if pre-eclampsia is not monitored and treated, which is why women with severe pre-eclampsia are admitted to hospital, and medicine to prevent seizures may be given by injection or drip as part of their treatment. Eclampsia can occur before, during, or after birth. It is a medical emergency: a seizure in a pregnant or recently delivered woman requires immediate emergency care, calling an ambulance if outside hospital. The best protection against eclampsia is what routine antenatal care already does: detecting pre-eclampsia early through blood pressure and urine checks, monitoring it closely, and delivering the baby at the right time.

What is HELLP syndrome? HELLP syndrome is a rare, serious complication that occurs in women with severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia. The name stands for Haemolysis (breakdown of red blood cells), Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets, describing the three blood abnormalities that define it. According to NHS clinical guidance, HELLP involves problems with the liver, blood cells, and clotting alongside the high blood pressure and protein in the urine of pre-eclampsia. Symptoms can include pain in the upper right abdomen or just below the ribs, nausea and vomiting, headache, and generally feeling unwell, sometimes worse at night; these can be mistaken for normal pregnancy discomforts, which is why new upper abdominal pain in later pregnancy should always be assessed. HELLP most commonly occurs in the third trimester, and roughly a third of cases develop shortly after the baby is born, so vigilance continues postpartum. Treatment is delivered in hospital and centres on stabilising the mother and delivering the baby.

Can pre-eclampsia be prevented with aspirin? For women at increased risk, yes, partially. According to NHS clinical guidance based on NICE recommendations, low-dose aspirin reduces the risk of pre-eclampsia if it is started before 16 weeks of pregnancy. This is why your midwife assesses your pre-eclampsia risk factors at the booking appointment early in pregnancy: factors such as a previous pregnancy with pre-eclampsia, chronic high blood pressure, diabetes, kidney disease, autoimmune conditions, a first pregnancy, age over 40, a high BMI, a family history of pre-eclampsia, or a multiple pregnancy can lead to a recommendation to take a daily low-dose aspirin tablet, usually from 12 weeks until birth. Aspirin at this low dose, taken on medical advice, is considered safe in pregnancy for this purpose, but do not start aspirin on your own; the decision and dose should come from your midwife or doctor based on your individual risk profile.

How common is pre-eclampsia and is it usually serious? Pre-eclampsia is common, and most cases are mild. According to the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG), pre-eclampsia affects between 1 and 5 in 100 women during pregnancy, and it is usually mild, but in a small number of cases it can develop into a more serious illness affecting the mother and the baby's growth and wellbeing. Pre-eclampsia usually happens after 20 weeks of pregnancy, and its exact cause is not fully understood, though it is linked to how the placenta develops. It can also happen again in a future pregnancy; the likelihood depends partly on how early and how severely it occurred the first time, which is why women with previous pre-eclampsia receive closer monitoring and preventive low-dose aspirin in later pregnancies. Knowing that most cases are mild and well-managed helps balance appropriate vigilance with reassurance.

What is the third stage of labour? The third stage of labour is the period from the birth of your baby to the delivery of the placenta. According to NHS guidance, there are two options for this stage. With a physiological (natural) third stage, your body delivers the placenta on its own: no drugs are given, your own hormones trigger contractions of the uterus, the placenta peels away, and you push it out; this can take up to an hour. With an active (managed) third stage, the delivery of the placenta is managed by the team: an injection is given into your thigh as the baby is being delivered, causing the uterus to contract strongly so the placenta comes away, and the midwife then delivers it with gentle cord traction; this usually takes five to ten minutes. Your preference can go in your birth plan, though active management is recommended in some situations, such as after an induced labour or heavier bleeding.

What is delayed cord clamping and why is it done? Delayed (or optimal) cord clamping means waiting before clamping and cutting the umbilical cord after birth, rather than clamping immediately. According to NHS guidance, after your baby's birth the midwife delays placing a clamp on the cord, typically for at least one minute and up to five minutes, or until the cord has stopped pulsating, to allow oxygenated blood to continue pulsing from the placenta to your baby. This extra placental blood benefits the baby, and waiting is considered especially important for premature babies. A common misconception is that delayed clamping is only possible with a natural third stage; in fact, delayed cord clamping can still take place with active management of the placenta delivery, so you can have both the bleeding-reduction benefit of the injection and the benefits of delayed clamping. You can note your cord clamping preference in your birth plan and discuss it with your midwife.

Should I choose an active or natural third stage of labour? Both approaches are safe options in a straightforward birth, with different trade-offs. According to NHS guidance, the main advantage of an actively managed third stage, where an injection helps the placenta come away, is a possibly lower risk of heavy bleeding immediately after the birth, and you are also less likely to become anaemic in the days afterwards. A physiological (natural) third stage avoids the injection and lets your own hormones do the work, but it tends to take longer, and skin-to-skin contact and breastfeeding are encouraged because they stimulate the natural contractions that help the placenta separate. Your midwife may advise switching to active management partway through if bleeding is heavier than expected or the placenta is slow to deliver, which is routine rather than a failure of the natural approach. Discuss your preference antenatally and record it in your birth plan, staying flexible on the day.
source_name: NHS (Wrightington, Wigan and Leigh NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Which symptoms should make me seek help immediately for possible pre-eclampsia? Certain symptoms in the second half of pregnancy, or in the weeks after birth, need immediate medical review because they can indicate pre-eclampsia. According to NHS clinical guidance, pregnant women are advised to see a healthcare professional immediately if they experience: a severe headache; problems with vision, such as blurring or flashing before the eyes; severe pain just below the ribs; vomiting; or sudden swelling of the face, hands, or feet. Any one of these symptoms is enough to warrant a same-day call to your maternity unit, midwife, or the NHS 111 service; do not wait for a scheduled appointment, and do not wait to see whether other symptoms join it. These symptoms matter even if your blood pressure was normal at your last check, because pre-eclampsia can develop between appointments. Most callers turn out to be fine, and maternity units would always rather check than miss a case.

What happens to the placenta after it is delivered? After your baby is born and the placenta is delivered in the third stage of labour, your midwife examines the placenta and membranes carefully to check they are complete, because fragments left inside the womb can cause bleeding or infection and occasionally need removal. According to NHS guidance, during a physiological third stage the placenta peels away from the wall of the uterus as it contracts, drops down into the vagina, and is pushed out; in an active third stage, the midwife delivers it with gentle controlled cord traction while supporting the uterus, after checking for signs of separation such as the cord lengthening. The midwife also checks that your uterus feels firm and central afterwards, a sign it has contracted well; a full bladder can stop it contracting, so you may be encouraged to pass urine. Unless the parents have other plans for it, the placenta is then disposed of by the hospital.
source_name: NHS (Norfolk and Norwich University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Are saunas, hot tubs, and steam rooms safe during pregnancy? Caution is advised. According to the NHS, little is known about the safety of saunas, hot tubs, and steam rooms in pregnancy, and you may choose to avoid them because of the risks of overheating, dehydration, and fainting, particularly in the first 12 weeks of pregnancy. The concern is that in these hot environments your body cannot lose heat effectively by sweating, so your core temperature rises, and a significant rise in core temperature could be harmful in early pregnancy while the baby's organs are forming. Pregnancy also naturally makes you feel warmer, due to hormonal changes and increased blood supply to the skin, which makes overheating and feeling faint more likely. If you use a hydrotherapy pool, NHS guidance is that the water temperature should not be above 35C. A warm (not hot) bath at home remains a safe way to relax.

Why do pregnant women feel faint, especially when hot? Feeling faint in pregnancy has a clear physiological explanation. According to NHS guidance, hormonal changes in pregnancy can make women feel faint, and heat makes it worse: when you overheat, more blood flows close to your skin to help cool your body by sweating, which means there is less blood flow to internal organs such as your brain, and if your brain does not get enough blood and oxygen, you feel faint or can actually faint. This is why hot environments like jacuzzis, steam rooms, crowded warm spaces, and standing for long periods in heat are common fainting triggers in pregnancy. Practical steps that help: avoid getting too hot, drink plenty of fluids, avoid standing still for long periods, get up slowly from sitting or lying, and sit or lie down with your head lowered at the first sign of lightheadedness. Mention frequent fainting to your midwife, as it occasionally needs investigation.

Do I need to check all medicines with someone before taking them in pregnancy? Yes, all of them. According to the NHS, not all medicines are safe to take when you are pregnant, and this includes prescribed medicines as well as medicines you can buy in a pharmacy or shop, such as painkillers, cold remedies, allergy tablets, and herbal products. The rule is simple: check with a doctor, pharmacist, or midwife before you take any medicine while pregnant, since pharmacists can quickly confirm pregnancy-safe options for common complaints without an appointment. Equally important is the reverse rule: if you are already taking prescribed medicine for an ongoing condition, do not stop taking it without talking to your doctor first, because suddenly stopping treatment for conditions like epilepsy, asthma, depression, or thyroid disease can be more dangerous for you and the baby than the medicine itself. Your doctor can switch you to a safer alternative where one exists.

Why do I feel so hot all the time during pregnancy? Feeling warmer than normal is a standard part of being pregnant. According to NHS guidance, this is caused by hormonal changes and an increase in blood supply to the skin: your blood volume rises substantially during pregnancy, and more of that blood circulates near the skin surface, which makes you feel hot and can make you sweat more, especially in later pregnancy and at night. Because your body is already running warm, situations that add heat, hot weather, heavy clothing, hot baths, crowded rooms, saunas, or steam rooms, push you toward overheating, dizziness, and faintness faster than they would when not pregnant. Sensible measures include wearing loose layers of natural fabrics, drinking plenty of water, keeping rooms ventilated, using a fan at night, and cooling your wrists under cold water. Feeling hot is normal; a high measured temperature (fever) is different and should be checked, as infections in pregnancy need treatment.

Can I use fake tan or tanning products during pregnancy? Mostly yes, with two exceptions. According to NHS guidance, tanning creams (self-tan lotions and mousses applied to the skin) can be used during pregnancy; the active ingredient reacts with the outer layer of skin and very little is absorbed, though it is sensible to test any product on a small patch of skin first, because pregnancy can make skin more sensitive and reactive than usual. The exceptions: tanning pills should not be used, as they may be toxic to the baby, and spray tans are uncertain territory because it is not known whether breathing in tanning spray is harmful to the baby, so if you do have a spray tan, ensure good ventilation and consider protecting your nose and mouth, or choose a cream instead. Sunbeds are best avoided for general skin-health reasons, and pregnancy skin burns more easily in the sun, so use high-factor sunscreen.

How does pregnancy affect my hair and skin? Pregnancy hormones change how your hair and skin behave, and both can react differently to products you have always used. According to NHS-aligned guidance, skin can become more sensitive, greasier, or drier during pregnancy, and hair changes are common: many women notice thicker, shinier hair during pregnancy because hormones extend the hair growth phase, while hair shedding or loss is also common, particularly after birth, and usually grows back once hormones settle after the baby is born. Because skin sensitivity changes, it is worth patch-testing familiar products, including hair dyes and skin treatments, before full use, and choosing milder products where possible. Common pregnancy skin changes such as the linea nigra, darkened facial patches (chloasma), greasier or spotty skin, and stretch marks are all hormone-driven and largely fade after birth. Persistent itching without a rash, however, is different and should be reported to your midwife.

Do I get free prescriptions and dental care while pregnant? In the UK, yes. According to the NHS, if you are pregnant or have had a baby in the past 12 months, you are entitled to free NHS prescriptions and free NHS dental treatment. To access these, you need a maternity exemption certificate, known as a MatEx, and you are usually entitled to it as soon as you know you are pregnant. You do not apply yourself: your midwife, GP, or health visitor completes the application form for you, so ask them about it at an early appointment if you have not been offered it. The certificate remains valid throughout pregnancy and for a year after the birth. This entitlement covers NHS dental care, so you need to be seen as an NHS patient at a dentist providing NHS treatment; it does not cover private dental fees. Using free dental care during pregnancy matters because pregnancy gum disease is common.

Who is entitled to NHS pregnancy care? Everyone who is pregnant. According to the NHS, NHS pregnancy (antenatal) care is available to everyone who is pregnant; this is the care you have while pregnant to help make sure you and your baby are as healthy as possible, including midwife appointments, ultrasound scans, screening tests, and specialist referrals where needed. You do not need to wait until a particular stage of pregnancy or meet conditions to start care; contact a midwife service or your GP as soon as you know you are pregnant so your booking appointment can be arranged in the recommended window of 8 to 12 weeks. Alongside clinical care, pregnancy brings practical entitlements, including the maternity exemption certificate for free prescriptions and NHS dental treatment, paid time off work for antenatal appointments once your employer knows you are pregnant, and, for eligible families, financial support schemes; your midwife can point you to what applies in your area.

How do I protect my teeth from morning sickness? Frequent vomiting in pregnancy exposes your teeth to stomach acid, which wears away the protective enamel and exposes the softer dentin underneath; less enamel can lead to tooth sensitivity and tooth decay. According to March of Dimes guidance, there is one counterintuitive but important rule: after vomiting, do not brush your teeth right away, because brushing immediately scrubs the acid-softened enamel and wears it away further. Instead, rinse your mouth with water (adding a teaspoon of baking soda to the water helps neutralise acid), or with a fluoride mouthwash, and wait around 30 to 60 minutes before brushing. Keep up twice-daily brushing with fluoride toothpaste at other times, stay hydrated, and tell your dentist about your morning sickness so they can check for early erosion. With dental care free during pregnancy in the UK via the maternity exemption certificate, a check-up during pregnancy is worthwhile even without symptoms.

What causes pregnancy gingivitis? Pregnancy gingivitis, swollen, tender gums that bleed easily when brushing, is one of the most common dental problems in pregnancy, and hormones are the driver. According to NHS guidance, hormonal changes during pregnancy make your gums more vulnerable to plaque, leading to inflammation and bleeding; NHS Scotland guidance explains that the body produces more progesterone, which boosts blood supply to the mouth and makes gums more sensitive to the bacteria in plaque. The condition usually improves after birth as hormone levels fall, but during pregnancy it should be actively managed rather than ignored, because untreated gum inflammation can progress. The management is straightforward: thorough twice-daily brushing with fluoride toothpaste, daily cleaning between the teeth, limiting sugary foods to mealtimes and avoiding sugary drinks, and regular dental check-ups, which are free during pregnancy in the UK. See your dentist if bleeding, swelling, or soreness is persistent or worsening.

What are the earliest signs of pregnancy at 4 weeks? At 4 weeks, you may have no symptoms at all, and that is completely normal. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, symptoms begin as the pregnancy hormone human chorionic gonadotrophin (hCG) starts to kick in. During the first trimester you may experience a missed period, which is often one of the first signs of pregnancy, nausea (morning sickness, though it can strike at any time of day), tender breasts, tiredness, needing to urinate more often, and light spotting as the fertilised egg burrows into the lining of your uterus; this implantation spotting is normally light and brief, but see your doctor about any bleeding in pregnancy. Some home pregnancy tests can detect pregnancy from about three and a half weeks and are 99% accurate. If you think you could be pregnant but have no symptoms, you still might be, so a test is worthwhile.

What is happening at 5 weeks pregnant? At 5 weeks, your baby, still called an embryo, is around 2 millimetres long, about the size of a sesame seed, yet development is racing ahead. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, it is still early days: many women do not know they are pregnant at 5 weeks, particularly if their menstrual cycles are irregular, and some notice light bleeding and mistake implantation bleeding for a period. This is the week many women first realise they are pregnant, noticing a late period and perhaps feeling a bit under the weather; extreme tiredness is common, and now is a good time to take a pregnancy test if you have not already. Keep taking 400 micrograms of folic acid daily, which helps form your baby's nervous system and offers protection against conditions such as spina bifida, and consider a daily 10 microgram vitamin D supplement.

How is my baby developing at 6 weeks pregnant? At 6 weeks, your baby, or embryo, is around 6 millimetres long, about the size and shape of a pea; some people think it resembles a tadpole with its little tail. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, the arms and legs are starting to form as small projections known as limb buds, there are tiny dents where the ears will be, and there is a bump where the heart is and another bulge where the head will be. The embryo is covered with a thin layer of transparent skin. Sometimes the heartbeat can already be picked up by a vaginal ultrasound scan at this stage, although you are unlikely to be offered one unless you have had IVF. If you have not yet done so, this is a good week to share the news with your GP or ask for a midwife appointment so your antenatal care can begin.

What is happening to my body at 7 weeks pregnant? At 7 weeks, big changes are underway even though you probably do not look pregnant yet. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, there is more blood pumping around your body than before pregnancy, and over the course of pregnancy your blood volume will increase by up to 50%; this extra blood feeds your uterus with the oxygen and nutrients your baby needs, and it can make you feel thirstier than usual, so try to drink about 8 medium glasses of fluid a day, such as water, fruit tea, juice, or skimmed or semi-skimmed milk. Your womb is now about the size of a lemon, while your baby is the size of a grape and growing very quickly. In a first pregnancy you may not look pregnant until around week 12; in later pregnancies you may show earlier, because your womb and stomach muscles are more stretched.

When do I legally have to tell my employer I'm pregnant? Not for quite a while. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, legally you do not have to tell your employer until the 15th week before the baby is due, which at 7 weeks pregnant is still about 4 months away. This means the timing of telling your employer is largely your choice: some people tell early to access support, adjustments, or a workplace risk assessment, while others wait until after the first trimester when the risk of miscarriage falls and the first scan is done. Remember that certain rights only begin once your employer knows, including paid time off for antenatal appointments and the duty to assess and address workplace risks to your pregnancy, so if your job involves physical hazards, chemicals, heavy lifting, or long standing, telling earlier is safer. Organisations such as Maternity Action provide detailed guidance on maternity rights at work.

What should I know at 11 weeks pregnant? At 11 weeks you are only 2 weeks from the second trimester, when many women start to 'glow' and regain their energy as hormones settle down. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, around now you will be offered a range of tests looking at your blood, urine, and blood pressure, which provide an opportunity to have your health and your baby's health checked as part of routine antenatal care. As you start to show a little, your muscles and ligaments stretch, which can give you pains around your stomach; if it hurts a lot, see your midwife or doctor as soon as possible rather than assuming it is just stretching. Your body is now pumping up to 50% more blood than usual, which feeds your womb but can also make you feel hot, sweaty, and dizzy — your baby's way of telling you to rest.

Do I need vitamin D all year round in pregnancy? It depends on season and circumstances. According to the NHS, vitamin D keeps bones and muscles healthy, and from late March or early April to the end of September, most people make enough vitamin D from sunlight on their skin. However, between October and early March, sunlight is too weak, so you should consider taking a daily vitamin D supplement of 10 micrograms during those months, and in pregnancy a daily 10 microgram supplement is generally recommended throughout. Some people should take vitamin D all year round regardless of season, including those with darker skin, those who cover their skin outdoors, and those who spend little time outside; check whether this applies to you. The dose is the same 10 micrograms for adults and children, and you may be entitled to free vitamins through schemes such as Healthy Start, so ask your midwife.

What should I organise at 9 weeks pregnant? At 9 weeks you are nearing the end of the first trimester, and this is the week to get your antenatal care fully organised if you have not already. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, you will get your first dating scan between 8 and 14 weeks, and if this is your first pregnancy you will probably have around 10 appointments and 2 scans in total; it is worth asking whether you can see the same carer for your entire pregnancy for continuity. Ask your midwife or doctor about antenatal classes, including online options, which help you meet other expectant parents and prepare for birth. Keep up the essentials: 400 micrograms of folic acid daily until at least week 12, a 10 microgram vitamin D supplement, no smoking or alcohol, easy on the caffeine, and if you have a long-term health condition, make sure your specialist knows you are pregnant.

How is my baby developing at 14 weeks pregnant? At 14 weeks, your baby (now called a foetus) is around 8.5 centimetres long from head to bottom, about the size of a kiwi fruit. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, the head is getting rounder and more in proportion with the rest of the body, and your baby is kicking around, although you probably will not feel it yet. Your midwife might be able to hear the heartbeat using a handheld monitor placed on your tummy at appointments from around this stage. Inside you, your baby is doing something quite miraculous: having a wee. Small amounts of amniotic fluid are swallowed by the baby and pass into the stomach; the kidneys then kick in, and the fluid is passed back out again as urine. This swallowing-and-weeing cycle is an important part of how the baby's digestive system and kidneys develop and mature.

Why am I less steady on my feet as my bump grows? From mid-pregnancy onward, balance genuinely changes. According to the NHS week-by-week guide for week 15, you might feel fine now, but as your bump grows you will become more unstable on your feet, because the growing baby comes with a lot of packaging, the amniotic sac and fluid plus the placenta, shifting your centre of gravity forward and changing how you move. This can feel worrying when you are out and about, especially on public transport; the NHS advice is practical: if you cannot find a seat, do not be shy about asking somebody for theirs. Reduce fall risk by wearing supportive low-heeled shoes, using handrails on stairs, taking extra care on wet or icy surfaces, and slowing down rather than rushing. Around this week you may also notice the odd jabbing round ligament pain on the sides of your bump, and skin that feels a bit itchy; resting with your feet up helps both.

What does the baby's first movement feel like at 18 weeks? Around 18 weeks, many women feel their baby move for the very first time, and it is easy to miss. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, you might think that bubbling feeling in your tummy is wind, but it could be your baby moving; these first sensations, often described as flutters, bubbles, or butterflies, are called quickening, and you will soon be able to recognise them as definite movements. First-time mothers often notice movements later than this, sometimes after 20 weeks, which is normal. At 18 weeks your baby is around 14.2 centimetres from head to bottom, approximately the size of a bell pepper. Hopefully you are feeling more energetic now and managing your pregnancy symptoms; if you are struggling with day-to-day life, talk to your midwife or doctor, who are there to support you. Also make sure you have your Maternity Exemption Certificate for free prescriptions and dental care.

How is my baby developing at 19 weeks pregnant? At 19 weeks, your baby is around 15.3 centimetres long from head to bottom, approximately the size of a beef tomato, and is putting on weight in preparation for birth in about 21 weeks' time. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, a remarkable detail of this stage is that your baby's adult teeth are already starting to grow, lining up behind the first set of baby teeth that formed earlier; both sets are in place in the gums long before any tooth appears after birth. Common symptoms at this stage include round ligament pains on the sides of the belly, headaches, and a white milky vaginal discharge, with medical advice needed for any bleeding. If sleep is becoming difficult, pregnancy-specific remedies can help. You might still be deciding where to give birth, and there is plenty of time; trust your instincts and share any worries with your health professional.

Can I see the same midwife throughout my pregnancy? It is worth asking, and the NHS encourages you to. According to NHS guidance in the week-by-week pregnancy guide, when organising your antenatal care you should ask if it is possible to see the same carer for your entire pregnancy, to give you continuity. Continuity of carer means the same midwife or small team follows you through pregnancy, and where available it helps in practical ways: your carer knows your history without you repeating it, changes in your health or mood are noticed sooner, trust builds over time making it easier to raise sensitive concerns, and research links continuity models with better experiences of care. Availability varies between services and areas, so it cannot always be guaranteed, but expressing the preference early, at booking, gives the best chance. If continuity is not available, your handheld or digital maternity notes carry your full history between different carers, so bring them to every appointment.

What happens at 16 weeks pregnant? At 16 weeks you might start to feel your baby move; you will not be able to tell exactly what your baby is up to yet, but soon you could be feeling every kick, punch, hiccup, and somersault. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, an important safety topic this week is carbon monoxide: you cannot see, smell, or taste it, but carbon monoxide gas is a killer. You can be exposed by breathing in cigarette smoke, and also through faulty or poorly ventilated cooking or heating appliances. Routine carbon monoxide tests are now done during midwife appointments, and if you have a carbon monoxide detector at home, check it is working using the test button; if it does not beep, replace the battery or the detector, available at most supermarkets. From week 16 you can also have the whooping cough vaccine, offered up to week 32.

What changes when the second trimester begins at week 13? Week 13 marks the start of the second trimester: you are now a third of the way through your pregnancy and hopefully through the worst of your early symptoms. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, a small baby bump may now be visible as your uterus grows upwards and outwards, and if you have been feeling the urge to pee more often, that should stop as the womb moves up and away from your bladder. There is also a lot more blood pumping around your pelvic area, and some women find this increases their sex drive; it can also make you more thirsty, so drink lots of fluids. Sex during pregnancy is perfectly safe, unless your midwife or doctor has advised you otherwise, for example because of a low-lying placenta or bleeding. Many women feel their energy return during this trimester, often called the pregnancy 'glow'.

What should I know at 26 weeks pregnant? At 26 weeks there is a lot of activity going on inside your bump, and getting to know your baby's movement pattern matters: if you are worried that movements have slowed down or stopped, contact your midwife or maternity unit straight away, at any time of day or night. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, leg cramps may increase now, particularly during the night; foot and ankle exercises help, as does pulling your toes upwards during a cramp or rubbing the muscle where it hurts. You might also notice forgetfulness, sometimes called 'baby brain'; this is not a medical condition or scientifically proven, just something some pregnant women report, likely reflecting tiredness and having a lot on your mind. Check you have had the whooping cough vaccine, usually offered around week 20 and available from week 16 to week 32; if not, speak to your midwife or GP.

How is my baby developing at 27 weeks pregnant? At 27 weeks, at the very end of the second trimester, your baby is around 36.6 centimetres long from head to heel and weighs about 875 grams, approximately the length of a big leek and the weight of a head of cauliflower. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, two important milestones are underway: your baby's lungs are now capable of breathing, a major step toward readiness for life outside the womb, and your baby is getting plumper by the day. A few weeks ago your baby looked a bit like a wrinkled prune; now the folds of skin are being filled out by fat, and all the organs are maturing as your baby prepares for life outside the uterus. This is also a great time to start thinking about where you would like to give birth, if you have not decided already.

Until when can I fly without a fit-to-fly letter? Week 27 is a practical travel milestone. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, this is probably the last week you can fly anywhere without a 'fitness to fly' note: if you need to fly after this week, you will need a note from your midwife or doctor confirming that your pregnancy is uncomplicated. Airlines set their own rules, and most will not carry passengers at all in the last month before the due date, so always check your specific airline's policy before booking, both for the outbound and, importantly, the return flight, since you will be further along coming home. Week 27 also carries a sleep-position reminder for the trimester ahead: NHS guidance says sleeping on either side is better than on your back, because after week 28 research suggests back-sleeping can double the risk of stillbirth, and it also worsens backache, constipation, and piles.

I'm going to be a single parent. Is support available? Yes, and you are far from alone. According to the NHS week-by-week pregnancy guide, around 1 in 4 families are single-parent families, so the worries you may have about money or how you will cope are shared by a very large group of parents who manage well. Practical steps during pregnancy include: telling your midwife about your circumstances so they can connect you with local support; bringing a trusted friend or family member as your birth partner and to antenatal classes; checking your entitlement to financial support such as the Healthy Start scheme for free milk, fruit, vegetables, and vitamins; and building a support network before the birth through antenatal groups. Single-parent charities offer specific advice on benefits, childcare, and legal questions. If anxiety or low mood about the future is weighing on you, talk to your midwife or doctor, who can point you toward the right support.

What is normal at 29 weeks pregnant? At 29 weeks, in the third trimester, emotions and physical symptoms both intensify. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, being pregnant stirs up all kinds of emotions, excitement, nerves, or both, and the important thing is to talk to friends, family, your midwife, or doctor about anything worrying you. Physically, you might feel a bit breathless as your baby pushes against your lungs, and carrying the extra weight strains your body. Sleep often becomes harder: leg cramps are common, the baby pressing against your bladder means more night-time bathroom trips, and babies seem to have a habit of being really active just as you want to go to sleep. It is fine to feel irritated when people tell you to enjoy your sleep while you can. Keep tracking your baby's movement pattern, and report any reduction straight away, at any hour.

Why am I having strange dreams at 30 weeks pregnant? Vivid or disturbing dreams are a recognised feature of late pregnancy. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, at 30 weeks, three-quarters of the way there, you may be having trouble sleeping, and when you do sleep, you may have disturbing dreams. Try to remember these dreams are not real: they are fuelled by your hormones and by the anxiety you are probably feeling about the big changes ahead, and talking about your dreams helps put everything into perspective. If you are feeling under stress, talk to your midwife or doctor. Your baby is now around 39.9 centimetres from head to heel, approximately the size of a cabbage. Week 30 also carries a vaccine deadline reminder: you should have the whooping cough vaccine before week 33; you can have it later, but your baby is less likely to get the full protection, so speak to your midwife or GP this week if you have not had it.

What is checked at antenatal appointments around 31 weeks? At 31 weeks you are almost there: you are very unlikely to go beyond another 9 or 10 weeks, and you could have your baby in just 6 weeks, which would not be considered early. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, your baby and bump are still growing, with a final growth spurt coming in a couple of weeks, and your baby still has lots of fattening up to do. If this is your first baby, your midwife or doctor will probably check the size of your uterus at appointments around now, estimated by measuring up your stomach from the top of your pubic bone to the top of your bump; this measurement, plotted over time, tracks your baby's growth. They will also gently feel your belly to work out which way up your baby is positioned; some babies will already be head down, ready for birth, while others turn later.

Why do my pregnancy symptoms suddenly feel better at 34 weeks? A late-pregnancy easing of symptoms often has a specific cause: your baby dropping. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, it can feel as though some pregnancy symptoms have vanished when your baby moves head down into the pelvis, a staged process called 'engagement'; the drop itself is called 'lightening'. This frees up space in your abdomen and gives your lungs a bit of a break, so breathlessness should ease, and it reduces pressure on your stomach, so heartburn may disappear too. The shift can feel like a relief, but it does not mean you are about to give birth; you will probably still wait several more weeks. You will also have your week 34 antenatal appointment this week, where the usual blood pressure, urine, and growth checks are done. Keep eating healthily, take gentle exercise, and rest often as the final weeks approach.

Why are there yellow stains in my bra during late pregnancy? If you have spotted yellow stains in your bra in the final weeks, that is probably colostrum, the early milk that is rich in antibodies. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, some pregnant women start to make colostrum weeks or even months before the birth, and leaking it before the baby arrives is completely normal and does not affect your milk supply. When you breastfeed after birth, colostrum helps protect your baby from stomach bugs and other infections. Around 3 to 5 days after the birth, your milk will come in and your breasts will look even bigger, so you may wish to prepare by getting measured for a feeding bra that allows a bit of growth, and stocking up on breast pads to soak up extra milk and protect your clothes. Week 35 is also when many women choose to begin maternity leave, though others work until the birth; do what feels right for you.

What does 'full term' mean at 37 weeks? At 37 weeks your baby could come any day now, and it would not be considered early. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, your baby is now 'full term', meaning they are probably big enough and mature enough to survive in the outside world, although you may still have to wait several more weeks for labour to start; pregnancy normally lasts from 37 to 42 weeks. If you are carrying twins, you will probably give birth this week, since twin pregnancies rarely go beyond 38 weeks. By now, around 95% of babies are head down, facing their mother's back, which is the best position for labour; when the baby's head moves down into the pelvis it is said to be 'engaged', and you might see your bump drop a bit. If your baby is still bottom-down (breech), do not worry; your midwife will discuss the options with you.

What signs should I watch for at 39 weeks pregnant? At 39 weeks, labour genuinely could start any day: pregnancy normally lasts around 40 weeks, and most women go into labour within a week either side of their due date. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, you might notice more vaginal discharge than usual, which should be thin, white, and not smell of much. If you spot a slimy blob of mucus that is yellow or bloody, that is a 'show': the sticky plug that sealed your cervix coming away, and it can be one of the first signs your baby is on the way, although you could still have days to wait, so no need to grab the hospital bag immediately. Back pain is common as the baby moves down the pelvis and presses against your spine, and you will feel increased pressure at the bottom of your bump. Practice contractions may increase; if they become painful and regular, they could be the real thing, so phone your maternity unit for guidance.

What should I have ready at 36 weeks pregnant? Week 36 is the final preparation checkpoint. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, make sure your hospital bag is packed and that you have written down your hospital reference number and important phone numbers, and keep your maternity notes where you can quickly grab them. Many women are on maternity leave now or about to stop work. It is a good time to tone up your pelvic floor muscles: gentle exercises help prevent leakage when you laugh, sneeze, or cough; get the muscles going by pretending you are having a pee and then stopping midflow, then practising that squeeze regularly (without actually stopping urine flow on the toilet). If you have not attended antenatal classes, ask your midwife about online options and invite your partner to take part. From 37 weeks your baby is full term, so from now on labour could genuinely start at any time.

How likely am I to need a caesarean or assisted birth? Knowing the numbers helps set realistic expectations before labour. According to the NHS week-by-week guide, nearly 3 out of 10 babies are delivered by caesarean; this is ideally planned in advance, but it can also be carried out in an emergency during labour. In a caesarean, a 10 to 20 centimetre cut is made in your stomach and womb, your baby is delivered through the opening and brought over to you, the wound is closed with stitches, and you are offered an injection of oxytocin to help your womb contract and reduce blood loss; the procedure takes 40 to 50 minutes and you may recover in hospital for a few days. Separately, just over 1 in 10 births are 'assisted', meaning the baby needs some help getting out using a ventouse (suction cup) or forceps, with a local anaesthetic or epidural offered for pain relief. Most births need neither.

When should I pack my hospital bag? Earlier than you might think. According to the NHS, it is a good idea to get your hospital bag packed at least 3 weeks ahead of your due date, so you are ready to go if your baby decides to make an early appearance; NHS maternity units commonly suggest starting to pack from around 32 to 36 weeks of pregnancy. Many parents find it helpful to pack two bags: one with things for labour itself, and one with everything needed afterwards, including something for your new baby to wear going home. Involve your birth partner in packing so they can find things quickly during labour when you might not be able to direct them. Even if you are planning a home birth, pack a small bag anyway, in case you need to transfer to hospital at any point. Keep the bag somewhere easy to grab, along with your maternity notes.

What should I pack in my hospital bag? Keep it compact: most hospital stays after birth are short, from around six hours to two days, so you only need enough for that time. According to NHS guidance, for yourself pack: your birth plan and hospital notes; loose comfortable clothing for labour; a dressing gown, slippers or flip-flops (useful in the shower), and front-opening nightwear; a wash bag with toothpaste, toothbrush, hairbrush, flannel, soap, lip balm, deodorant, hair bands, hand sanitiser, and body lotion; maternity pads and comfortable underwear; a going-home outfit; and an eye mask, which can help you sleep on a brightly lit ward. For your baby: nappies, cotton wool or water wipes, vests and sleepsuits in newborn and 0-3 month sizes, a hat, and a going-home outfit. Your birth partner should bring spare clothes, toiletries, drinks, and snacks. The car seat is only needed at discharge, not during your stay.

How does my body prepare for labour in the final weeks? In the last few weeks of pregnancy, your hormones are actively preparing your pelvis for birth. According to NHS guidance, they do this by softening the ligaments at the front of your pelvis and in the lower back region, which makes the pelvis more flexible for the baby's passage but also makes those areas more vulnerable to strain. Be mindful of this during daily tasks: avoid extra stress and strain on your back and pelvis by sticking to light duties, changing your posture regularly, and avoiding heavy lifting and awkward twisting. Practical comforts include using a gym (birthing) ball as an alternative to sitting on the sofa, which encourages good posture and can help position the baby, and gently massaging the lower back muscles as they start to ache. These same hormonal changes explain why pelvic girdle pain can flare in late pregnancy; mention significant pain to your midwife.
source_name: NHS (South Tees Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: Is it safe to dye my hair during pregnancy? Most evidence says yes. According to the NHS, most research, although limited, shows it is safe to colour your hair while pregnant. The chemicals in permanent and semi-permanent hair dyes are not highly toxic, and only very small amounts are absorbed through the skin during colouring. If you want to be extra cautious, sensible options include waiting until after the first 12 weeks of pregnancy, when the baby's rapid early development is complete; using highlights, which are applied to hair strands rather than the scalp, reducing skin contact; choosing semi-permanent vegetable-based dyes such as henna; colouring in a well-ventilated space; wearing gloves if applying dye yourself; and doing a patch test first, because pregnancy can make skin more sensitive and reactive than usual, even to products you have used before. If you work with hair dyes professionally, ask your employer for a workplace risk assessment.

Where can I choose to give birth? You have three main options: at home, in a unit run by midwives (a midwifery unit or birth centre), or in hospital. According to the NHS, your choices depend on your needs, risks, and to some extent on where you live, and you can usually consider any of these locations. If your pregnancy is high risk or you have certain medical conditions, it is safest to give birth in hospital, where specialists are available in case you need treatment during labour. If your pregnancy is low risk and you give birth at home or in a midwife-led unit, you are less likely to need assistance such as forceps or ventouse. Wherever you choose, the place should feel right for you, and importantly, you can change your mind at any point in your pregnancy. Your midwife will discuss local options, but if you are willing to travel, you are free to choose any maternity service.

Is a home birth safe? For many women, yes, with important nuance. According to the NHS, if you have had a baby before, a planned home birth is as safe as having your baby in hospital or a midwife-led unit. For first babies, home birth carries a slightly higher risk of a serious problem for the baby compared with hospital birth, which your midwife will discuss with you. At a home birth you are supported by a midwife who stays with you through labour, and if you need help or labour is not progressing as it should, your midwife arranges transfer to hospital; needing to transfer is relatively common with first babies and is a planned-for part of the pathway rather than an emergency in most cases. Benefits include being in familiar surroundings, and, for low-risk pregnancies, a lower likelihood of intervention such as forceps or ventouse than in hospital. Note that epidurals are not available at home.

What is a midwife-led unit (birth centre) and who is it for? A midwife-led unit, also called a birth centre, is a birth setting run by midwives, designed to feel more homely than a hospital labour ward while still being professionally staffed. According to NHS guidance, midwives care for you during labour and support you to have a normal birth, and rooms are typically equipped for active labour with birthing pools, balls, bean bags, and mats. Midwife-led units suit women with low-risk pregnancies, and for this group, giving birth in a midwifery-led unit reduces the chance of medical intervention compared with a hospital labour ward. The key trade-off to understand: if you need specialist medical care or pain relief that only doctors can provide, such as an epidural, you will need to transfer to the nearest consultant-led unit, which may be by ambulance if the birth centre is not on a hospital site. Your midwife can tell you the transfer arrangements for your local unit.
source_name: NHS (University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is an obstetric (consultant-led) unit and when is it recommended? An obstetric unit, also called a consultant-led unit or labour ward, is the hospital birth setting providing full medical, obstetric, neonatal, and anaesthetic care. According to NHS guidance, you will be advised to give birth in an obstetric unit if you or your baby are likely to need care from an obstetrician or other health professionals before, during, or after labour; examples include high-risk medical conditions, complications in the current pregnancy, a planned VBAC, twins, or an induced labour. Even in a consultant-led unit, a midwife cares for you throughout your time on the labour ward, and you can have whoever you would like supporting you: your partner, a relative, or a close friend, with doulas welcome in most units. Some women with no expected problems still choose an obstetric unit, for example because they want guaranteed epidural access; that choice is yours.

What does the evidence say about birth outcomes in different settings? The main UK evidence comes from the Birthplace study, reflected in NICE guidance. According to NHS maternity information summarising this research, low-risk women planning their second or later birth at home or in a midwifery-led unit had fewer interventions, less need for pain relief, a higher vaginal birth rate, a lower likelihood of caesarean section, and reported greater satisfaction with their birth experience, compared with similar women giving birth on hospital labour wards. For first-time mothers, out-of-hospital settings still show fewer interventions, but home birth specifically carries a small increase in risk of a serious problem for the baby, which is why the setting discussion is individualised. Around 45% of women are considered at very low risk of complications, and NICE recommends they be offered the full choice of settings. Discuss what the evidence means for your specific circumstances with your midwife.
source_name: NHS (Mid Cheshire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What should I be doing at 32 weeks pregnant? At 32 weeks you are deep in the third trimester and practical preparation ramps up. NHS maternity units commonly recommend starting to pack your hospital bag around 32 weeks, packing one bag for you and one for the baby, and involving your birth partner so they can find things quickly during labour. According to NHS guidance, this is also within the window for finalising key decisions: the whooping cough vaccine should ideally be done by now (before week 33 for best protection), your birth plan and preferred birth place should be taking shape, and you should be firmly in the habit of monitoring your baby's movement pattern, reporting any change immediately. Common symptoms at this stage include breathlessness, heartburn, leg cramps, swelling, backache, and Braxton Hicks practice tightenings. From 28 weeks onward, remember to settle to sleep on your side rather than your back, for every sleep including naps.
source_name: NHS (The Hillingdon Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is reflux in babies and is it serious? Reflux is when a baby brings up milk, or is sick, during or shortly after feeding. According to the NHS, it is very common and usually gets better on its own: reflux usually starts before a baby is 8 weeks old and gets better by the time they are 1. The cause is simple mechanics: the muscle at the bottom of the food pipe acts as a kind of door into the stomach, and while this muscle is still developing during the first year, it can open when it should not, usually when the baby's tummy is full. It is common for a baby under 1 to bring up a little milk during or after a feed without being distressed, and your baby does not usually need to see a doctor for reflux as long as they are happy, healthy, and gaining weight.

What is silent reflux in babies? Sometimes babies have the signs of reflux but do not bring up milk or vomit; this is known as silent reflux. According to NHS guidance, silent reflux happens when milk travels back up the food pipe but is swallowed again rather than spat out, which makes it harder to identify because there are no obvious clues like spitting up. Signs that may point to silent reflux include crying or seeming uncomfortable during or after feeds, difficulty feeding or refusing to feed, coughing or hiccupping around feeds, and unsettled behaviour that resembles colic. Because some symptoms overlap with colic and with cow's milk allergy, it is worth talking to your pharmacist, GP, or health visitor if you suspect silent reflux, and keeping a simple record of feeds, symptoms, and crying episodes, which helps professionals decide whether your baby needs assessment or treatment.

How can I ease my baby's reflux at home? Several simple feeding adjustments help most babies with reflux. According to NHS guidance, get advice on your baby's breastfeeding position or bottle-feeding technique, hold your baby upright during feeding and for as long as possible afterwards, at least 30 minutes where you can, and give formula-fed babies smaller feeds more often rather than large feeds, since an overfull tummy makes reflux worse. Burp your baby in the middle of a feed as well as at the end to release swallowed air, and for bottle-fed babies, consider a teat and bottle designed to reduce milk flow. Check that a formula-fed baby is getting the right amount of milk and not being overfed. One rule must never be bent for reflux: your baby should still always sleep flat on their back, never on their side or front, because safe sleep guidance takes priority.
source_name: NHS (Kingston and Richmond NHS Foundation Trust)

text: When should I see a GP about my baby's reflux? Most reflux needs no medical treatment, but certain patterns warrant a GP visit. According to NHS guidance, contact your GP if your baby is not improving after you have spent 2 weeks trying to help them with reflux measures at home; if your baby gets reflux for the first time after they are 6 months old, since reflux normally starts before 8 weeks; if your baby is older than 1 year and still has reflux; if your baby is not gaining weight or is losing weight; or if you are struggling or feel unable to cope with your baby's symptoms, which is a valid reason to seek help in itself. A GP or specialist may also want to check whether your baby has a cow's milk allergy, because its symptoms can closely mimic reflux. If home measures fail, options include thicker formula and, if needed, medicines that reduce stomach acid.
source_name: NHS (Kingston and Richmond NHS Foundation Trust)

text: My baby brings up a little milk after every feed. Is that normal? Yes, in most cases. Bringing up small amounts of milk after feeds, known as posseting, is completely normal in young babies. According to NHS Scotland clinical guidance, milk may flow readily back up from the stomach in the early weeks and months because of low muscle tone in the sphincter between the stomach and oesophagus, which simply takes time to mature. A baby's stomach contents are also less acidic than an adult's, which is why gastro-oesophageal reflux is common in babies while true reflux disease (GORD) is rare. The reassuring test is the baby, not the milk: a baby who is generally content, feeding well, and gaining weight is fine even if they posset after most feeds. Keeping the baby upright after feeds and avoiding overfeeding reduce it. Effortless small possets are different from forceful or projectile vomiting, which should be checked.

How and when should I burp (wind) my baby? Winding releases air your baby swallowed during feeding, and doing it at the right moments prevents discomfort and reduces milk being brought back up. According to NHS guidance, burp your baby in the middle of a feed as well as at the end; for breastfed babies a natural moment is before switching breasts, and for bottle-fed babies, pause periodically during the feed. Hold your baby upright against your shoulder, sitting upright on your knee with their chin supported, or lying face-down across your lap, and gently but firmly rub or pat their back until the air comes up; some babies burp quickly, others take a few minutes, and not every session produces a burp. Keeping the baby upright during feeding and for at least 30 minutes afterwards also aids digestion. Note that breastfed babies often swallow less air and may not always need burping.
source_name: NHS (Kingston and Richmond NHS Foundation Trust)

text: What is tongue-tie and how does it affect breastfeeding? Tongue-tie is when the strip of tissue attaching the tongue to the floor of the mouth, called the frenulum, is shorter than normal. According to the NHS, it can make it harder for your baby to breastfeed by preventing them from latching on properly, which can then lead to sore or cracked nipples, and to engorged breasts and even mastitis caused by poor latching and ineffective feeding. Tongue-tie ranges from mild to severe; in severe cases the tongue may be completely fused to the floor of the mouth. You may be able to spot it by looking into your baby's mouth when they are yawning or crying, though it is not always easy to see. Signs during feeding can include difficulty latching, a clicking sound as they feed, and long or frustrated feeds. Importantly, some babies with tongue-tie have no problems at all and need no treatment.

Does every tongue-tie need to be treated? No. According to NHS guidance, not all tongue-ties need to be divided, and in some situations breastfeeding problems can be prevented or resolved simply by the mother receiving correct positioning and attachment support right from the beginning. A baby with tongue-tie who is not well attached will compress the nipple rather than the breast when feeding, which causes nipple pain and can mean the baby does not get sufficient milk; skilled adjustment of positioning often fixes this without any procedure. The pathway is therefore: a thorough feeding assessment first, by a midwife, health visitor, infant feeding team, or tongue-tie practitioner; a feeding plan may be tried initially, with review the following week to see whether feeding has improved; and only if difficulties persist after proper support is division of the tongue-tie recommended to allow increased tongue movement. Ask your midwife or health visitor for a referral if feeding is painful or difficult.

What happens during a tongue-tie division? Tongue-tie division, sometimes called releasing or snipping, is a quick, simple procedure. According to NHS guidance, the baby is supported at the head and shoulders while a trained practitioner uses sharp, blunt-ended scissors to gently release the membrane (frenulum) under the tongue. There is little, if any, pain or bleeding, and no anaesthetic is normally needed in young babies.
