Blood pressure checks, urine tests, and blood tests in prenatal care help your provider find conditions like high blood pressure and anemia early. These routine measures help keep both the mother and baby safer during pregnancy.
Good prenatal checkups include listening to the baby’s heartbeat and measuring the mother’s belly size. These simple steps help the provider track growth and spot concerns early.

As the due date gets closer, the baby gains more fat and grows stronger lungs. This last stage helps the baby stay warm and breathe well after birth. Your provider watches these changes through discussion and sometimes scans or tests.

Your bag for the birth place can include clothes, important papers, and items that help you feel comfortable. Having a plan for transport and knowing who will accompany you can ease stress when labor begins.

During vaginal birth, the baby moves through the birth canal, and the placenta follows after the baby is born. Providers look for steady progress and may offer support for breathing and pushing. A healthy delivery depends on good care and a calm birth environment.

Your body will still be changing after birth, and it is normal to need time to rest. Use this period to recover, accept help from family, and watch for any heavy bleeding, fever, or pain that does not get better.

Breastfeeding can be easier if you keep the baby close and feed on demand. Look for signals like hand-to-mouth movement, rooting, or lip smacking. Being patient and offering the breast when the baby is calm can help both of you settle into feeding.

Keep the umbilical cord stump clean and dry until it falls off. Do not cover it with tight clothing or wet diapers. Check the area for redness or pus, and tell a health worker if you notice any signs of infection.

If you feel mild pain or a headache, your provider may recommend paracetamol. Do not take ibuprofen or other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs in pregnancy unless your provider says they are safe. Always check before taking any medicine.

Whooping cough can be serious for newborns, so the Tdap vaccine in pregnancy helps protect the baby after birth. It is usually given in the third trimester. Ask your provider about timing so the vaccine gives the baby the best protection.
source_name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

text: Partners can support pregnancy by attending visits, helping with household tasks, and encouraging healthy habits. A supportive home helps reduce stress and makes it easier for the mother to rest, eat well, and follow provider advice.

Heavy bleeding after birth, a very fast heartbeat, confusion, or fainting are emergency signs that need immediate care. If any of these happen, go to the nearest health facility or call emergency services right away.

Managing gestational diabetes often includes checking your blood sugar and choosing vegetables, lean protein, and whole grains. Avoid large portions of sugary foods and drinks. Your provider may work with you to find a safe meal plan and support you with regular follow-up.

Chronic high blood pressure is present before pregnancy or diagnosed early, while pregnancy hypertension starts after 20 weeks. Both need careful monitoring. Your provider may suggest measuring your blood pressure regularly and may prescribe medicine if needed.

If your provider finds anemia in pregnancy, eating iron-rich foods and taking supplements as directed often helps. Try pairing iron-rich meals with vitamin C–rich fruits like oranges or guava, because vitamin C helps your body absorb iron better.

An ectopic pregnancy is when a fertilised egg implants and grows outside the main cavity of the uterus, most often in a fallopian tube. Because a fallopian tube cannot support a growing pregnancy, the pregnancy cannot survive and can be dangerous for the pregnant person if it is not treated. Symptoms usually appear between weeks 4 and 12 of pregnancy and can include one-sided pain low in the abdomen, unusual vaginal bleeding that may be watery and dark, and discomfort when passing urine or stool. Some people have no obvious symptoms at first and only find out during an early scan. Risk is higher with previous ectopic pregnancy, past tubal surgery, pelvic infection, or pregnancy conceived with an intrauterine device in place. An ectopic pregnancy is diagnosed with a blood test for the pregnancy hormone hCG and an ultrasound scan. Treatment options include medication, keyhole surgery, or, in some cases, close monitoring. Anyone who thinks they may have an ectopic pregnancy should contact a doctor or early pregnancy unit promptly.

Certain symptoms in early pregnancy may signal an ectopic pregnancy, which is a medical emergency when a fallopian tube ruptures. Warning signs to act on include persistent pain low on one side of the abdomen, vaginal bleeding or a brown watery discharge, shoulder-tip pain (pain where the shoulder ends and the arm begins), and pain or discomfort when going to the toilet. If a fallopian tube ruptures, the person may suddenly feel sharp, intense abdominal pain, become very dizzy or faint, look pale, or have a fast heartbeat. These are signs of internal bleeding and need emergency help right away. Anyone with these severe symptoms should call emergency services or go to the nearest emergency department immediately, as ruptured ectopic pregnancy is life-threatening without rapid treatment. Milder one-sided pain or unusual bleeding in early pregnancy is not an emergency by itself but should still be checked urgently by a doctor or an early pregnancy unit, because ectopic pregnancy is easier and safer to treat before a tube ruptures.

A miscarriage is the loss of a pregnancy during the first 23 weeks. Most miscarriages happen in the first trimester, before 12 weeks, and are usually caused by chromosome problems in the developing baby that mean it cannot grow normally. These causes are almost never related to anything the pregnant person did or did not do. The main sign is vaginal bleeding, which may come with cramping or pain low in the abdomen. Some miscarriages are found during a routine scan before any symptoms appear; this is called a missed miscarriage. Not all early bleeding means a miscarriage is happening, because light spotting is common in early pregnancy, but any bleeding should be checked. Miscarriage is common, occurring in about 1 in 8 known pregnancies, and having one does not usually mean there is a problem conceiving again. After a miscarriage, physical recovery and the return of normal cycles vary, and emotional support is important. A doctor or early pregnancy unit can confirm what is happening and explain the options for care.

Vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy should always be checked, because it can be a sign of miscarriage. Contact a midwife, doctor, or early pregnancy unit if you have bleeding, especially when it comes with cramping, lower abdominal pain, or the passing of tissue or clots. Seek urgent same-day advice if bleeding is heavy, if you soak through a pad, or if you feel unwell. Call emergency services or go to an emergency department right away if you have very heavy bleeding, severe abdominal pain, shoulder-tip pain, or feel faint, dizzy, or lightheaded, as these can indicate a serious problem such as an ectopic pregnancy or significant blood loss. It is important to know that light spotting can be normal in early pregnancy and does not always mean a miscarriage. Even so, it is safer to have any bleeding assessed rather than wait. An early pregnancy unit can arrange a scan and blood tests to check whether the pregnancy is continuing and to guide the next steps in care.

Pre-eclampsia is a pregnancy condition involving high blood pressure and signs that organs such as the kidneys and liver are under strain, usually after 20 weeks. Early on there are often no symptoms, and it is picked up through raised blood pressure and protein in the urine at routine antenatal checks. As it progresses, warning symptoms can include a severe headache that does not ease, vision problems such as blurring or seeing flashing lights, pain just below the ribs, vomiting, and sudden swelling of the face, hands, or feet. Anyone with these symptoms should seek medical advice straight away, because pre-eclampsia can worsen quickly and lead to serious complications for both mother and baby, including eclampsia (seizures). Pre-eclampsia can also develop or worsen in the days after birth, so new severe headaches or swelling after delivery should be reported too. The only cure is the birth of the baby, so people with pre-eclampsia are monitored closely and may need to give birth early. Regular antenatal appointments are the main way it is detected early.

For people at increased risk of pre-eclampsia, taking a low daily dose of aspirin can help lower that risk. Low-dose aspirin, often about 81 milligrams a day, is usually started between 12 and 28 weeks of pregnancy (ideally before 16 weeks) and continued until birth, only on the advice of a healthcare provider. It is generally recommended for people with high-risk factors such as a previous pregnancy with pre-eclampsia, chronic high blood pressure, kidney disease, diabetes, or an autoimmune condition, and may be offered when several moderate-risk factors are present, such as a first pregnancy, older maternal age, obesity, or a family history of pre-eclampsia. Aspirin is thought to help by improving blood flow through the placenta. It should not be started without medical guidance, because the right dose and timing matter and aspirin is not suitable for everyone. Anyone unsure whether they are at higher risk should ask their midwife or doctor to review their history early in pregnancy so preventive steps can begin at the best time.

Eclampsia is the onset of seizures (fits) in a pregnant person who has pre-eclampsia, and it is a rare but serious emergency. Pre-eclampsia involves high blood pressure and signs of organ strain, and when it becomes severe it can affect the brain and trigger convulsions. Warning symptoms that may come before a seizure include a severe or persistent headache, changes in vision such as blurring or flashing lights, severe pain in the upper abdomen, and increasing swelling. During an eclamptic seizure the person may lose consciousness and have jerking movements of the body. Eclampsia needs immediate hospital treatment; if a pregnant person has a seizure, call emergency services at once. Treatment focuses on stopping and preventing further seizures, usually with a medicine called magnesium sulfate, controlling blood pressure, and delivering the baby once the mother is stable, because birth is the definitive treatment. Good antenatal care that detects and manages pre-eclampsia early is the best way to prevent eclampsia. Most people who receive prompt treatment recover well after delivery.

HELLP syndrome is a rare but serious complication of pregnancy that is considered a severe form of pre-eclampsia. The name stands for its three main features: haemolysis (the breakdown of red blood cells), elevated liver enzymes (a sign the liver is under strain), and low platelet count (which affects the blood's ability to clot). It usually develops in the last third of pregnancy but can appear in the days after birth. Symptoms can include pain in the upper right part of the abdomen or below the ribs, nausea or vomiting, a general feeling of being unwell, headache, and sometimes shoulder pain or bleeding that is hard to stop. Because these symptoms can seem like a stomach upset or ordinary pregnancy discomfort, HELLP can be missed, so any severe upper abdominal pain in pregnancy should be checked urgently. HELLP syndrome is diagnosed with blood tests and needs immediate hospital care. The main treatment is to deliver the baby, along with measures to control blood pressure and support the mother. Prompt treatment greatly improves outcomes.

Placental abruption is when the placenta partly or completely separates from the wall of the uterus before the baby is born. The placenta supplies the baby with oxygen and nutrients, so an abruption can reduce that supply and cause bleeding, making it a serious emergency. It happens in roughly 1 in 100 pregnancies, usually in the third trimester. Common signs are vaginal bleeding, sudden or constant abdominal or back pain, frequent contractions, and a uterus that feels tense or hard. Sometimes blood is trapped behind the placenta, so pain can be severe even when visible bleeding is light. Risk factors include high blood pressure or pre-eclampsia, smoking or drug use, abdominal injury, carrying twins or more, and a previous abruption. Treatment depends on how severe the separation is and how far along the pregnancy is; a small abruption late in pregnancy may be managed by close monitoring or planned birth, while heavy bleeding usually needs urgent delivery, often by cesarean. Anyone with these symptoms should get emergency care right away.

Placental abruption, where the placenta separates from the uterus early, is an emergency that needs immediate care. Seek help straight away if you have vaginal bleeding along with abdominal or back pain, especially if the pain is sudden and constant rather than coming and going like normal contractions. Other warning signs include very frequent contractions, a belly that feels hard or tender, and reduced movements from the baby. Because blood can sometimes be trapped behind the placenta, severe abdominal pain may occur with little or no visible bleeding, so do not wait for heavy bleeding before getting help. Feeling faint, dizzy, or having a racing heartbeat can be signs of significant blood loss and are also reasons for emergency care. Call emergency services or go to the nearest maternity unit or emergency department without delay. Quick assessment protects both mother and baby, because reduced blood flow through the placenta can affect the baby's oxygen supply. Any heavy vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy should always be treated as an emergency.

Placenta previa is when the placenta lies low in the uterus and partly or completely covers the cervix, the opening at the neck of the womb. Because the baby normally passes through the cervix during birth, a placenta covering it can cause heavy bleeding and usually means a cesarean delivery is needed. Placenta previa affects about 1 in 200 pregnancies in the third trimester. The main sign is painless, bright red vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy, though some people have no bleeding and it is found on a routine ultrasound. Many low-lying placentas found early in pregnancy move upward as the uterus grows, so an early finding is often rechecked with a later scan around 32 weeks. Risk factors include previous cesarean births, previous placenta previa, carrying twins or more, and older maternal age. Management includes avoiding activities that could trigger bleeding, monitoring, and planning a cesarean birth. Any vaginal bleeding with a known low-lying placenta should be reported urgently, and heavy bleeding needs emergency care.

Prelabour rupture of membranes, sometimes called PROM, is when the fluid-filled sac (the amniotic sac) around the baby breaks before labour begins. At term, meaning 37 weeks or later, this often means labour will start on its own soon afterward. The main sign is fluid leaking from the vagina, which may be a sudden gush or a slow trickle, and the fluid is usually clear or pale and does not smell like urine. Once the waters have broken, there is a higher chance of infection reaching the baby, so it is important to contact a midwife or maternity unit promptly for advice. Healthcare providers will check how far along the pregnancy is, whether the fluid is clear, and whether there are signs of infection such as fever. If labour does not begin within a certain time, induction of labour may be recommended to reduce infection risk. People are usually advised not to put anything into the vagina and to watch for a change in fluid colour, a temperature, or reduced baby movements, which should be reported straight away.

Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes, known as PPROM, is when the waters break before 37 weeks of pregnancy, ahead of labour starting. It is more serious than waters breaking at term because the baby would be born prematurely and because there is a risk of infection, cord problems, and early labour. The main sign is fluid leaking from the vagina, either as a gush or a steady trickle. Anyone who thinks their waters have broken before 37 weeks should contact their maternity unit immediately. Management depends on how many weeks along the pregnancy is. Between about 34 and 37 weeks, planned early birth may be advised. Before 34 weeks, the aim is often to prolong the pregnancy safely while watching closely for infection; this can include a hospital stay, antibiotics to reduce infection risk, and steroid injections to help the baby's lungs mature. Signs of infection, such as fever, a fast heartbeat, tummy pain, or discoloured or smelly fluid, need urgent review. Reduced baby movements should also be reported straight away.

Premature or preterm labour is labour that starts before 37 weeks of pregnancy. Babies born early may need extra care because their lungs and other organs are still maturing, so recognising the signs early allows treatment that can help. Signs can include regular contractions or tightenings, period-like cramps, a low dull backache, a feeling of pressure in the pelvis, and fluid or a change in discharge leaking from the vagina. Anyone with these signs before 37 weeks should contact their midwife or maternity unit straight away. If preterm labour is confirmed or likely, hospital teams may offer treatments to improve the baby's outcome, such as steroid injections to help the baby's lungs develop, magnesium sulfate to help protect the baby's brain, and sometimes medicines to slow contractions for a short time so steroids can work or transfer to a specialist unit can be arranged. Not every episode of tightening means labour is starting, but it is always safer to be checked. Prompt assessment gives the baby the best possible start if an early birth cannot be prevented.

Contact your midwife or maternity unit right away if you have signs of labour before 37 weeks of pregnancy, because early treatment can protect your baby. Warning signs of premature labour include regular contractions or tightenings that do not go away, period-type cramping, persistent low backache, a feeling of increasing pressure in your pelvis or vagina, and any leaking of fluid, which may mean your waters have broken. A change in vaginal discharge, or discharge that becomes watery, mucous-like, or bloodstained, can also be a sign. Do not wait to see if the symptoms settle; it is safer to be assessed. If your waters break before 37 weeks, phone your maternity unit immediately rather than waiting for contractions. Also seek urgent help if you notice your baby moving less than usual. Early assessment lets the team decide whether to give treatments such as steroids for the baby's lungs or medicines to delay birth. Even if it turns out not to be labour, being checked provides reassurance and ensures nothing serious is missed.

Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) means heavy bleeding after the birth of a baby. It is one of the more common serious complications of childbirth and is divided into two types. Primary PPH is heavy bleeding within the first 24 hours after birth, most often caused by the uterus not contracting firmly enough after the placenta is delivered. Secondary PPH is abnormal or heavy bleeding between 24 hours and 12 weeks after birth, which can be linked to a piece of placenta left behind or to infection. Risk factors include a long labour, an assisted or cesarean birth, carrying twins or more, a large baby, and having had heavy bleeding before. To lower the risk, an injection that helps the uterus contract is usually offered as the placenta is delivered. Treatment for PPH depends on the cause and may include medicines to firm up the uterus, measures to help the womb contract, fluids or a blood transfusion, and sometimes a procedure or surgery. Prompt treatment is important, so any heavy bleeding after birth should be reported immediately.
source_name: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists

text: Some vaginal bleeding after birth is normal and is called lochia, which gradually lessens and changes colour over several weeks. However, heavy bleeding after birth, known as postpartum haemorrhage, can be dangerous and needs urgent care. Seek emergency help if you are soaking through more than one pad an hour, passing large clots (bigger than a golf ball), bleeding that suddenly becomes heavier rather than lighter, or if you feel faint, dizzy, short of breath, or notice a racing heartbeat, as these can be signs of significant blood loss. In the weeks after birth, also contact a healthcare provider promptly if bleeding smells unpleasant or is bright red and heavy again after settling, or if you develop a fever or abdominal pain, because these may signal secondary bleeding from retained placental tissue or infection. If bleeding is severe or you feel very unwell, call emergency services immediately. Quick treatment can stop the bleeding and prevent complications. If you are unsure whether your bleeding is normal, it is always safest to ask your midwife or doctor.
source_name: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists

text: Feeling your baby move is a sign that they are well, and a change in the usual pattern can be an important warning sign. Most people begin to feel movements between 16 and 24 weeks. Rather than counting to a set number of kicks, get to know your baby's normal pattern of movements. If you think your baby is moving less than usual, or if the pattern has changed, contact your midwife or maternity unit straight away, at any time of day or night. Do not wait until the next day. It is a myth that babies move less toward the end of pregnancy; you should continue to feel your baby move right up to and during labour. Do not rely on home handheld heartbeat monitors (dopplers) to check on your baby, because hearing a heartbeat does not confirm your baby is well and can give false reassurance. If movements are reduced, the maternity team will check your baby, often with monitoring of the heartbeat and sometimes a scan. Reporting reduced movements promptly can help detect and manage problems early.

Vaginal bleeding during pregnancy can range from light spotting to heavy bleeding, and it should always be checked, though it does not always mean something is seriously wrong. In early pregnancy, light spotting can happen as the embryo implants and may be harmless, but bleeding can also be a sign of miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy. Later in pregnancy, bleeding may come from the cervix or, less commonly, from a problem with the placenta. Contact your midwife, maternity unit, or a doctor promptly about any bleeding. Seek emergency care if the bleeding is heavy, if you pass clots, or if bleeding comes with severe abdominal pain, shoulder-tip pain, dizziness, or fainting, as these may point to an ectopic pregnancy or placental problem. In the second half of pregnancy, treat heavy bleeding as an emergency and go to hospital. Note the amount and colour of the bleeding and any pain to help the team assess you. Depending on how far along you are, they may arrange an examination, a scan, or blood tests to find the cause and plan care.

Group B Streptococcus (GBS) is a common type of bacteria that many healthy adults carry in the gut and genital area without any symptoms. In most people it causes no harm, but if it is present during birth it can occasionally pass to the baby and cause a serious infection in the first days or weeks of life. Because of this, pregnant people may be screened for GBS, often with a swab late in pregnancy, or managed based on risk factors, depending on local guidelines. If GBS is found or the risk is high, antibiotics given through a vein during labour greatly reduce the chance of the baby becoming infected. Signs that a newborn may have a GBS infection include being unusually sleepy or floppy, poor feeding, a very high or low temperature, fast or difficult breathing, and irritability; these need urgent medical attention. Carrying GBS does not mean a person is unclean or unwell, and it usually needs no treatment during pregnancy itself. The key protection is antibiotics at the right time during labour when they are indicated.
source_name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

text: Stillbirth is the loss of a baby before or during birth after 24 weeks of pregnancy. While not every stillbirth can be prevented, several steps can lower the risk. Do not smoke, and avoid alcohol and drugs in pregnancy, as these increase the risk. Attend all your antenatal appointments so that growth and blood pressure can be monitored and problems found early. Get to know your baby's usual pattern of movements and report any reduction or change straight away, at any hour. From 28 weeks, go to sleep on your side rather than your back, because back-sleeping in later pregnancy is linked to a higher risk; if you wake on your back, simply settle back onto your side. Take folic acid as advised and eat a healthy, balanced diet, and try to reach and stay at a healthy weight. Report any vaginal bleeding, severe itching, or symptoms of pre-eclampsia such as severe headache, vision changes, or sudden swelling. Following these steps, along with regular maternity care, gives your baby the best chance of a safe arrival.

Rhesus disease, also called haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn, can happen when a pregnant person has rhesus-negative (RhD negative) blood and their baby has rhesus-positive (RhD positive) blood inherited from the father. If the mother's immune system is exposed to the baby's RhD positive blood, it can make antibodies that attack the baby's red blood cells, usually affecting a future pregnancy rather than the first. Everyone is offered blood tests early in pregnancy, around 8 to 12 weeks, to check their blood group and RhD status. To prevent the problem, RhD negative people are offered injections of a medicine called anti-D immunoglobulin during pregnancy and after birth if the baby is RhD positive, which stops harmful antibodies from forming. Anti-D is also given after events that could mix the blood, such as bleeding, a fall, or certain procedures. When rhesus disease does affect a baby, it can cause anaemia and jaundice, and it is monitored and treated with measures such as light therapy, and in severe cases a blood transfusion before or after birth.

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy, also known as obstetric cholestasis, is a liver condition that causes intense itching, usually without a rash. It tends to appear in the later part of pregnancy. The itching is often worse at night and is frequently felt on the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet, though it can affect the whole body. Mild itching from stretching skin is common and usually harmless, but severe or persistent itching, especially on the palms and soles, should always be reported so it can be checked with blood tests for liver function and bile acids. Cholestasis matters because it is linked to a higher risk of complications for the baby, including preterm birth and, in severe cases, stillbirth, so people with the condition are monitored more closely. Management may include regular blood tests, closer monitoring of the baby, and sometimes planned early birth depending on how high the bile acid levels are. The itching usually settles within a few days after the baby is born. Anyone with troublesome itching in pregnancy should contact their midwife or doctor.

A severe headache in pregnancy, particularly one that does not go away with rest or ordinary pain relief, can be a warning sign of pre-eclampsia and should be checked promptly. Pre-eclampsia usually develops after 20 weeks and involves high blood pressure and strain on organs such as the kidneys and liver. A concerning headache may come with other symptoms such as changes in vision (blurring, spots, or flashing lights), pain just below the ribs, nausea or vomiting, and sudden swelling of the face, hands, or feet. If you have a bad headache together with any of these symptoms, contact your midwife or maternity unit straight away, or seek emergency care, because pre-eclampsia can worsen quickly. Headaches can also occur or worsen in the days after birth, so new severe headaches after delivery should be reported too. Not every headache is serious, and many are due to tension, dehydration, or tiredness, but a severe or persistent headache is worth checking because early detection of pre-eclampsia allows monitoring and treatment that protect both mother and baby.

Sudden changes in your vision during pregnancy can be a warning sign of pre-eclampsia and need urgent assessment. These changes can include blurred vision, seeing flashing lights or spots, sensitivity to light, or temporary loss of vision. Pre-eclampsia is a condition of high blood pressure and organ strain that usually appears after 20 weeks, and visual symptoms suggest it may be becoming severe. Vision changes often occur alongside other warning signs such as a severe headache, pain below the ribs, vomiting, or sudden swelling of the face and hands. If you notice new visual disturbances, especially with any of these other symptoms, contact your maternity unit immediately or seek emergency care. Because severe pre-eclampsia can progress to seizures (eclampsia), it should not be ignored. These symptoms can also happen in the days after giving birth. While occasional blurring can have minor causes, new or sudden vision changes in pregnancy are best treated as urgent until pre-eclampsia has been ruled out with a blood pressure check and a urine test for protein.

Your waters breaking means the amniotic sac around your baby has ruptured, releasing amniotic fluid. It can feel like a sudden gush or a slow, uncontrollable trickle of fluid from the vagina, and the fluid is usually clear or pale yellow and does not smell like urine. If you think your waters have broken, contact your midwife or maternity unit for advice, noting the time it happened and the colour of the fluid. Seek urgent care if the fluid is green, brown, or bloodstained, if it smells unpleasant, if the waters break before 37 weeks, or if you feel your baby moving less, because these can signal a problem for the baby. Also call urgently if you feel something in the vagina or see the umbilical cord, which is a rare emergency. After the waters break, there is a higher chance of infection, so it is important to be assessed and to follow advice about when to come in. Labour often begins within a day at term; if it does not, induction may be recommended to reduce the risk of infection.

Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy, often called morning sickness, are very common in the first trimester, though they can happen at any time of day. The exact cause is not fully known, but it is thought to be linked to the hormonal changes of early pregnancy, especially the rise in the pregnancy hormone hCG. Nausea usually starts before 9 weeks and, for most people, eases by around weeks 16 to 20. Some people feel queasy at the sight or smell of certain foods, when their stomach is empty, or when they are tired. Having more than one baby can make symptoms stronger. Although unpleasant, mild to moderate morning sickness does not harm the baby and is often considered a normal part of early pregnancy. It is different from hyperemesis gravidarum, a much more severe form with persistent vomiting that can lead to dehydration and needs medical treatment. If nausea is mild, simple self-help measures often help. If you cannot keep food or fluids down, are losing weight, or feel very unwell, contact your midwife or doctor for advice.

If you have mild morning sickness, several self-help steps can ease nausea and vomiting in pregnancy. Try to get plenty of rest, as tiredness can make nausea worse. Eat small, frequent meals rather than large ones, and choose plain, starchy foods such as toast, crackers, or plain biscuits. Many people find it helps to eat something dry, like a plain biscuit, before getting up in the morning. Avoid foods or smells that trigger your nausea, and eat cold meals if the smell of hot food is off-putting. Sip fluids often, taking small amounts frequently rather than large drinks, and try ginger, which some find soothing. Drinks that are cold, fizzy, or slightly sharp may be easier to manage. Ask for support at home so you can rest when you feel sick. If these measures are not enough and vomiting is affecting your daily life, or you cannot keep fluids down, see your midwife or doctor, as safe anti-sickness medicines can be prescribed in pregnancy. Persistent, severe vomiting needs medical assessment to prevent dehydration.

Most morning sickness is mild and manageable at home, but sometimes nausea and vomiting in pregnancy need medical help. Contact your midwife or doctor if you are vomiting many times a day and cannot keep any food or fluids down, if you have very dark urine or have not passed urine for more than eight hours, or if you feel weak, dizzy, or faint when standing. Also seek advice if you have lost weight, have tummy pain, a high temperature, or vomit blood. These can be signs of hyperemesis gravidarum, a severe form of pregnancy sickness, or of dehydration, both of which need treatment. Being unable to stay hydrated is the main concern, because it can affect your health and, if untreated, the pregnancy. Treatment may include anti-sickness medicines that are safe in pregnancy and, in more severe cases, fluids given through a vein in hospital. Do not simply endure severe or constant vomiting; effective and safe treatments are available, and getting help early can prevent you from becoming seriously unwell.

Hyperemesis gravidarum is a condition of severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy that is much worse than ordinary morning sickness. It affects a small number of pregnant people and can lead to dehydration, weight loss, and difficulty carrying out everyday activities. Signs include vomiting many times a day, being unable to keep food or fluids down, feeling constantly nauseous, and becoming dehydrated, with symptoms such as dark urine, dizziness, and a dry mouth. Unlike typical morning sickness, hyperemesis often does not settle by the second trimester and usually needs medical treatment. It is important to get help, because untreated dehydration and poor nutrition can affect your wellbeing. Treatment may include anti-sickness medicines that are safe to use in pregnancy, vitamins such as thiamine, and, if dehydration is significant, admission to hospital for fluids given through a vein. Many people also benefit from practical and emotional support, as the condition can be exhausting and low mood is common. If you have severe or persistent vomiting in pregnancy, contact your midwife or doctor promptly so treatment can begin.

Heartburn and indigestion are common in pregnancy, especially in the later months. Heartburn is a burning feeling in the chest, and indigestion can bring bloating, a feeling of fullness, belching, or bringing up food. Two things make these symptoms more likely in pregnancy. Hormonal changes relax the ring of muscle between the stomach and the food pipe, which lets stomach acid rise more easily. Later on, the growing uterus presses upward on the stomach, adding to the effect. Symptoms often come after eating, when bending over, or when lying down. Certain triggers, such as large meals, rich, spicy, or fatty foods, caffeine, and fizzy drinks, can make heartburn worse. Although uncomfortable, heartburn in pregnancy is usually harmless and tends to ease after the baby is born. However, severe upper abdominal pain, particularly on the right side or below the ribs, can occasionally signal a more serious problem such as pre-eclampsia and should be checked. Simple changes to eating habits and posture help many people, and safe medicines are available if symptoms are troublesome.

You can ease heartburn and indigestion in pregnancy with some simple changes. Eat smaller meals more often rather than large meals, and eat slowly. Try not to eat within about three hours of going to bed, and avoid foods and drinks that trigger your symptoms, such as rich, spicy, or fatty foods, fruit juice, caffeine, and fizzy drinks. Sit up straight when you eat to take pressure off your stomach, and avoid lying down or bending over soon after a meal. Propping yourself up with pillows when sleeping, so your head and shoulders are higher than your stomach, can stop acid rising at night. Cutting down on caffeine and not smoking also help. If these steps are not enough, a pharmacist, midwife, or doctor can recommend medicines that are safe in pregnancy, such as antacids to neutralise acid or alginates to protect the food pipe. Always check before taking any medicine in pregnancy. See your doctor if heartburn is severe, if you also have pain below your ribs, or if simple measures and pharmacy remedies do not bring relief.

Constipation is common in pregnancy, partly because hormonal changes slow the movement of the bowel and partly because iron supplements can make stools harder. It can cause infrequent, hard, or difficult-to-pass stools and a bloated, uncomfortable feeling. Several everyday steps can help. Eat foods that are high in fibre, such as wholemeal bread and cereals, fruit and vegetables, and pulses like beans and lentils. Drink plenty of water throughout the day to keep stools soft. Staying physically active with gentle exercise, such as walking, also helps keep the bowels moving. If you take iron supplements and constipation is a problem, tell your midwife or doctor, who may be able to review the type or dose. If diet and lifestyle changes are not enough, a pharmacist or doctor can advise on a laxative that is safe to use in pregnancy; do not use over-the-counter laxatives without checking first. Constipation itself is uncomfortable rather than dangerous, but see your provider if it is severe, painful, or comes with bleeding, so the cause can be checked.

Feeling very tired is common in pregnancy, particularly in the first 12 weeks and again toward the end. Early tiredness is linked to the hormonal changes of pregnancy, especially rising progesterone, and to the extra work your body is doing as the baby develops. Later in pregnancy, disrupted sleep, the weight of the growing baby, and needing to pass urine at night can leave you feeling exhausted. The best way to cope is to rest when you can: go to bed earlier, take short naps if possible, and accept help with tasks at home and work. Gentle, regular activity such as walking can actually boost energy, and eating a healthy, balanced diet with regular meals helps keep energy steady. Because extreme tiredness can sometimes be a sign of iron-deficiency anaemia, it is worth mentioning ongoing exhaustion to your midwife, who may check your blood. If tiredness is severe, comes with breathlessness, palpitations, or dizziness, or does not improve with rest, speak to your midwife or doctor so any underlying cause can be found and treated.

Back pain is very common in pregnancy as the growing bump changes your posture and pregnancy hormones soften the ligaments that support your joints. It is often felt in the lower back and may be worse after standing or sitting for a long time. Several measures can ease and prevent it. Bend your knees and keep your back straight when lifting, and avoid lifting heavy objects. Move your feet to turn rather than twisting your spine, and wear flat shoes that spread your weight evenly. Keep your back straight and supported when sitting, and try to balance the weight you carry between two bags. Rest when you can, and consider a firm mattress or extra support. Gentle exercise, such as walking or swimming, and pregnancy-safe stretches or yoga can strengthen the muscles that support your back. A warm bath or a supportive pregnancy pillow at night may help. If the pain is severe, does not improve, or is felt high up with other symptoms, tell your midwife. Back pain with a fever, or pain when passing urine, needs checking, as it may signal an infection.

Pelvic girdle pain (PGP), sometimes called symphysis pubis dysfunction (SPD), is pain in the joints of the pelvis during pregnancy. It can be felt at the front over the pubic bone, across the lower back, between the vagina and anus, or spreading to the thighs. Many people find it hurts to walk, climb stairs, turn over in bed, or stand on one leg to get dressed, and some notice a grinding or clicking sensation. PGP is caused by the pelvic joints moving unevenly as pregnancy hormones loosen the ligaments and the joints become less stable. It is common and, while distressing, is not harmful to the baby. Early treatment from a physiotherapist can help, so ask your midwife for a referral to a physiotherapist who specialises in pelvic joint problems. Treatment may include gentle exercises to strengthen the pelvic and tummy muscles, advice on posture and daily movements, and sometimes a support belt. Keeping active within your comfort, avoiding movements that make the pain worse, and moving your knees together when getting in and out of a car can all reduce strain on the joints.

Round ligament pain is a common cause of lower abdominal or groin discomfort in pregnancy, usually in the second trimester. The round ligaments support the uterus, and as the uterus grows these ligaments stretch and thicken. Sudden movements can pull on them and cause a brief, sharp, or stabbing pain, or a dull ache, typically on one or both sides of the lower belly or groin. The pain often happens when you roll over in bed, stand up quickly, cough, sneeze, or laugh, and it usually lasts only a few seconds. Although it can be startling, round ligament pain is a normal part of pregnancy and is not harmful. You can ease it by moving more slowly when changing position, bending and flexing your hips before you cough or sneeze, and resting or gently supporting your belly. A warm bath may also help. Because lower abdominal pain can occasionally have other causes, contact your provider if the pain is severe, constant, or comes with fever, chills, bleeding, heavy discharge, contractions, or pain when passing urine, so a more serious problem can be ruled out.

Leg cramps are sudden, sharp pains, usually in the calf muscles, that are common in pregnancy and often happen at night, especially in the second and third trimesters. The exact reason is not fully understood. Although painful, they are not usually a sign of anything serious. Regular gentle exercise, particularly ankle and leg movements, can help prevent cramps. Try bending and stretching your foot up and down vigorously several times, and rotating your ankles, and repeat these foot exercises during the day and before bed. When a cramp strikes, it often helps to pull your toes up toward your ankle or to rub the muscle firmly, and then to walk around for a few minutes. Staying well hydrated may also help. See your midwife or doctor if the pain does not ease, if the cramps are very frequent, or, importantly, if you have pain, swelling, redness, or warmth in one leg (usually the calf), because that could be a sign of a blood clot (deep vein thrombosis), which needs urgent assessment. A one-sided, tender, swollen calf is not an ordinary cramp.

Swelling in the feet, ankles, and fingers, called oedema, is common in pregnancy, especially in the later months and toward the end of the day. It happens because your body holds more water and because the growing uterus can slow the return of blood from your legs. Gradual swelling that builds up over the day is usually normal. You can ease it by avoiding standing for long periods, sitting with your feet raised when you can, wearing comfortable shoes, and doing gentle foot exercises to keep the circulation moving. Drinking plenty of water and staying active also help. However, swelling can sometimes be a warning sign. Contact your maternity unit straight away if swelling comes on suddenly, or is severe, or affects your face and hands, or if it comes with a headache, vision problems, or pain below the ribs, because these can be signs of pre-eclampsia. Also seek urgent advice if one leg is much more swollen than the other and is painful, red, or warm, as this could indicate a blood clot. When in doubt, have any sudden or one-sided swelling checked.

Braxton Hicks contractions are sometimes called practice contractions. They are a tightening of the muscles of the uterus that many people feel from the middle of pregnancy onward, as the body prepares for labour. During a Braxton Hicks contraction the belly may feel hard for a short time and then relax. Unlike true labour contractions, Braxton Hicks are usually irregular, do not get closer together over time, do not grow steadily longer or stronger, and often ease off if you change position, rest, or drink some water. They can be uncomfortable but are not usually painful and do not mean labour has started. Real labour contractions become regular, last longer, come more frequently, and increase in intensity, and they do not stop when you move around. If you are unsure whether what you are feeling is labour, especially before 37 weeks, contact your midwife or maternity unit for advice. Also seek help if tightenings come with vaginal bleeding, leaking fluid, or reduced baby movements. Staying hydrated and resting can help settle Braxton Hicks contractions when they occur.

Piles, also called haemorrhoids, are swollen blood vessels in and around the back passage (anus). They are common in pregnancy because pregnancy hormones relax the blood vessel walls, the growing uterus increases pressure, and constipation and straining make them more likely. Symptoms can include itching, aching, or soreness around the anus, a lump you can feel, discomfort after passing a stool, and sometimes bright red blood on the toilet paper. Piles are uncomfortable but usually not serious and often improve after the baby is born. The most helpful step is to avoid constipation and straining: eat plenty of fibre from fruit, vegetables, and wholegrains, drink lots of water, and stay active. Do not delay going to the toilet when you need to. To ease symptoms, keep the area clean, apply a cold pack, and avoid sitting for long periods. A pharmacist can suggest creams or ointments that are safe in pregnancy to relieve pain and itching. See your doctor if the pain is severe, if bleeding continues, or if the piles do not improve, so other causes of bleeding can be ruled out.

Headaches are common, particularly in the first trimester, and are usually not a cause for concern. They may be linked to hormonal changes, tiredness, stress, hunger, dehydration, or caffeine withdrawal. You can often ease and prevent headaches without medicine by drinking plenty of fluids, eating regular meals, getting enough rest and good sleep, and reducing stress where you can. Gentle exercise and fresh air may help, and keeping to a routine with regular sleep and meals can reduce how often headaches occur. If you do need pain relief, paracetamol is generally considered safe to use in pregnancy at the recommended dose, but it is best to take the lowest dose for the shortest time and to check with a pharmacist, midwife, or doctor first. Avoid ibuprofen and other anti-inflammatory painkillers in pregnancy unless a doctor advises them. Importantly, a severe or persistent headache after 20 weeks, especially with vision changes, pain below the ribs, or sudden swelling, can be a sign of pre-eclampsia and should be checked urgently. Contact your maternity unit straight away if you have these symptoms.

Feeling faint or dizzy is fairly common in pregnancy. It can happen because hormonal changes and the growing uterus affect your circulation and blood pressure, meaning less blood can reach your brain at times. You are more likely to feel faint if you stand up too quickly, stand for a long time, lie on your back later in pregnancy, get too hot, or have not eaten enough. To reduce dizziness, get up slowly from sitting or lying down, and if you feel faint while standing, find a seat quickly and the feeling should pass; if it does not, lie down on your side. Avoid lying flat on your back in later pregnancy, as this can press on a large blood vessel; lie on your side instead. Eat regularly to keep your blood sugar steady, and drink enough fluids. Try not to get overheated, and wear loose, comfortable clothing. While occasional faintness is usually harmless, tell your midwife if you feel dizzy often, as it can sometimes be linked to anaemia or low blood sugar. Seek urgent help if fainting comes with bleeding, abdominal pain, or a racing heartbeat.

Feeling short of breath is common in pregnancy, especially later on, and is usually a normal result of the changes your body is going through. Early in pregnancy, hormonal changes increase your breathing so that more oxygen reaches the baby, which can make you more aware of your breathing. Later, the growing uterus pushes up against the diaphragm, the muscle used for breathing, leaving less room for the lungs to expand and making you breathless with activity or when lying flat. You can ease mild breathlessness by resting, changing position, sitting or standing upright to give your lungs more room, moving more slowly, and using extra pillows to prop yourself up in bed. Gentle regular activity also helps your overall fitness. However, breathlessness can occasionally point to a problem such as anaemia or, rarely, a blood clot. Contact your midwife or doctor if the breathlessness is severe or comes on suddenly, if you have chest pain, a fast heartbeat, a cough, or you feel breathless even at rest, or if your lips or fingers look blue. Sudden severe breathlessness needs emergency care.

Folic acid, the manmade form of the B vitamin folate, is one of the most important supplements in early pregnancy. Taking folic acid helps the baby's neural tube, which becomes the brain and spinal cord, to form and close properly, greatly lowering the risk of neural tube defects such as spina bifida. Because the neural tube develops in the first weeks, ideally you should take folic acid before conception and continue until you are 12 weeks pregnant. The usual recommended dose is 400 micrograms a day. Some people need a higher dose of 5 milligrams a day, prescribed by a doctor, for example if they or their partner have a neural tube defect, have had a previous pregnancy affected by one, have diabetes, have a raised body weight, or take certain medicines such as some for epilepsy. Even with a healthy diet it is hard to get enough folate from food alone, which is why a supplement is advised. If you did not take folic acid before becoming pregnant, start as soon as you find out and continue to 12 weeks. Ask your midwife or doctor if you are unsure which dose is right for you.

As well as taking a folic acid supplement in early pregnancy, you can include foods that contain folate, the natural form of the vitamin, as part of a healthy diet. Good sources include green leafy vegetables such as spinach, kale, and broccoli, as well as peas, chickpeas, beans, and lentils. Some breakfast cereals and fat spreads are fortified with folic acid, which the label will show. Other sources include Brussels sprouts, asparagus, oranges, and wholegrain foods. Folate can be lost when vegetables are overcooked or boiled in lots of water, so steaming, microwaving, or lightly cooking vegetables helps keep more of the vitamin. It is important to know that food alone usually cannot provide enough folate to give the same protection as a supplement in early pregnancy, so eating folate-rich foods should be in addition to, not instead of, taking 400 micrograms of folic acid a day until 12 weeks. Combining a supplement with a varied diet that includes these vegetables, pulses, and fortified foods supports the baby's early development and your own overall nutrition during pregnancy.

Iron is a mineral your body uses to make haemoglobin, the part of red blood cells that carries oxygen. During pregnancy your blood volume increases and your baby and placenta need iron too, so your requirement rises to about 27 milligrams a day. Getting enough iron helps prevent iron-deficiency anaemia, which can make you very tired, weak, and short of breath, and which in pregnancy is linked to a higher risk of premature birth and low birth weight. Iron also supports your baby's growth and brain development. Because needs are high, some people cannot meet them through diet alone and may be advised to take a supplement, especially if a blood test shows low iron. Your provider checks for anaemia during pregnancy and will recommend supplements if needed; do not start high-dose iron on your own, as too much can cause side effects such as constipation and stomach upset. Eating iron-rich foods and pairing them with vitamin C helps your body absorb iron, while tea, coffee, and calcium-rich foods eaten at the same time can reduce absorption. Discuss any tiredness or breathlessness with your midwife.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: You can help meet your higher iron needs in pregnancy by eating iron-rich foods. There are two types of dietary iron. Heme iron, which the body absorbs more easily, comes from animal foods such as lean red meat, poultry, and fish. Non-heme iron comes from plant foods and includes beans, lentils, tofu, dark green leafy vegetables like spinach, nuts and seeds, dried fruit such as apricots, and iron-fortified breads and breakfast cereals. To get the most from plant sources, eat them with a food rich in vitamin C, such as oranges, strawberries, tomatoes, or peppers, because vitamin C boosts the absorption of non-heme iron. On the other hand, tea, coffee, and calcium supplements or dairy eaten at the same meal can reduce how much iron you absorb, so it can help to have them between meals instead. A varied diet that includes these foods supports healthy red blood cells for you and your baby. If a blood test shows your iron is low despite a good diet, your provider may recommend an iron supplement to prevent or treat anaemia.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Calcium is a mineral needed to build your baby's bones and teeth, and it also supports the healthy working of your heart, muscles, and nerves. Adults, including most pregnant people, need about 1,000 milligrams of calcium a day, while pregnant teenagers need more, around 1,300 milligrams, because their own bones are still growing. If you do not get enough calcium, your body draws on the calcium stored in your bones to supply the baby. Good food sources include milk, yoghurt, and cheese, calcium-fortified plant milks and cereals, tinned fish with soft edible bones such as sardines, tofu set with calcium, and green vegetables such as kale and broccoli. In some situations, calcium supplements are recommended in pregnancy, and adequate calcium may help lower the risk of high blood pressure and pre-eclampsia in people at higher risk, so follow your provider's advice. Vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium, so the two work together. Aim to include calcium-rich foods across the day, and speak to your midwife or doctor before taking supplements, as very high intakes above the recommended upper limit are not advised.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium and is needed to keep bones, teeth, and muscles healthy, both for you and your developing baby. During pregnancy a daily intake of about 15 micrograms (600 international units) is recommended. Our bodies make vitamin D from sunlight on the skin, but many people do not get enough, particularly in winter, if they spend little time outdoors, cover their skin, or have darker skin. Because it is hard to get enough from food, a daily supplement is often advised throughout pregnancy. Food sources are limited but include oily fish such as salmon and sardines, eggs, and foods fortified with vitamin D such as some breakfast cereals and fat spreads. A supplement containing 10 micrograms a day is commonly recommended for pregnant and breastfeeding people. Taking vitamin D during pregnancy supports the baby's bone development and helps prevent low vitamin D levels in the newborn. Avoid very high doses, as too much vitamin D over a long period can be harmful; stay within the recommended amount unless a doctor advises otherwise. Ask your midwife which supplement and dose are right for you.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Vitamin B12 helps your body make red blood cells and DNA and supports the development of your baby's brain and nervous system. During pregnancy the recommended intake is about 2.6 micrograms a day. Vitamin B12 is found naturally in animal foods, including meat, fish, eggs, milk, cheese, and other dairy products, so most people who eat these regularly get enough. People who follow a vegan or strict vegetarian diet are at higher risk of B12 deficiency, because plant foods do not naturally contain it. If you avoid animal products, you can get B12 from fortified foods such as some plant milks, breakfast cereals, and nutritional yeast, or from a supplement; a reliable daily source is important in pregnancy. A deficiency can lead to a type of anaemia and, if severe, may affect the baby's development, so it is worth discussing your diet with your midwife or doctor, who can arrange a blood test if needed. Getting enough B12, along with folate, supports healthy blood and your baby's growing nervous system. If you are vegan or vegetarian, ask about supplements to make sure your intake is adequate.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Vitamin C, also called ascorbic acid, helps protect cells, supports the immune system, and is needed to make collagen, a protein important for healthy skin, blood vessels, and connective tissue. It also greatly improves the absorption of iron from plant foods, which is useful in pregnancy when iron needs are high. During pregnancy the recommended intake is about 85 milligrams a day for adults, and slightly less for teenagers. Vitamin C is found in many fruits and vegetables, including oranges and other citrus fruits, strawberries, kiwi, tomatoes, peppers, broccoli, and potatoes. Because the body does not store vitamin C and it is easily lost in cooking, it helps to eat some raw or lightly cooked fruit and vegetables each day. A balanced diet with plenty of produce usually provides enough, so routine high-dose supplements are not needed and very large amounts are not advised. A practical tip is to pair vitamin C-rich foods with iron-rich meals, for example having fruit or a salad alongside beans, lentils, or fortified cereal, to help your body absorb more iron.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Iodine is a mineral your body needs to make thyroid hormones, which regulate metabolism and are essential for your baby's brain and nervous system development. Iodine needs rise in pregnancy to about 220 micrograms a day, roughly 50 percent higher than usual, because the baby depends on your supply. Not getting enough iodine during pregnancy can affect the baby's growth and brain development, so a steady intake matters. Good food sources include dairy products such as milk and yoghurt, eggs, and fish and other seafood. Iodised salt is a source in some countries, though salt intake should still be moderate. People who avoid dairy and fish, including some vegans, may find it harder to get enough and should look at fortified foods or discuss supplements with their provider. Some prenatal supplements contain iodine; the American Thyroid Association has recommended that pregnant and breastfeeding people consider a supplement providing iodine. However, both too little and too much iodine can affect the thyroid, so it is best not to take high-dose iodine or kelp supplements without advice. Ask your midwife or doctor if you are unsure whether your diet provides enough.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Choline is a nutrient that supports your baby's brain and spinal cord development and helps with the formation of cell membranes. It works alongside folate, and getting enough during pregnancy is important for healthy growth. Pregnant people are advised to get about 450 milligrams of choline a day, yet many do not reach this amount, so pregnancy is considered a time when intake can fall short. The body makes some choline, but not enough to meet needs, so it is important to get it from food. Rich sources include eggs (especially the yolk), lean meat, poultry, fish, and dairy products, along with smaller amounts in beans, nuts, and cruciferous vegetables such as broccoli and Brussels sprouts. People who eat few or no animal products may find it harder to get enough choline and should pay attention to plant sources or discuss supplements with their provider, as many prenatal vitamins contain little or no choline. Including choline-rich foods such as eggs as part of a balanced diet is a simple way to support your baby's developing brain. Ask your midwife if you are concerned about your intake.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Omega-3 fatty acids, especially DHA (docosahexaenoic acid), support your baby's brain and eye development during pregnancy. The main dietary source is oily fish. Health authorities advise pregnant people to eat about 8 to 12 ounces (two to three servings) a week of fish that are lower in mercury to provide these beneficial fats. Good lower-mercury choices include salmon, sardines, herring, trout, and anchovies. At the same time, certain fish high in mercury should be avoided in pregnancy, such as shark, swordfish, king mackerel, and marlin, and oily fish should be limited to no more than a couple of portions a week. If you do not eat fish, you can get the plant omega-3 called ALA from walnuts, flaxseed, chia seeds, and rapeseed oil, though the body converts only a small amount of ALA into DHA. Some people choose a fish oil or algae-based DHA supplement; algae oil is a suitable option for vegetarians and vegans. Avoid supplements made from fish liver oil, such as cod liver oil, in pregnancy because they can contain high levels of vitamin A. Ask your provider before starting any supplement.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Protein is a building block your body uses to make new tissue, and during pregnancy it supports the growth of your baby, the placenta, and your own expanding blood supply and tissues. Including a source of protein at each meal helps your baby grow and can also help you feel full and keep energy steady. Good sources include lean meat and poultry, fish that is low in mercury, eggs, and dairy foods such as milk, yoghurt, and cheese. Plant sources are also excellent and include beans, lentils, chickpeas, tofu and other soya foods, nuts, seeds, and peanut butter. Combining different plant proteins across the day, such as beans with grains, helps provide the full range of amino acids, which is especially useful for vegetarians and vegans. Most people can meet their increased protein needs through a varied, balanced diet without special supplements. Simple ways to add protein include having eggs or yoghurt at breakfast, adding beans or lentils to soups and stews, and choosing nuts or hummus as snacks. If you follow a plant-based diet, pay attention to protein variety and speak to your provider if you have concerns.

Fibre is the part of plant foods that the body cannot fully digest, and it is helpful in pregnancy because it keeps the bowels moving and helps prevent constipation, which is common when pregnancy hormones slow digestion and when taking iron supplements. A high-fibre diet also supports steady energy and general gut health. Good sources of fibre include wholemeal bread, wholegrain cereals and oats, brown rice and wholewheat pasta, fruit and vegetables (with skins where suitable), and pulses such as beans, lentils, and chickpeas. Aim to include a variety of these foods across the day, for example wholegrain cereal at breakfast, fruit as snacks, and vegetables or beans with meals. It is important to drink plenty of fluids as you increase fibre, because water helps fibre do its job and keeps stools soft. Staying physically active with gentle exercise such as walking also helps the bowels move. Increasing fibre gradually can reduce bloating. If you are constipated despite a high-fibre diet and plenty of fluids, speak to your midwife, pharmacist, or doctor, who can advise on a laxative that is safe to use in pregnancy.

It is fine to have some caffeine in pregnancy, but high levels are best avoided because too much has been linked to low birth weight and, in some studies, a higher risk of miscarriage. The advice is to limit caffeine to no more than 200 milligrams a day. To picture that amount, a mug of instant coffee has about 100 milligrams, a mug of filter coffee about 140 milligrams, a mug of tea about 75 milligrams, a can of cola about 40 milligrams, and a 50 gram bar of plain chocolate up to about 25 milligrams. Caffeine is also found in energy drinks, which can be high, and in some cold and flu remedies, so check labels. You do not need to cut out caffeine completely; keeping under the daily limit is what matters. If you would rather avoid it, decaffeinated tea and coffee, fruit juice diluted with water, and plain water are good choices. Spreading drinks through the day and keeping track of hidden sources such as chocolate and soft drinks makes it easier to stay within the recommended amount. If you are unsure, ask your midwife for guidance.

The safest approach is to not drink any alcohol during pregnancy, because no level has been proven safe. Alcohol passes across the placenta to the baby, whose liver is not developed enough to process it. Drinking in pregnancy can increase the risk of miscarriage, premature birth, and low birth weight, and regular or heavy drinking can cause fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD), a range of lasting problems that can affect a child's learning, behaviour, growth, and physical development. The risk is greater the more you drink, and drinking in the first three months may particularly increase the chance of miscarriage. Because of this uncertainty, official guidance is to avoid alcohol entirely if you are pregnant or trying to conceive. If you drank small amounts before you knew you were pregnant, the risk to the baby is likely to be low, but it is best to stop once you know. If you are finding it hard to stop drinking, this is important to discuss, and your midwife or doctor can offer support and referral to services without judgement. Choosing alcohol-free drinks is the safest option throughout pregnancy.

Listeria is a type of bacteria that can cause an infection called listeriosis. Pregnant people are far more likely than others to get listeriosis, and although it may cause only mild flu-like illness in the mother, it can lead to miscarriage, stillbirth, premature birth, or serious illness in the newborn, so avoiding it is important. Listeria can grow even in the fridge, so certain higher-risk foods are best avoided in pregnancy. These include unpasteurised (raw) milk and soft cheeses made from it, soft mould-ripened cheeses such as brie and camembert and soft blue cheeses, refrigerated pâté and meat spreads, refrigerated smoked seafood, pre-prepared or store-bought salads such as coleslaw, and hot dogs or deli/luncheon meats unless they are heated until steaming hot. Chill foods promptly, keep your fridge cold, wash fruits and vegetables well, and keep raw and ready-to-eat foods separate. Reheat leftovers and ready meals until piping hot all the way through. If you develop fever, muscle aches, or flu-like symptoms after eating a higher-risk food, contact your provider, as listeriosis can be treated with antibiotics. Choosing pasteurised and thoroughly cooked foods lowers the risk.

Toxoplasmosis is an infection caused by a parasite called Toxoplasma. It is usually mild in adults, but if you catch it for the first time during pregnancy it can, in some cases, harm the baby, so prevention is important. The parasite can be found in raw or undercooked meat, in unwashed fruit and vegetables, in soil, and in the faeces of infected cats. You can lower your risk with some simple steps. Cook meat thoroughly until it is steaming hot with no pink inside, and avoid raw or cured meats such as some salami and Parma ham unless cooked. Wash fruits, vegetables, and salads well to remove any soil, and wash hands, knives, and boards after handling raw meat or unwashed produce. Wear gloves when gardening or handling soil and wash your hands afterward. If you have a cat, avoid emptying the litter tray if possible; if you must, wear gloves and wash your hands well, and change litter daily since the parasite becomes infectious after a day or two. Avoid contact with lambs and sheep during lambing season. If you are worried you may have been exposed, ask your provider, as testing and treatment are available.

Fish is a healthy part of a pregnancy diet, providing protein and omega-3 fats that help your baby's brain and eyes develop, but some fish contain higher levels of mercury, which can harm a baby's developing nervous system. The guidance is to enjoy fish that are lower in mercury while avoiding the highest-mercury types. Aim for about 8 to 12 ounces (two to three servings) a week of a variety of lower-mercury fish. Good lower-mercury choices include salmon, sardines, trout, herring, pollock, tilapia, cod, catfish, canned light tuna, and shrimp. Fish to avoid in pregnancy because they are highest in mercury include shark, swordfish, king mackerel, marlin, orange roughy, tilefish from the Gulf of Mexico, and bigeye tuna. Albacore (white) tuna and yellowfin tuna are higher than canned light tuna, so limit these to a smaller amount. If you eat fish caught by family or friends, check local advisories, and if none are available, limit that fish to one serving a week. Choosing a range of lower-mercury fish lets you gain the nutritional benefits while keeping your baby safe.

Most foods are safe in pregnancy, but a few are best avoided or limited to protect you and your baby. Avoid certain cheeses, specifically soft mould-ripened cheeses like brie and camembert and soft blue cheeses, unless cooked until steaming, because of the risk of listeria. Avoid unpasteurised (raw) milk and dairy. Do not eat raw or undercooked meat, and avoid pâté of any type and cured meats unless cooked, to reduce the risk of toxoplasmosis and listeria. Eggs produced under recognised safety schemes can be eaten runny or raw, but other eggs should be cooked until the white and yolk are solid. Avoid liver and products such as liver pâté and high-dose vitamin A supplements, because too much vitamin A can harm the baby. Do not eat raw shellfish, and cook all shellfish thoroughly. Limit oily fish to no more than two portions a week and avoid high-mercury fish such as shark, swordfish, and marlin. Wash fruits, vegetables, and salads to remove soil, keep caffeine under 200 milligrams a day, and avoid alcohol. Following these food-safety steps lowers the risk of infections and problems in pregnancy.

A vegetarian or vegan diet can be healthy in pregnancy with some planning to make sure you and your baby get all the nutrients you need. The nutrients to pay particular attention to are protein, iron, calcium, vitamin B12, vitamin D, iodine, and omega-3 fats. For protein, include beans, lentils, chickpeas, tofu and other soya foods, nuts, seeds, and, for vegetarians, eggs and dairy. Get iron from pulses, dark green vegetables, fortified cereals, and dried fruit, and eat them with vitamin C-rich foods to boost absorption. Calcium can come from fortified plant milks and cereals, tofu set with calcium, and green vegetables, or dairy for vegetarians. Vitamin B12 is found naturally only in animal foods, so vegans need fortified foods or a supplement. Iodine can be low on plant-based diets, and omega-3 DHA can be obtained from an algae-based supplement. A daily vitamin D supplement is recommended for everyone in pregnancy. Because a vegan diet in particular can fall short of B12 and iodine, it is wise to discuss your diet with your midwife or a dietitian, who can advise on suitable fortified foods and supplements to keep both of you well nourished.

Vitamin A is important for your baby's growth, eyes, and immune system, but in pregnancy it is a nutrient where too much can be harmful. High amounts of preformed vitamin A (retinol), the type found in animal foods and some supplements, can cause birth defects, so pregnant people are advised not to take high-dose vitamin A supplements and to avoid foods very high in it. In particular, avoid liver and liver products such as liver pâté and cod liver oil, because these are extremely rich in retinol. Also avoid multivitamins or fish liver oil supplements that contain vitamin A unless they are specifically designed for pregnancy and stay within safe limits; check that any prenatal supplement does not exceed the recommended amount. You do not need to avoid vitamin A altogether, and there is no concern with the plant form, beta-carotene, found in colourful fruit and vegetables such as carrots, sweet potato, and spinach, which the body converts to vitamin A only as needed. Eating a normal balanced diet with these vegetables safely provides the vitamin A you need. If you take supplements, check the label and ask your provider whether the vitamin A content is safe.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: How much weight it is healthy to gain in pregnancy depends on your weight before you became pregnant, measured by your body mass index (BMI). General guidance for a single baby is that people who were underweight before pregnancy may gain about 28 to 40 pounds, those at a healthy weight about 25 to 35 pounds, those who were overweight about 15 to 25 pounds, and those with obesity about 11 to 20 pounds. People expecting twins are advised to gain more. Most weight gain happens after the first trimester and should be gradual. Gaining within the recommended range supports your baby's growth while lowering the chance of complications such as gestational diabetes, high blood pressure, and a difficult birth. Rather than eating for two, focus on a balanced diet with the right amount of extra energy, which is only modest and mainly in later pregnancy. Regular gentle activity also helps. Your provider can give you a personal target based on your starting weight and check your progress at appointments. If you are gaining much more or less than expected, discuss it with your midwife or doctor so any concerns can be addressed.
source_name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

text: At around 4 weeks of pregnancy, the developing baby is a tiny ball of cells called a blastocyst that has recently implanted into the lining of the uterus. Although it is only about 2 millimetres long, a lot is already happening. The cells are beginning to arrange themselves into layers that will go on to form the different parts of the body, and the structures that will become the placenta and the amniotic sac are developing to nourish and protect the pregnancy. This is roughly the time many people miss a period and might take a positive pregnancy test, as the body starts producing the pregnancy hormone hCG. You may notice early symptoms such as tender breasts, tiredness, mild cramping, or light spotting as implantation occurs, though some people feel nothing yet. It is an important window for the baby's early development, which is why taking 400 micrograms of folic acid daily and avoiding alcohol and smoking matter from as early as possible. If you have not already, arrange to see your midwife or doctor to start your pregnancy (antenatal) care.

By 8 weeks of pregnancy, the embryo has grown to around 1.6 centimetres, roughly the size of a raspberry, and is changing rapidly. The major organs and body systems are forming, and the tiny limbs are developing, with the beginnings of arms and legs and the formation of hands and feet, which at this stage may look like paddles. Facial features are taking shape, and the neural tube that becomes the brain and spinal cord is closing. The heart has formed and is beating. Around the end of this period the embryo starts to be called a fetus. For you, early pregnancy symptoms are often at their strongest around now, including nausea, tiredness, tender breasts, and a heightened sense of smell, all linked to rising pregnancy hormones. You may need to pass urine more often. This is still an important time for the baby's development, so continue taking folic acid until 12 weeks, eat well, and avoid alcohol, smoking, and other harmful substances. If you have not yet booked your first antenatal appointment, contact your midwife to arrange it.

At 12 weeks, the first trimester is coming to an end, and the baby is now fully formed in outline, measuring around 5.4 centimetres from head to bottom. All the major organs, muscles, limbs, and bones are in place, and from now on the baby will mainly grow and mature. The fingers and toes are separate, the baby can make small movements, and the kidneys have started to produce urine. On an ultrasound scan around this time, the heartbeat can be seen, and many people have their dating scan between about 10 and 14 weeks, which confirms how many weeks pregnant they are and checks the baby's development. For many people, early pregnancy symptoms such as nausea and extreme tiredness begin to ease as the first trimester closes, though this varies. The risk of miscarriage also falls significantly after this point. This is often the time people choose to share their news. Continue with a healthy diet, your vitamin D supplement, and regular antenatal care, and attend your scan appointment, where you may also be offered screening tests.

By 16 weeks, the baby is around 11.6 centimetres long from head to bottom and continues to develop quickly. The muscles and bones are getting stronger, and the baby can make a range of movements, such as clenching a fist and pulling faces, though you may not feel them yet. Fine hair called lanugo may start to grow, and the nervous system is maturing so that movements become more coordinated. The eyes and ears are moving toward their final positions. Some people begin to feel the first fluttering movements, known as quickening, between about 16 and 24 weeks, often earlier in those who have been pregnant before. For you, many early pregnancy symptoms have usually settled by now, and some people feel more energetic in the second trimester. You may notice a small bump beginning to show. Keep up with healthy eating, gentle activity, and your antenatal appointments.
