Later, the growing uterus pushes up against the diaphragm, the muscle used for breathing, leaving less room for the lungs to expand and making you breathless with activity or when lying flat. You can ease mild breathlessness by resting, changing position, sitting or standing upright to give your lungs more room, moving more slowly, and using extra pillows to prop yourself up in bed. Gentle regular activity also helps your overall fitness. However, breathlessness can occasionally point to a problem such as anaemia or, rarely, a blood clot. Contact your midwife or doctor if the breathlessness is severe or comes on suddenly, if you have chest pain, a fast heartbeat, a cough, or you feel breathless even at rest, or if your lips or fingers look blue. Sudden severe breathlessness needs emergency care.

Folic acid, the manmade form of the B vitamin folate, is one of the most important supplements in early pregnancy. Taking folic acid helps the baby's neural tube, which becomes the brain and spinal cord, to form and close properly, greatly lowering the risk of neural tube defects such as spina bifida. Because the neural tube develops in the first weeks, ideally you should take folic acid before conception and continue until you are 12 weeks pregnant. The usual recommended dose is 400 micrograms a day. Some people need a higher dose of 5 milligrams a day, prescribed by a doctor, for example if they or their partner have a neural tube defect, have had a previous pregnancy affected by one, have diabetes, have a raised body weight, or take certain medicines such as some for epilepsy. Even with a healthy diet it is hard to get enough folate from food alone, which is why a supplement is advised. If you did not take folic acid before becoming pregnant, start as soon as you find out and continue to 12 weeks. Ask your midwife or doctor if you are unsure which dose is right for you.

As well as taking a folic acid supplement in early pregnancy, you can include foods that contain folate, the natural form of the vitamin, as part of a healthy diet. Good sources include green leafy vegetables such as spinach, kale, and broccoli, as well as peas, chickpeas, beans, and lentils. Some breakfast cereals and fat spreads are fortified with folic acid, which the label will show. Other sources include Brussels sprouts, asparagus, oranges, and wholegrain foods. Folate can be lost when vegetables are overcooked or boiled in lots of water, so steaming, microwaving, or lightly cooking vegetables helps keep more of the vitamin. It is important to know that food alone usually cannot provide enough folate to give the same protection as a supplement in early pregnancy, so eating folate-rich foods should be in addition to, not instead of, taking 400 micrograms of folic acid a day until 12 weeks. Combining a supplement with a varied diet that includes these vegetables, pulses, and fortified foods supports the baby's early development and your own overall nutrition during pregnancy.

Iron is a mineral your body uses to make haemoglobin, the part of red blood cells that carries oxygen. During pregnancy your blood volume increases and your baby and placenta need iron too, so your requirement rises to about 27 milligrams a day. Getting enough iron helps prevent iron-deficiency anaemia, which can make you very tired, weak, and short of breath, and which in pregnancy is linked to a higher risk of premature birth and low birth weight. Iron also supports your baby's growth and brain development. Because needs are high, some people cannot meet them through diet alone and may be advised to take a supplement, especially if a blood test shows low iron. Your provider checks for anaemia during pregnancy and will recommend supplements if needed; do not start high-dose iron on your own, as too much can cause side effects such as constipation and stomach upset. Eating iron-rich foods and pairing them with vitamin C helps your body absorb iron, while tea, coffee, and calcium-rich foods eaten at the same time can reduce absorption. Discuss any tiredness or breathlessness with your midwife.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: You can help meet your higher iron needs in pregnancy by eating iron-rich foods. There are two types of dietary iron. Heme iron, which the body absorbs more easily, comes from animal foods such as lean red meat, poultry, and fish. Non-heme iron comes from plant foods and includes beans, lentils, tofu, dark green leafy vegetables like spinach, nuts and seeds, dried fruit such as apricots, and iron-fortified breads and breakfast cereals. To get the most from plant sources, eat them with a food rich in vitamin C, such as oranges, strawberries, tomatoes, or peppers, because vitamin C boosts the absorption of non-heme iron. On the other hand, tea, coffee, and calcium supplements or dairy eaten at the same meal can reduce how much iron you absorb, so it can help to have them between meals instead. A varied diet that includes these foods supports healthy red blood cells for you and your baby. If a blood test shows your iron is low despite a good diet, your provider may recommend an iron supplement to prevent or treat anaemia.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Calcium is a mineral needed to build your baby's bones and teeth, and it also supports the healthy working of your heart, muscles, and nerves. Adults, including most pregnant people, need about 1,000 milligrams of calcium a day, while pregnant teenagers need more, around 1,300 milligrams, because their own bones are still growing. If you do not get enough calcium, your body draws on the calcium stored in your bones to supply the baby. Good food sources include milk, yoghurt, and cheese, calcium-fortified plant milks and cereals, tinned fish with soft edible bones such as sardines, tofu set with calcium, and green vegetables such as kale and broccoli. In some situations, calcium supplements are recommended in pregnancy, and adequate calcium may help lower the risk of high blood pressure and pre-eclampsia in people at higher risk, so follow your provider's advice. Vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium, so the two work together. Aim to include calcium-rich foods across the day, and speak to your midwife or doctor before taking supplements, as very high intakes above the recommended upper limit are not advised.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium and is needed to keep bones, teeth, and muscles healthy, both for you and your developing baby. During pregnancy a daily intake of about 15 micrograms (600 international units) is recommended. Our bodies make vitamin D from sunlight on the skin, but many people do not get enough, particularly in winter, if they spend little time outdoors, cover their skin, or have darker skin. Because it is hard to get enough from food, a daily supplement is often advised throughout pregnancy. Food sources are limited but include oily fish such as salmon and sardines, eggs, and foods fortified with vitamin D such as some breakfast cereals and fat spreads. A supplement containing 10 micrograms a day is commonly recommended for pregnant and breastfeeding people. Taking vitamin D during pregnancy supports the baby's bone development and helps prevent low vitamin D levels in the newborn. Avoid very high doses, as too much vitamin D over a long period can be harmful; stay within the recommended amount unless a doctor advises otherwise. Ask your midwife which supplement and dose are right for you.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Vitamin B12 helps your body make red blood cells and DNA and supports the development of your baby's brain and nervous system. During pregnancy the recommended intake is about 2.6 micrograms a day. Vitamin B12 is found naturally in animal foods, including meat, fish, eggs, milk, cheese, and other dairy products, so most people who eat these regularly get enough. People who follow a vegan or strict vegetarian diet are at higher risk of B12 deficiency, because plant foods do not naturally contain it. If you avoid animal products, you can get B12 from fortified foods such as some plant milks, breakfast cereals, and nutritional yeast, or from a supplement; a reliable daily source is important in pregnancy. A deficiency can lead to a type of anaemia and, if severe, may affect the baby's development, so it is worth discussing your diet with your midwife or doctor, who can arrange a blood test if needed. Getting enough B12, along with folate, supports healthy blood and your baby's growing nervous system. If you are vegan or vegetarian, ask about supplements to make sure your intake is adequate.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Vitamin C, also called ascorbic acid, helps protect cells, supports the immune system, and is needed to make collagen, a protein important for healthy skin, blood vessels, and connective tissue. It also greatly improves the absorption of iron from plant foods, which is useful in pregnancy when iron needs are high. During pregnancy the recommended intake is about 85 milligrams a day for adults, and slightly less for teenagers. Vitamin C is found in many fruits and vegetables, including oranges and other citrus fruits, strawberries, kiwi, tomatoes, peppers, broccoli, and potatoes. Because the body does not store vitamin C and it is easily lost in cooking, it helps to eat some raw or lightly cooked fruit and vegetables each day. A balanced diet with plenty of produce usually provides enough, so routine high-dose supplements are not needed and very large amounts are not advised. A practical tip is to pair vitamin C-rich foods with iron-rich meals, for example having fruit or a salad alongside beans, lentils, or fortified cereal, to help your body absorb more iron.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Iodine is a mineral your body needs to make thyroid hormones, which regulate metabolism and are essential for your baby's brain and nervous system development. Iodine needs rise in pregnancy to about 220 micrograms a day, roughly 50 percent higher than usual, because the baby depends on your supply. Not getting enough iodine during pregnancy can affect the baby's growth and brain development, so a steady intake matters. Good food sources include dairy products such as milk and yoghurt, eggs, and fish and other seafood. Iodised salt is a source in some countries, though salt intake should still be moderate. People who avoid dairy and fish, including some vegans, may find it harder to get enough and should look at fortified foods or discuss supplements with their provider. Some prenatal supplements contain iodine; the American Thyroid Association has recommended that pregnant and breastfeeding people consider a supplement providing iodine. However, both too little and too much iodine can affect the thyroid, so it is best not to take high-dose iodine or kelp supplements without advice. Ask your midwife or doctor if you are unsure whether your diet provides enough.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Choline is a nutrient that supports your baby's brain and spinal cord development and helps with the formation of cell membranes. It works alongside folate, and getting enough during pregnancy is important for healthy growth. Pregnant people are advised to get about 450 milligrams of choline a day, yet many do not reach this amount, so pregnancy is considered a time when intake can fall short. The body makes some choline, but not enough to meet needs, so it is important to get it from food. Rich sources include eggs (especially the yolk), lean meat, poultry, fish, and dairy products, along with smaller amounts in beans, nuts, and cruciferous vegetables such as broccoli and Brussels sprouts. People who eat few or no animal products may find it harder to get enough choline and should pay attention to plant sources or discuss supplements with their provider, as many prenatal vitamins contain little or no choline. Including choline-rich foods such as eggs as part of a balanced diet is a simple way to support your baby's developing brain. Ask your midwife if you are concerned about your intake.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Omega-3 fatty acids, especially DHA (docosahexaenoic acid), support your baby's brain and eye development during pregnancy. The main dietary source is oily fish. Health authorities advise pregnant people to eat about 8 to 12 ounces (two to three servings) a week of fish that are lower in mercury to provide these beneficial fats. Good lower-mercury choices include salmon, sardines, herring, trout, and anchovies. At the same time, certain fish high in mercury should be avoided in pregnancy, such as shark, swordfish, king mackerel, and marlin, and oily fish should be limited to no more than a couple of portions a week. If you do not eat fish, you can get the plant omega-3 called ALA from walnuts, flaxseed, chia seeds, and rapeseed oil, though the body converts only a small amount of ALA into DHA. Some people choose a fish oil or algae-based DHA supplement; algae oil is a suitable option for vegetarians and vegans. Avoid supplements made from fish liver oil, such as cod liver oil, in pregnancy because they can contain high levels of vitamin A. Ask your provider before starting any supplement.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: Protein is a building block your body uses to make new tissue, and during pregnancy it supports the growth of your baby, the placenta, and your own expanding blood supply and tissues. Including a source of protein at each meal helps your baby grow and can also help you feel full and keep energy steady. Good sources include lean meat and poultry, fish that is low in mercury, eggs, and dairy foods such as milk, yoghurt, and cheese. Plant sources are also excellent and include beans, lentils, chickpeas, tofu and other soya foods, nuts, seeds, and peanut butter. Combining different plant proteins across the day, such as beans with grains, helps provide the full range of amino acids, which is especially useful for vegetarians and vegans. Most people can meet their increased protein needs through a varied, balanced diet without special supplements. Simple ways to add protein include having eggs or yoghurt at breakfast, adding beans or lentils to soups and stews, and choosing nuts or hummus as snacks. If you follow a plant-based diet, pay attention to protein variety and speak to your provider if you have concerns.

Fibre is the part of plant foods that the body cannot fully digest, and it is helpful in pregnancy because it keeps the bowels moving and helps prevent constipation, which is common when pregnancy hormones slow digestion and when taking iron supplements. A high-fibre diet also supports steady energy and general gut health. Good sources of fibre include wholemeal bread, wholegrain cereals and oats, brown rice and wholewheat pasta, fruit and vegetables (with skins where suitable), and pulses such as beans, lentils, and chickpeas. Aim to include a variety of these foods across the day, for example wholegrain cereal at breakfast, fruit as snacks, and vegetables or beans with meals. It is important to drink plenty of fluids as you increase fibre, because water helps fibre do its job and keeps stools soft. Staying physically active with gentle exercise such as walking also helps the bowels move. Increasing fibre gradually can reduce bloating. If you are constipated despite a high-fibre diet and plenty of fluids, speak to your midwife, pharmacist, or doctor, who can advise on a laxative that is safe to use in pregnancy.

It is fine to have some caffeine in pregnancy, but high levels are best avoided because too much has been linked to low birth weight and, in some studies, a higher risk of miscarriage. The advice is to limit caffeine to no more than 200 milligrams a day. To picture that amount, a mug of instant coffee has about 100 milligrams, a mug of filter coffee about 140 milligrams, a mug of tea about 75 milligrams, a can of cola about 40 milligrams, and a 50 gram bar of plain chocolate up to about 25 milligrams. Caffeine is also found in energy drinks, which can be high, and in some cold and flu remedies, so check labels. You do not need to cut out caffeine completely; keeping under the daily limit is what matters. If you would rather avoid it, decaffeinated tea and coffee, fruit juice diluted with water, and plain water are good choices. Spreading drinks through the day and keeping track of hidden sources such as chocolate and soft drinks makes it easier to stay within the recommended amount. If you are unsure, ask your midwife for guidance.

The safest approach is to not drink any alcohol during pregnancy, because no level has been proven safe. Alcohol passes across the placenta to the baby, whose liver is not developed enough to process it. Drinking in pregnancy can increase the risk of miscarriage, premature birth, and low birth weight, and regular or heavy drinking can cause fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD), a range of lasting problems that can affect a child's learning, behaviour, growth, and physical development. The risk is greater the more you drink, and drinking in the first three months may particularly increase the chance of miscarriage. Because of this uncertainty, official guidance is to avoid alcohol entirely if you are pregnant or trying to conceive. If you drank small amounts before you knew you were pregnant, the risk to the baby is likely to be low, but it is best to stop once you know. If you are finding it hard to stop drinking, this is important to discuss, and your midwife or doctor can offer support and referral to services without judgement. Choosing alcohol-free drinks is the safest option throughout pregnancy.

Listeria is a type of bacteria that can cause an infection called listeriosis. Pregnant people are far more likely than others to get listeriosis, and although it may cause only mild flu-like illness in the mother, it can lead to miscarriage, stillbirth, premature birth, or serious illness in the newborn, so avoiding it is important. Listeria can grow even in the fridge, so certain higher-risk foods are best avoided in pregnancy. These include unpasteurised (raw) milk and soft cheeses made from it, soft mould-ripened cheeses such as brie and camembert and soft blue cheeses, refrigerated pâté and meat spreads, refrigerated smoked seafood, pre-prepared or store-bought salads such as coleslaw, and hot dogs or deli/luncheon meats unless they are heated until steaming hot. Chill foods promptly, keep your fridge cold, wash fruits and vegetables well, and keep raw and ready-to-eat foods separate. Reheat leftovers and ready meals until piping hot all the way through. If you develop fever, muscle aches, or flu-like symptoms after eating a higher-risk food, contact your provider, as listeriosis can be treated with antibiotics. Choosing pasteurised and thoroughly cooked foods lowers the risk.

Toxoplasmosis is an infection caused by a parasite called Toxoplasma. It is usually mild in adults, but if you catch it for the first time during pregnancy it can, in some cases, harm the baby, so prevention is important. The parasite can be found in raw or undercooked meat, in unwashed fruit and vegetables, in soil, and in the faeces of infected cats. You can lower your risk with some simple steps. Cook meat thoroughly until it is steaming hot with no pink inside, and avoid raw or cured meats such as some salami and Parma ham unless cooked. Wash fruits, vegetables, and salads well to remove any soil, and wash hands, knives, and boards after handling raw meat or unwashed produce. Wear gloves when gardening or handling soil and wash your hands afterward. If you have a cat, avoid emptying the litter tray if possible; if you must, wear gloves and wash your hands well, and change litter daily since the parasite becomes infectious after a day or two. Avoid contact with lambs and sheep during lambing season. If you are worried you may have been exposed, ask your provider, as testing and treatment are available.

Fish is a healthy part of a pregnancy diet, providing protein and omega-3 fats that help your baby's brain and eyes develop, but some fish contain higher levels of mercury, which can harm a baby's developing nervous system. The guidance is to enjoy fish that are lower in mercury while avoiding the highest-mercury types. Aim for about 8 to 12 ounces (two to three servings) a week of a variety of lower-mercury fish. Good lower-mercury choices include salmon, sardines, trout, herring, pollock, tilapia, cod, catfish, canned light tuna, and shrimp. Fish to avoid in pregnancy because they are highest in mercury include shark, swordfish, king mackerel, marlin, orange roughy, tilefish from the Gulf of Mexico, and bigeye tuna. Albacore (white) tuna and yellowfin tuna are higher than canned light tuna, so limit these to a smaller amount. If you eat fish caught by family or friends, check local advisories, and if none are available, limit that fish to one serving a week. Choosing a range of lower-mercury fish lets you gain the nutritional benefits while keeping your baby safe.

Most foods are safe in pregnancy, but a few are best avoided or limited to protect you and your baby. Avoid certain cheeses, specifically soft mould-ripened cheeses like brie and camembert and soft blue cheeses, unless cooked until steaming, because of the risk of listeria. Avoid unpasteurised (raw) milk and dairy. Do not eat raw or undercooked meat, and avoid pâté of any type and cured meats unless cooked, to reduce the risk of toxoplasmosis and listeria. Eggs produced under recognised safety schemes can be eaten runny or raw, but other eggs should be cooked until the white and yolk are solid. Avoid liver and products such as liver pâté and high-dose vitamin A supplements, because too much vitamin A can harm the baby. Do not eat raw shellfish, and cook all shellfish thoroughly. Limit oily fish to no more than two portions a week and avoid high-mercury fish such as shark, swordfish, and marlin. Wash fruits, vegetables, and salads to remove soil, keep caffeine under 200 milligrams a day, and avoid alcohol. Following these food-safety steps lowers the risk of infections and problems in pregnancy.

A vegetarian or vegan diet can be healthy in pregnancy with some planning to make sure you and your baby get all the nutrients you need. The nutrients to pay particular attention to are protein, iron, calcium, vitamin B12, vitamin D, iodine, and omega-3 fats. For protein, include beans, lentils, chickpeas, tofu and other soya foods, nuts, seeds, and, for vegetarians, eggs and dairy. Get iron from pulses, dark green vegetables, fortified cereals, and dried fruit, and eat them with vitamin C-rich foods to boost absorption. Calcium can come from fortified plant milks and cereals, tofu set with calcium, and green vegetables, or dairy for vegetarians. Vitamin B12 is found naturally only in animal foods, so vegans need fortified foods or a supplement. Iodine can be low on plant-based diets, and omega-3 DHA can be obtained from an algae-based supplement. A daily vitamin D supplement is recommended for everyone in pregnancy. Because a vegan diet in particular can fall short of B12 and iodine, it is wise to discuss your diet with your midwife or a dietitian, who can advise on suitable fortified foods and supplements to keep both of you well nourished.

Vitamin A is important for your baby's growth, eyes, and immune system, but in pregnancy it is a nutrient where too much can be harmful. High amounts of preformed vitamin A (retinol), the type found in animal foods and some supplements, can cause birth defects, so pregnant people are advised not to take high-dose vitamin A supplements and to avoid foods very high in it. In particular, avoid liver and liver products such as liver pâté and cod liver oil, because these are extremely rich in retinol. Also avoid multivitamins or fish liver oil supplements that contain vitamin A unless they are specifically designed for pregnancy and stay within safe limits; check that any prenatal supplement does not exceed the recommended amount. You do not need to avoid vitamin A altogether, and there is no concern with the plant form, beta-carotene, found in colourful fruit and vegetables such as carrots, sweet potato, and spinach, which the body converts to vitamin A only as needed. Eating a normal balanced diet with these vegetables safely provides the vitamin A you need. If you take supplements, check the label and ask your provider whether the vitamin A content is safe.
source_name: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements

text: How much weight it is healthy to gain in pregnancy depends on your weight before you became pregnant, measured by your body mass index (BMI). General guidance for a single baby is that people who were underweight before pregnancy may gain about 28 to 40 pounds, those at a healthy weight about 25 to 35 pounds, those who were overweight about 15 to 25 pounds, and those with obesity about 11 to 20 pounds. People expecting twins are advised to gain more. Most weight gain happens after the first trimester and should be gradual. Gaining within the recommended range supports your baby's growth while lowering the chance of complications such as gestational diabetes, high blood pressure, and a difficult birth. Rather than eating for two, focus on a balanced diet with the right amount of extra energy, which is only modest and mainly in later pregnancy. Regular gentle activity also helps. Your provider can give you a personal target based on your starting weight and check your progress at appointments. If you are gaining much more or less than expected, discuss it with your midwife or doctor so any concerns can be addressed.
source_name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

text: At around 4 weeks of pregnancy, the developing baby is a tiny ball of cells called a blastocyst that has recently implanted into the lining of the uterus. Although it is only about 2 millimetres long, a lot is already happening. The cells are beginning to arrange themselves into layers that will go on to form the different parts of the body, and the structures that will become the placenta and the amniotic sac are developing to nourish and protect the pregnancy. This is roughly the time many people miss a period and might take a positive pregnancy test, as the body starts producing the pregnancy hormone hCG. You may notice early symptoms such as tender breasts, tiredness, mild cramping, or light spotting as implantation occurs, though some people feel nothing yet. It is an important window for the baby's early development, which is why taking 400 micrograms of folic acid daily and avoiding alcohol and smoking matter from as early as possible. If you have not already, arrange to see your midwife or doctor to start your pregnancy (antenatal) care.

By 8 weeks of pregnancy, the embryo has grown to around 1.6 centimetres, roughly the size of a raspberry, and is changing rapidly. The major organs and body systems are forming, and the tiny limbs are developing, with the beginnings of arms and legs and the formation of hands and feet, which at this stage may look like paddles. Facial features are taking shape, and the neural tube that becomes the brain and spinal cord is closing. The heart has formed and is beating. Around the end of this period the embryo starts to be called a fetus. For you, early pregnancy symptoms are often at their strongest around now, including nausea, tiredness, tender breasts, and a heightened sense of smell, all linked to rising pregnancy hormones. You may need to pass urine more often. This is still an important time for the baby's development, so continue taking folic acid until 12 weeks, eat well, and avoid alcohol, smoking, and other harmful substances. If you have not yet booked your first antenatal appointment, contact your midwife to arrange it.

At 12 weeks, the first trimester is coming to an end, and the baby is now fully formed in outline, measuring around 5.4 centimetres from head to bottom. All the major organs, muscles, limbs, and bones are in place, and from now on the baby will mainly grow and mature. The fingers and toes are separate, the baby can make small movements, and the kidneys have started to produce urine. On an ultrasound scan around this time, the heartbeat can be seen, and many people have their dating scan between about 10 and 14 weeks, which confirms how many weeks pregnant they are and checks the baby's development. For many people, early pregnancy symptoms such as nausea and extreme tiredness begin to ease as the first trimester closes, though this varies. The risk of miscarriage also falls significantly after this point. This is often the time people choose to share their news. Continue with a healthy diet, your vitamin D supplement, and regular antenatal care, and attend your scan appointment, where you may also be offered screening tests.

By 16 weeks, the baby is around 11.6 centimetres long from head to bottom and continues to develop quickly. The muscles and bones are getting stronger, and the baby can make a range of movements, such as clenching a fist and pulling faces, though you may not feel them yet. Fine hair called lanugo may start to grow, and the nervous system is maturing so that movements become more coordinated. The eyes and ears are moving toward their final positions. Some people begin to feel the first fluttering movements, known as quickening, between about 16 and 24 weeks, often earlier in those who have been pregnant before. For you, many early pregnancy symptoms have usually settled by now, and some people feel more energetic in the second trimester. You may notice a small bump beginning to show. Keep up with healthy eating, gentle activity, and your antenatal appointments. This is a good time to continue any recommended supplements such as vitamin D and to raise any questions with your midwife about upcoming scans and screening, including the mid-pregnancy anomaly scan that usually takes place around 18 to 21 weeks.

At 20 weeks you are around halfway through your pregnancy. The baby is roughly 25 centimetres long from head to heel and is becoming more active, with movements that many people can now feel clearly. The skin is developing and is covered with a protective greasy coating called vernix, and fine hair called lanugo helps hold it in place. The baby is starting to develop a pattern of sleeping and waking, and the senses, including hearing, are maturing. Around this time you are usually offered the mid-pregnancy scan, often called the anomaly or anatomy scan, which is typically carried out between 18 and 21 weeks. This detailed ultrasound checks the baby's growth and looks closely at the development of the organs, spine, limbs, brain, and heart to screen for certain physical conditions. If you wish, you may be able to find out the baby's sex at this scan, depending on local policy and the baby's position. For you, the bump is now usually visible. Continue with healthy eating, staying active, and attending appointments, and use the scan as a chance to ask your sonographer or midwife any questions.

By 24 weeks the baby is considered viable, meaning that from this point there is a chance of survival if the baby were born, with specialist neonatal care. The baby is around 30 centimetres long and is becoming more active and responsive, with regular periods of movement that you should be feeling by now. The lungs are developing the structures needed for breathing, though they are not yet mature, and the baby is beginning to practise breathing movements. The face is fully formed, and the baby may respond to sound and touch. The skin is still thin and wrinkled as fat has not yet built up fully. Because this is a milestone for viability, it is especially important from now on to be aware of your baby's pattern of movements and to report any reduction straight away. For you, you may notice the bump growing and symptoms such as backache or Braxton Hicks tightenings. Around this stage you may be offered screening for gestational diabetes if you have risk factors. Continue attending antenatal appointments, eat well, stay active as able, and keep taking any recommended supplements.

At 28 weeks you are entering the third and final trimester. The baby is around 37 centimetres long and now spends time with eyes that can open and close, and can blink. The baby is putting on weight and beginning to lay down fat, which will help with warmth and energy after birth, and the lungs continue to mature. The baby may respond to light and sound from outside, and periods of movement and rest become more defined. For you, this is often when antenatal appointments become more frequent so your provider can monitor your blood pressure, check for anaemia, and follow the baby's growth. You may be offered blood tests around now, and if you are RhD negative, you may be offered anti-D treatment. It is also the point from which side-sleeping is advised, because going to sleep on your back in the third trimester is linked to a higher risk of stillbirth. Continue to monitor your baby's movements and report any change without delay. Keep eating well, stay gently active, watch for warning signs such as severe headache or swelling, and attend all your appointments.

By 32 weeks the baby is around 42 centimetres long and is growing steadily, putting on weight and building up fat stores under the skin, which is becoming smoother and less wrinkled. The baby is now well developed and is often beginning to settle into a head-down position ready for birth, though there is still time for the position to change. The bones are hardening, although the skull stays soft and flexible to ease the journey through the birth canal. The baby practises breathing movements and can suck and swallow. For you, the growing uterus may bring symptoms such as heartburn, breathlessness, backache, and more frequent Braxton Hicks contractions, and you may feel tired as sleep becomes less comfortable. Antenatal appointments continue, with checks on your blood pressure, the baby's growth, and their position. Keep going to sleep on your side, keep a close eye on your baby's movements, and report any reduction promptly. Now is a good time to think about your birth plan and to prepare practical things for the birth. Continue eating well and resting when you can.

At 36 weeks the baby is around 47 centimetres long and is now getting close to full term, which is reached at 37 weeks. By this stage the lungs are usually well developed and are likely ready to work on their own after birth. The baby continues to gain weight and lay down fat, and the soft downy hair (lanugo) that covered the skin is mostly disappearing. Most babies have settled into a head-down position ready for labour; if your baby is bottom-down (breech) at this stage, your provider will discuss the options with you. Space in the uterus is now tight, so movements may feel different, more like squirms and rolls than kicks, but you should still feel your baby regularly and should report any reduction. For you, the baby may drop lower into the pelvis, which can ease breathlessness but increase pressure on the bladder. Keep monitoring movements, continue side-sleeping, and watch for signs of labour and any warning signs. Make sure your hospital bag and birth plan are ready, and keep going to your antenatal appointments as your due date approaches.

At 40 weeks you have reached your due date, though only a small proportion of babies arrive exactly on it, and it is normal to give birth in the days before or after. The baby is fully developed, on average around 51 centimetres long, with organs ready to work independently after birth. The baby is usually curled up in a head-down position ready for labour. You should continue to feel your baby moving right up to and during labour, so report any reduction in movements straight away rather than assuming babies move less at the end. Signs that labour may be starting include regular, strengthening contractions, a show (a plug of mucus from the cervix), backache, and your waters breaking. If your waters break or contractions become regular, contact your midwife or maternity unit for advice. If your pregnancy continues beyond your due date, your provider will discuss monitoring and, if needed, induction of labour, which is usually offered when you are past your due date to reduce the risks of a longer pregnancy. Rest when you can, stay in touch with your maternity team, and seek help promptly for any warning signs.

The first trimester covers the first 12 to 13 weeks of pregnancy, dated from the first day of your last period. It is a time of rapid, crucial development, as the fertilised egg implants and grows into an embryo and then a fetus, with the major organs, the brain and spinal cord, the heart, and the limbs all forming. Because so much is developing, this is when it is especially important to take folic acid, avoid alcohol and smoking, and be careful with medicines and food safety. For you, the first trimester often brings symptoms driven by rising hormones, such as nausea and vomiting, extreme tiredness, tender breasts, and needing to pass urine more often, though experiences vary. This is when you should arrange your first antenatal (booking) appointment, usually offered before 10 weeks, where your health is reviewed, blood tests are taken, and your care is planned. A dating scan is generally offered between about 10 and 14 weeks to confirm how far along you are, and screening tests may be discussed. The risk of miscarriage is highest in early pregnancy and falls considerably by the end of the first trimester.

The second trimester runs from about 14 to 28 weeks and is often the most comfortable part of pregnancy. Many early symptoms such as nausea and extreme tiredness ease, energy often returns, and the bump becomes visible. During these weeks the baby grows rapidly: the bones and muscles strengthen, the senses develop, and you usually begin to feel movements (quickening), often between 16 and 24 weeks. A key milestone is the mid-pregnancy ultrasound, commonly done around 18 to 21 weeks, which checks the baby's growth and looks in detail at the organs and development to screen for certain conditions, and which may show the baby's sex if you wish to know. Antenatal appointments continue, with checks on your blood pressure, urine, and the baby's growth. Screening for gestational diabetes is often carried out in this trimester, usually around 24 to 28 weeks, particularly if you have risk factors. This is a good time to plan ahead, stay active, and eat well. Toward the end of the trimester the baby reaches the point of viability, so being aware of movements becomes increasingly important.

The third trimester lasts from about 28 weeks until birth. During these final weeks the baby gains weight, lays down fat, and matures ready for life outside the womb, with the lungs among the last organs to be fully ready. The baby usually settles into a head-down position in preparation for labour. For you, the growing baby can bring discomforts such as backache, heartburn, breathlessness, swelling of the feet and ankles, frequent urination, and difficulty sleeping, and Braxton Hicks (practice) contractions become more noticeable. Antenatal appointments become more frequent so your provider can monitor your blood pressure, check the baby's growth and position, and watch for problems such as pre-eclampsia. From 28 weeks it is advised to go to sleep on your side. It is very important to be aware of your baby's movements and to report any reduction immediately. This is the time to prepare for the birth: make a birth plan, pack your hospital bag, and learn the signs of labour and the warning signs that need urgent care. If your pregnancy goes past your due date, induction may be discussed.

Antenatal appointments are regular checkups during pregnancy that help keep you and your baby healthy and pick up any problems early. For a first pregnancy without complications you may have around ten appointments, and for later pregnancies around seven, though you will be seen more often if you need extra care. The first, longest appointment is the booking appointment, ideally before 10 weeks, where your medical history is reviewed, blood and urine tests are taken, your blood pressure is measured, and your care is planned. A dating scan usually follows between about 10 and 14 weeks, and the mid-pregnancy scan around 18 to 21 weeks. Later appointments, at intervals such as 16, 25, 28, 31, 34, 36, 38, 40, and 41 weeks, become more frequent toward the end of pregnancy. At these visits your provider checks your blood pressure and urine, measures your bump to follow the baby's growth, listens to the baby's heartbeat, and discusses how you are feeling and any concerns. Attending all your appointments is one of the best ways to protect your health and your baby's; if you miss one, contact your clinic to rebook.

Your first antenatal appointment, called the booking appointment, is usually the longest and ideally takes place before you are 10 weeks pregnant. Its purpose is to review your health, plan your care, and give you information for a healthy pregnancy. Your midwife will ask about your medical and family history, any previous pregnancies, your mental health, medicines you take, and your lifestyle, so let them know about any conditions or concerns. You will be offered blood tests to check your blood group and rhesus status, to look for anaemia, and to screen for infections such as HIV, hepatitis B, and syphilis that can be treated to protect the baby. Your blood pressure and urine will be checked, your height and weight measured to work out your BMI, and you will be given advice on diet, supplements such as folic acid and vitamin D, and things to avoid. It is also a chance to discuss screening tests offered later in pregnancy and to ask questions. Bring a list of any medicines and note any worries beforehand. Attending early means any risks can be identified and your care tailored from the start.

The dating scan is an ultrasound usually offered between about 10 and 14 weeks of pregnancy, often called the 12-week scan. Its main purpose is to confirm how many weeks pregnant you are by measuring the baby, which gives a more accurate due date, and to check whether you are expecting one baby or more. The scan also checks that the baby is developing in the uterus and looks at early development. During the scan, a sonographer moves a probe over your belly, which is covered with gel, and shows the image on a screen; it is usually painless, though a full bladder is often needed for a clearer picture. At this scan you may also be offered screening for Down's syndrome and some other conditions. This can include the combined test, which brings together a measurement taken during the scan, called nuchal translucency, with a blood test to estimate the chance of certain chromosomal conditions. Screening is your choice, and you can discuss what the results would mean before deciding. The scan is also often a first chance to see your baby. Ask the sonographer if you have any questions.

The mid-pregnancy scan, often called the 20-week scan or anomaly scan, is a detailed ultrasound usually carried out between 18 and 21 weeks. Its main purpose is to check the baby's physical development and to look for certain conditions. The sonographer examines the baby's head, brain, face, spine, heart, stomach, kidneys, abdominal wall, and limbs, and checks the position of the placenta and the amount of amniotic fluid. It screens for a number of specific physical conditions, some of which may benefit from treatment or specialist care before or after birth. The scan cannot find every condition, and finding nothing of concern does not guarantee a baby will be born without any problem, but it provides valuable information. Most scans show that the baby appears to be developing as expected. If something is found, you will be offered further tests and support to help you understand what it means and your options. You can usually find out the baby's sex at this scan if you wish and local policy allows, though this is not the scan's main aim. As with all screening, having the scan is your choice.

Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT), also called non-invasive prenatal screening or cell-free DNA screening, is a blood test that can be done from about 10 weeks of pregnancy. It works by analysing small fragments of the baby's DNA that circulate in the pregnant person's blood, having crossed from the placenta. NIPT screens for certain chromosomal conditions, most commonly Down's syndrome (trisomy 21) and the less common trisomy 18 and trisomy 13, and it can often also indicate the baby's sex. Because it only needs a blood sample from the mother, NIPT carries no risk of miscarriage, unlike diagnostic tests such as amniocentesis. It is important to understand that NIPT is a screening test, not a diagnosis: it estimates the chance that a condition is present with high accuracy but cannot confirm it. A result suggesting a higher chance would usually be followed by a diagnostic test, such as amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling, to confirm before any decisions are made. Whether to have NIPT is your choice, and your midwife or doctor can explain what the possible results mean and how the test fits with other screening offered in pregnancy.

Amniocentesis is a diagnostic test offered during pregnancy, usually between 15 and 20 weeks, to find out whether the baby has certain genetic or chromosomal conditions such as Down's syndrome, or, less commonly, to check for some inherited or infectious conditions. It is generally offered when screening tests have suggested a higher chance of a condition, or when there is a known family risk. During the test, a thin needle is passed through the wall of the abdomen, guided by ultrasound, to take a small sample of the amniotic fluid that surrounds the baby; this fluid contains cells that can be analysed. The procedure usually causes only mild discomfort, like a period cramp. Because it is a diagnostic test, it can give a definite answer rather than a chance estimate. However, amniocentesis carries a small risk of miscarriage, so it is offered as a choice and you can take time to consider whether it is right for you. Results for the most common conditions are often available within a few days, with fuller results taking longer. Your provider will explain the benefits, risks, and what the results could mean before you decide.

Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) is a diagnostic test used in pregnancy to check whether the baby has a genetic or chromosomal condition such as Down's syndrome, or a specific inherited condition when there is a known family risk. It is usually carried out earlier than amniocentesis, generally between 11 and 14 weeks. During CVS, a small sample of cells is taken from the placenta, either by passing a needle through the wall of the abdomen or, less commonly, through the cervix, with the procedure guided by ultrasound. These placental cells contain the same genetic material as the baby, so they can be tested for chromosomal and genetic conditions. Like amniocentesis, CVS is a diagnostic test that can give a definite answer, and it is usually offered after screening has suggested a higher chance of a condition. It carries a small risk of miscarriage, so it is offered as a choice, and you can take time to weigh up whether to have it. An advantage of CVS is that it can be done earlier in pregnancy than amniocentesis. Your provider will explain the procedure, the risks, and what the results mean so you can make an informed decision.

The oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) is the test used to diagnose gestational diabetes, a type of diabetes that can develop in pregnancy. It is usually offered between 24 and 28 weeks, or earlier and sometimes repeated if you have risk factors, such as a raised body weight, a previous baby who was large, a family history of diabetes, certain ethnic backgrounds, or gestational diabetes in a past pregnancy. For the test you are asked not to eat or drink anything except water for a period beforehand, usually overnight. A blood sample is taken while you are fasting, then you drink a glucose (sugary) drink, and after resting for about two hours another blood sample is taken. Comparing the results shows how well your body is handling sugar. If gestational diabetes is diagnosed, it can usually be managed with changes to diet and activity, monitoring your blood sugar, and, for some people, medication, which helps protect both you and your baby. Managing it lowers the risk of a large baby and other complications. If you are offered the OGTT, follow the fasting instructions carefully so the result is accurate.

Being aware of your baby's movements is an important part of monitoring their wellbeing in the second half of pregnancy. Most people start to feel movements between 16 and 24 weeks. Rather than counting kicks to reach a set number or using a chart, the current advice is to get to know your baby's own usual pattern of movements, including how often and when they tend to be active. Every baby is different, so what is normal for your baby is what matters. There is no set number of movements you should feel each day. The key message is that a reduction in movements, or a change from the normal pattern, can be a sign that a baby is unwell, so you should contact your midwife or maternity unit straight away if you notice this, at any time of day or night. Do not wait until the next day, and do not rely on home handheld heartbeat monitors, which can give false reassurance. It is a myth that babies move less toward the end of pregnancy or during labour; you should continue to feel movements right up to and during birth. Reporting reduced movements promptly allows checks that can protect your baby.

A birth plan is a written record of what you would like to happen during your labour and birth. It is not a fixed contract, because births can be unpredictable and plans may need to change for safety, but it is a helpful way to think through your preferences and share them with your midwife and birth partner. In a birth plan you might note where you would like to give birth, who you want with you, and what positions you would like to try during labour. You can set out your preferences for pain relief, from breathing and water to gas and air, pethidine, or an epidural, and how you feel about interventions. You can also include wishes about the birth itself, such as delayed cord clamping, skin-to-skin contact straight after birth, feeding your baby, and who cuts the cord. It helps to learn about the options beforehand and to discuss your plan with your midwife, who can explain what is available at your birth setting. Keeping the plan flexible means you can adapt if circumstances change while still having your priorities understood. Bring a copy in your notes or hospital bag so your care team can see it.

There are several options for pain relief in labour, and you can choose what suits you, changing your mind as things progress. Simpler methods include staying mobile and upright, using a birthing pool or warm water, breathing and relaxation techniques, massage, and a TENS machine that sends small electrical pulses. Gas and air (Entonox) is a mix of oxygen and nitrous oxide that you breathe in during contractions; it takes the edge off pain, is easy to use, and wears off quickly. Some units offer injected opioid painkillers such as pethidine or diamorphine, which can help you relax, though they may make you feel drowzy or sick and can affect the baby if given close to birth. An epidural is a stronger form of pain relief given by an anaesthetist as an injection into the space around the nerves in your lower back, which numbs the lower body and is very effective. It requires monitoring, may make your legs feel heavy, and can lengthen the second stage of labour. Each method has benefits and drawbacks, so discuss the options with your midwife in advance and ask about what is available where you plan to give birth.

Staying active in pregnancy is good for you and your baby, and for most people it is safe to continue or start moderate exercise. Activity can help you cope with the changes to your body, reduce back pain, improve sleep and mood, and prepare you for labour. A useful guide to intensity is the talk test: you should be able to hold a conversation while exercising; if you become too breathless to talk comfortably, you are working too hard. If you were not active before pregnancy, start gently and build up. Good options include walking, swimming, stationary cycling, and pregnancy-specific yoga or exercise classes. Some activities are best avoided: contact sports and anything with a risk of falling or being hit, scuba diving, and exercising at high altitude. Avoid lying flat on your back for long periods after 16 weeks, as this can affect blood flow, and do not exercise to exhaustion or let yourself overheat. Stay hydrated and warm up and cool down. Stop and seek advice if you have pain, dizziness, breathlessness before exertion, palpitations, vaginal bleeding, contractions, or leaking fluid. If you have a pregnancy complication, check with your midwife before exercising.

In later pregnancy, the position you fall asleep in matters. Research has found that going to sleep on your back from 28 weeks onward is linked to a higher risk of stillbirth, so the advice is to go to sleep on your side, either side, for any sleep in the third trimester, including naps. This is because in later pregnancy the weight of the growing uterus can press on major blood vessels when you lie flat on your back, which may reduce blood flow to the baby. You do not need to worry if you wake up on your back, which is common; simply settle yourself back onto your side. It can help to use pillows behind your back or between your knees to make side-sleeping more comfortable and to discourage rolling flat. There is no evidence that one side is better than the other, so choose whichever is comfortable. Before 28 weeks, sleeping position is not thought to carry the same risk. If you find sleep difficult in late pregnancy, a supportive pillow, a consistent bedtime routine, and avoiding large meals and caffeine before bed may help. Speak to your midwife if you have concerns about sleep.

The flu (influenza) vaccine is recommended during pregnancy because pregnant people are more likely to become seriously ill if they catch flu, which can lead to complications such as pneumonia and, in some cases, premature labour or a low birth weight baby. The vaccine is safe to have at any stage of pregnancy and protects both you and, through antibodies passed across the placenta, your baby in the early months after birth before they can be vaccinated themselves. The flu vaccine used in pregnancy is an inactivated vaccine, meaning it does not contain live virus and cannot give you flu. Mild side effects such as a sore arm, mild fever, or aching are possible and usually settle quickly. Because flu strains change, the vaccine is offered each flu season, so having it in a previous year does not protect you now. It is best to have it as early as possible once it becomes available in the season. You can usually get the flu vaccine through your maternity service, GP, or pharmacy. If you are pregnant during flu season, ask your midwife or doctor about arranging your vaccination.

The whooping cough vaccine is recommended in every pregnancy to protect your baby in the first weeks of life. Whooping cough (pertussis) is a serious infection that can be life-threatening for young babies, who are too young to be fully vaccinated themselves until a few months old. By having the vaccine while pregnant, you pass protective antibodies across the placenta to your baby, giving them protection from birth until their own vaccinations begin. The vaccine is usually offered from around 16 weeks, and having it in the right window in each pregnancy gives the best protection. It is given as a combined vaccine that also protects against other diseases such as tetanus, diphtheria, and polio, and it does not contain live whooping cough bacteria, so it cannot cause the illness. Side effects are usually mild, such as a sore arm or a mild fever. Having the vaccine in pregnancy is considered safe for you and your baby and is strongly encouraged because it substantially reduces the risk of your newborn catching whooping cough. Ask your midwife or GP about arranging it at the recommended time, and have it in each pregnancy even if you were vaccinated before.

For most people with a healthy, uncomplicated pregnancy, having sex is safe throughout. The baby is protected by the amniotic sac and the muscular uterus, and a plug of mucus seals the cervix, so sex does not harm the baby. Your interest in sex may change during pregnancy because of hormones, tiredness, nausea, or body changes, and it is normal for desire to go up or down; open communication with your partner helps. You may need to try different positions as your bump grows to stay comfortable. There are some situations where a provider may advise avoiding sex, such as if you have certain types of vaginal bleeding, a placenta that is low-lying (placenta previa), a history or signs of preterm labour, or if your waters have broken, so follow any specific advice you are given. It is a myth that orgasm or sex triggers labour in a normal pregnancy, though mild cramping afterward can occur and usually settles. If you have pain, bleeding, or leaking fluid after sex, contact your provider. If you are unsure whether sex is safe in your particular pregnancy, ask your midwife or doctor.
source_name: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists

text: Many people wonder whether it is safe to dye their hair during pregnancy. The chemicals in permanent and semi-permanent hair dyes are not thought to be harmful in pregnancy, because only small amounts can be absorbed through the skin, so occasional use is generally considered low risk. As a cautious approach, some people choose to wait until after the first 12 weeks, when the risk of any chemical affecting the baby is thought to be lower. If you want to reduce contact with the chemicals further, you could consider highlights, where the dye is applied to strands of hair wrapped in foil rather than to the scalp, so less is absorbed. When dyeing your hair at home, it helps to wear gloves, work in a well-ventilated room, leave the dye on for the shortest time needed, and rinse the scalp well afterward. Doing a patch test first is sensible, as pregnancy can change how your skin reacts. Plant-based options such as pure henna are an alternative, though check that products are natural and not mixed with other chemicals. If you have concerns, you can ask your midwife or pharmacist for reassurance.

Looking after your teeth and gums is important in pregnancy, and dental care is safe. Pregnancy hormones can make your gums more likely to become swollen, sore, and to bleed, a condition sometimes called pregnancy gingivitis, so good oral care matters more than usual. Brush your teeth twice a day with fluoride toothpaste, clean between your teeth daily, and try to limit sugary foods and drinks. It is safe and recommended to see a dentist during pregnancy, and you should tell them you are pregnant. Routine dental treatment can be carried out, and dentists take pregnancy into account when planning any procedures. If you are being sick a lot, for example with morning sickness, avoid brushing straight after vomiting, as stomach acid softens the enamel; instead rinse your mouth with water and brush a little later. In some countries, NHS dental care is free during pregnancy and for a period after the birth, so it is worth arranging a checkup. See a dentist if your gums bleed a lot, become very sore, or if you have toothache. Keeping your mouth healthy is part of a healthy pregnancy.

Travel is generally safe in an uncomplicated pregnancy, and the middle of pregnancy, from about 14 to 28 weeks, is often the most comfortable time, when the risk of miscarriage is lower and early symptoms have usually settled. If you are flying, check the airline's policy, as many restrict travel in late pregnancy: after about 28 weeks they often ask for a letter from your midwife or doctor confirming your due date and that you are well, and many will not allow flying after around 37 weeks (or 32 weeks for twins). Long-distance travel, including long flights of four hours or more, carries a small increased risk of blood clots (deep vein thrombosis) in pregnancy, so drink plenty of water, move around regularly, do leg exercises, and consider wearing compression stockings. When travelling by car, wear your seatbelt with the lap strap under your bump across your hips and the shoulder strap between your breasts and to the side of the bump. Carry your maternity notes, arrange travel insurance that covers pregnancy, and check whether any vaccinations or malaria precautions for your destination are safe. Discuss any long or overseas trips with your midwife beforehand.

Paracetamol is generally the first-choice painkiller in pregnancy and is considered safe for treating mild pain and fever, such as headaches, backache, or a high temperature, when used correctly. To use it safely, take the lowest dose that helps for the shortest time needed, and do not exceed the recommended amount on the packet. If you need to take paracetamol for more than a day or two, or you are using it regularly, it is best to speak to your midwife, pharmacist, or doctor for advice. Some other painkillers are not recommended in pregnancy: ibuprofen and other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are generally avoided, especially in the third trimester, unless specifically advised by a doctor, and stronger opioid medicines should only be used under medical guidance. Always check before taking any medicine in pregnancy, including over-the-counter remedies and herbal products, because some are not safe. If you take regular medication for an ongoing condition, do not stop it without advice, as many conditions need continued treatment; instead discuss it with your provider. When in doubt about any medicine or remedy, a pharmacist can quickly tell you whether it is suitable during pregnancy.

If you are trying to conceive or could become pregnant, one of the most important steps you can take is to start taking folic acid before you are pregnant. Folic acid helps prevent neural tube defects, such as spina bifida, in the baby's developing brain and spinal cord. Because the neural tube forms in the very first weeks of pregnancy, often before you know you are pregnant, taking folic acid beforehand means protective levels are already present when it matters most. The usual advice is to take 400 micrograms of folic acid a day while trying to conceive and to continue until you are 12 weeks pregnant. Some people are advised to take a higher dose of 5 milligrams a day, on prescription, for example if they or their partner have a neural tube defect or a family history of one, have had a previous affected pregnancy, have diabetes, take certain epilepsy medicines, or have a raised body weight. Preconception is also a good time to reach a healthy weight, stop smoking, avoid alcohol, review any medicines with your doctor, and consider a vitamin D supplement. Speak to your GP or pharmacist for advice tailored to you.

Pregnancy begins when a fertilized egg implants in the uterus and a woman can expect changes in her body over the next nine months. Common early signs include missed periods, tiredness, nausea, and breast tenderness. These changes are usually normal, but they should be discussed with a health worker during your first prenatal visit. Eating a balanced diet, avoiding tobacco and alcohol, and keeping up with appointments can help keep both mother and baby healthy.

Prenatal care is regular health checkups during pregnancy that help detect problems early and keep the mother and baby safe. Care usually begins in the first trimester and includes checking blood pressure, measuring growth, and giving advice on diet and daily habits. Prenatal visits also include tests for anemia, infections, and gestational diabetes. Starting care early makes it easier to treat issues before they become serious.

Routine pregnancy checkups, often every four weeks at first, become more frequent later in pregnancy. During these visits, the provider checks the mother's weight, blood pressure, and the baby’s heartbeat. They also offer vaccines, screen for high blood pressure and diabetes, and answer questions. If you miss a visit, contact your clinic to reschedule. Regular checkups are one of the best ways to protect both mother and baby.

The baby grows quickly during pregnancy, with different stages each trimester. In the first trimester, the major organs form. In the second trimester, the baby’s bones, muscles, and senses develop. In the third trimester, the baby gains weight and prepares for birth. Feeling the baby move is a sign the pregnancy is progressing. Regular care helps the provider monitor growth and identify any problems early.

A healthy pregnancy diet includes vegetables, fruits, whole grains, beans, nuts, lean meat, and milk or fortified milk alternatives. These foods provide energy and important nutrients such as protein, iron, calcium, and folic acid. Avoid very sugary, salty, or fried foods as much as possible. Eating a variety of safe foods each day helps support the baby's growth and keeps the mother stronger.
source_name: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists

text: Drinking enough clean water is important in pregnancy because blood volume increases and the body needs fluid for the baby and placenta. Aim for plain water most of the day, and carry a cup or bottle to sip often. If you have severe nausea, take small sips of water or oral rehydration solution. Avoid too much caffeine and sugary drinks. Staying hydrated can also help reduce common problems like constipation and urinary tract infections.

Prenatal vitamins help fill gaps in the diet, especially folic acid and iron. Folic acid before and in early pregnancy lowers the risk of birth defects in the baby’s spine and brain. Iron supports the mother's blood and helps prevent anemia. Health workers may also recommend calcium or iodine in places where diets are low in these nutrients. Follow the local health provider's advice on which supplements to take and how much.

Healthy weight gain in pregnancy is gradual and depends on the woman’s body before pregnancy. Most women gain weight steadily after the first trimester. Gaining enough weight helps the baby grow, but gaining too much can make it harder to stay healthy. Your provider can give a target range based on your height and weight before pregnancy. Focus on eating balanced meals rather than eating for two.

Physical activity during pregnancy is usually safe and helpful for most women. Walking, swimming, and gentle stretching can keep your muscles strong and reduce back pain. Avoid exercises with a high risk of falling or contact sports. If you have a health condition or pregnancy complication, talk to your provider before starting exercise. Stop and rest if you feel dizzy, have chest pain, or cannot catch your breath.

Good sleep supports pregnancy health. Many women do best sleeping on their side, especially the left side, to help blood flow to the baby. Try a regular bedtime routine, use pillows for support, and rest if you feel tired. If you have trouble sleeping, avoid heavy meals and caffeine before bed. If you still cannot sleep or feel very tired during the day, tell your health worker.

A healthy pregnancy lifestyle includes stopping smoking, not drinking alcohol, limiting caffeine, and avoiding harmful substances. It also means getting rest, reducing stress, and asking for help when needed. Keep your home clean, wash your hands often, and avoid people who are sick. These simple steps support your health and help protect your growing baby.

Pregnancy can bring normal discomforts like nausea, mild back pain, heartburn, and frequent urination. These often begin in the first or second trimester. Eating small meals, using a pillow for back support, and resting when needed can ease many symptoms. If pain is severe, a fever develops, or symptoms get worse, see your health provider. Most common discomforts can be managed safely with simple home measures.

Feeling emotional, worried, or tired during pregnancy is common. Talk with a family member, friend, or health worker if you feel sad, anxious, or overwhelmed. Healthy habits like gentle exercise, rest, and talking about your feelings can help. If you have trouble sleeping, lose interest in daily life, or feel very hopeless, seek professional support. Mental health is an important part of pregnancy care.

Some medicines are safe in pregnancy and others are not. Always tell your health worker about any pills, traditional herbs, or supplements you use. Do not start a new medicine without advice. Your provider can balance the benefits and risks and suggest safe options. If you are already taking medicine for a chronic condition, continue only as directed by your provider.

Vaccines during pregnancy can protect both mother and baby. Influenza and Tdap vaccines are often recommended because they reduce the risk of serious illness and help the baby stay healthy after birth. Talk to your health provider about which vaccines are safe and available in your area. Vaccination is part of prenatal care in many countries.
source_name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

text: Gestational diabetes is high blood sugar that starts during pregnancy. It is usually checked with a screening test in the second trimester. Eating a healthy diet, staying active, and following the provider’s advice help keep blood sugar under control. Most women can manage this condition with diet and monitoring, but some may need medication. Regular care reduces the risk of problems for the baby and mother.

High blood pressure in pregnancy should be monitored carefully. It can begin before pregnancy or develop after 20 weeks. Your provider will check your blood pressure at visits and may recommend tests for urine protein or blood work. If your pressure is high, follow advice on rest, healthy food, and medications if needed. Good follow-up helps lower risks for the mother and baby.

Preeclampsia is a condition of high blood pressure with signs of organ stress, often after 20 weeks of pregnancy. It may cause severe headache, vision changes, upper belly pain, or sudden swelling in the hands and face. If you have these symptoms, seek care right away. Early detection and close monitoring are important to protect both mother and baby.

Anemia in pregnancy often means the body does not have enough iron to make healthy blood. It can cause tiredness, weakness, and lightheadedness. Health providers usually test for anemia early in pregnancy. Eating iron-rich foods like beans, green leafy vegetables, and fortified cereals together with vitamin C helps absorption. Your provider may also give iron supplements if needed.

Infections during pregnancy can affect the mother and baby, so prevention is important. Wash your hands often, avoid raw or undercooked foods, and stay away from people with colds or flu when possible. Report fever, chills, painful urination, or unusual discharge to your provider right away. Early treatment of infections helps keep pregnancy safer.

Feeling the baby move is an important sign of fetal health, usually after 18 to 24 weeks. Movements may feel like kicks, rolls, or flutters. Pay attention to your baby’s normal pattern. If the baby moves less than usual or stops moving, contact your health provider. Your provider may monitor the baby with a test to ensure the baby is doing well.

Labor often begins with regular contractions, a change in vaginal discharge, or when the water around the baby breaks. Contractions become longer, stronger, and closer together over time. If you are not sure whether you are in labor, call your provider or clinic for advice. Knowing these signs helps you get to the birth location in time.

Delivery is the process of the baby and placenta leaving the body. Many women have a vaginal birth, while some need a cesarean delivery for safety reasons. Your provider will discuss options based on your health and the baby’s position. Preparing for delivery includes choosing a trusted place to give birth and understanding the most likely plan for your situation.

The postpartum period is the weeks after birth when the mother’s body recovers and the newborn adapts to life outside the womb. It is normal to feel tired, sore, or emotional. Rest when possible, eat nourishing food, and ask for help with baby care. Attend the postpartum visit so the provider can check healing, mood, and breastfeeding progress.

Breastfeeding soon after birth helps the baby get protective colostrum and supports a strong milk supply. Hold the baby close, and find a comfortable position with support for your back and arms. Good latch and quiet practice make feeding easier. If you have pain or concerns, ask a health worker or breastfeeding counselor for help because small changes can improve comfort and milk transfer.

Newborn care includes keeping the baby warm, feeding often, and keeping the umbilical cord clean and dry. Skin-to-skin contact after birth helps the baby stay warm and calm. Feed the baby whenever they show signs of hunger, such as rooting, sucking motions, or hand movements. Take the baby to routine checkups to monitor growth and development.

Some symptoms in pregnancy need prompt attention. These include heavy bleeding, severe headache, sudden swelling of the face or hands, blurred vision, severe belly pain, or fast heart rate. These may be signs of conditions such as preeclampsia or complications that require medical care. Do not wait — contact your health worker right away if any warning sign appears.

Emergency care is needed if you have heavy vaginal bleeding, chest pain, trouble breathing, confusion, seizures, or if your baby stops moving. These are not normal pregnancy symptoms and can signal a serious problem. Go to the nearest health facility or call emergency services immediately. Quick care can protect both you and your baby.

Try to eat small meals every few hours so your body can absorb nutrients without making nausea worse. Include protein like beans, eggs, or lentils, and choose foods high in iron such as spinach, red meat, or fortified cereal. Add fruits and vegetables for vitamins and fiber. A steady pattern of healthy meals helps keep energy up and supports the baby’s development.

Drink water regularly, even if you are not thirsty. Aim for several cups of plain water each day and take small sips if you feel sick. You can also drink soups or herbal tea if plain water is hard to take. Check your urine color: light yellow is a sign you are well hydrated. Dark yellow means you should drink more fluids.

Folic acid is most important before pregnancy and during the first trimester because it helps your baby’s spine and brain form normally. Iron supplements are often started after the first visit if tests show low blood levels. Take supplements with food and a glass of water, unless your provider tells you otherwise. Always follow the amount your provider suggests.
source_name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

text: Gentle moves like walking, swimming, and pelvic floor exercises are often good in pregnancy. They help with circulation, posture, and energy. Avoid heavy lifting and sudden twisting. If exercise causes pain, dizziness, or shortness of breath, stop and rest. Talk to your provider if you have questions about which activities are safe for you.

Many women find it easier to sleep on their side with a pillow between their legs and under their belly. This position is often more comfortable and may help with blood flow. Rest during the day when you can, and keep a quiet, dark room for sleeping. If you have trouble falling asleep, try relaxing breathing, a short walk, or warm non-caffeinated drinks before bed.

Pregnancy can bring worry, mood changes, or feeling tired more easily. It helps to talk with your partner, family member, or friend about how you feel. Gentle movement, good sleep, and spending time with people you trust can support your mood. If sadness or anxiety lasts more than two weeks or affects your daily life, tell your health worker.

Back pain, heartburn, and swollen feet are common in pregnancy because your body is changing and the baby is growing. Use gentle stretches, wear supportive shoes, and rest with your feet raised when possible. For heartburn, eat slowly, avoid spicy foods, and do not lie flat right after eating. Most of these discomforts improve after birth.

A drop in fetal movement, especially after 28 weeks, can be a warning sign. If you notice the baby moves much less than usual or stops moving, contact your health provider right away. Your provider may ask you to count kicks or come in for a check to make sure the baby is healthy.

Your first prenatal visit usually includes questions about your health, a physical exam, and tests for blood type, anemia, and infections. This visit is also a good time to ask about your diet, medicines, and any worries you have. Early prenatal care helps providers find and treat problems before they affect the baby.

At prenatal checkups, the provider may measure your belly, check the baby’s heartbeat, and test your urine for protein and sugar. These routine checks help spot high blood pressure, diabetes, or infection early. If something is unclear, the provider may suggest extra tests or more frequent visits.

During the second trimester, the baby’s bones grow stronger and the senses begin to develop. The mother may feel the baby move and notice the baby’s shape changing as the belly grows. Keeping regular appointments lets providers check the baby’s size and development and gives you a chance to ask questions.

If your contractions are regular, last more than 30 seconds, and come closer together, it may be labor. Another sign is a change in vaginal discharge or water breaking. If you are unsure, call your provider or birth facility and explain your symptoms. They can tell you when to come in and what to expect next.

Planning for delivery includes choosing a safe place to give birth and knowing who will support you. Talk with your provider about what happens in labor and what choices are available. A birth plan can help you share your preferences for pain relief, who is with you, and how the baby is cared for after birth.

After birth, it is normal to have vaginal bleeding and cramps for a few weeks. Rest when you can and ask for help with baby care and household tasks. Eating well and drinking fluids supports healing. If bleeding becomes heavy, you pass clots larger than an egg, or you have a fever, contact your provider quickly.
